text: That being said, there is a wealth of literature in the Milanese dialect, so those with a deep interest in Milanese culture may consider learning it. There are many things to see in Milan - from fine churches, old palaces, excellent museums, world class theatres and opera houses, cultural gems, striking buildings, sleek modern architectural works and lovely streets and squares. Milan has some of the oldest churches in Italy, older than the ones in Rome because Milan was the capital of the Northern part of the late Roman Empire.
snippet: There are many things to see in Milan - from fine churches, old palaces, excellent museums, world class theatres and opera houses, cultural gems, striking buildings, sleek modern architectural works and lovely streets and squares.

text: It is located in the coolest area of ​​the city, Milano Sud. ( updated Jan 2021 ) Milano Pride. a parade and festival, is held at the end of June each year. (free). ( updated Jan 2021 ) In the area just south of the Central railway station you can find a dense concentration of hotels. This is a rather shabby part of the city where you can run into dubious individuals especially at nighttime. On the other hand, the hotels are clean and safe, for the most part streets are lit and the metro station isn't far away.
snippet: Milano Pride, a parade and festival, is held at the end of June each year. The hotels are clean and safe, for the most part streets are lit and the metro station isn't far away.

text: In essence, for a tourist, what makes Milan interesting compared to other places is that the city is truly more about the lifestyle of enjoying worldly pleasures: a paradise for shopping, football, opera, and nightlife. Milan remains the marketplace for Italian fashion — fashion aficionados, supermodels and international paparazzi descend upon the city twice a year for its spring and autumn fairs.
snippet: What makes Milan interesting compared to other places is that the city is truly more about the lifestyle of enjoying worldly pleasures: a paradise for shopping, football, opera, and nightlife. Milan remains the marketplace for Italian fashion!

text: Dedicated to antiques, the market has over 400 exhibitors, so you're certain to find something that catches your eye. Although Milan is a city that changes its mind as quickly as fashion trends come and go, it remains one of the strongest bastions of traditional Italian cooking, where homemade elements are still very much praised and appreciated. There are trattorias, enoteche (wine bars) and restaurants (including luxury ones) everywhere that offer traditional Milanese and Italian dishes to eat.
snippet: I enjoyed all the trattorias, enoteche (wine bars) and restaurants (including luxury ones) everywhere that offer traditional Milanese and Italian dishes to eat.

text: Located in the furthest of the Yasawa Island so it has the best sandy beaches with crystal clear water. The Fijian style bure is spacious, beautiful and the beach is right at our doorstep. We were coming to celebrate birthday and they specially prepared a cake with birthday song for us.  The food was decent and with variety of choices every meal. We especially liked the breakfast and the seafood platter is a must try with abundance of fresh lobster, prawns and fish from pacific ocean. I also liked the relaxing spa at the seaside. There were a number of complimentary activities to choose from daily - we really liked the guided snorkel trip by boat, the coconut tasting, and the private beach trip cannot be missed for sure. The complimentary sea plane trip is a breath-taking experience. This may not be the typical 5-star hotel you experience elsewhere but it definitely provides an authentic and enjoyable experience in the tropical island.
snippet: Located in the furthest of the Yasawa Island so it has the best sandy beaches with crystal clear water. The Fijian style bure is spacious, beautiful and the beach is right at our doorstep. We were coming to celebrate birthday and they specially prepared a cake with birthday song for us.

text: Every bit about this was amazing. From the food, to the location, and of course, the loveliest locals I've ever known. The locals know how to do happy. The resort is filled with amazing activities including going to the Blue Lagoon Cave or having a picnic on a secluded paradise beach where you're left to have the entire beach to yourself! Not doing any activities is equally amazing where you can just chill to take in the ocean views which you can never get tired of.
snippet: Every bit about this was amazing. From the food, to the location, and of course, the loveliest locals I've ever known. The locals know how to do happy.

text: Yasawa Islands, Fiji is some places have a great variety of fish up to 1 m in length and some even have sharks and turtles. On the whole, however, Fiji reef life is not yet up to the standard seen in other tropical areas, especially the Whitsundays and Great Barrier Reef in Australia.
snippet: Yasawa Islands, Fiji is some places have a great variety of fish up to 1 m in length and some even have sharks and turtles. Great for snorkeling and exploring.

text: Yasawa Islands, Fiji is there are many newer resorts but this is a boutique, traditional high end resort that has 27 years of experience and caters for couples who are looking to escape the fast lane, lie low, recharge and restore the soul. We’ve been to Fiji many times but first visit to Yasawa’s and the beaches here are wonderful, the snorkeling the best I have encountered. Smelgi….thanks for all the adventures out on the boats, loved the caves!!!
snippet: We’ve been to Fiji many times but first visit to Yasawa’s and the beaches here are wonderful, the snorkeling the best I have encountered. Smelgi….thanks for all the adventures out on the boats, loved the caves!!!

text: Whistler & Blackcomb mountain is unmatched for resort skiing. There is nothing like taking the gondolas up 5000 vertical feet and skiing 7th Heaven. Riding the Peak2Peak from Blackcomb to Whistler and skiing to the village for drinks at the Longhorn Saloon after a full day. I highly recommend for anyone who skis at any level.
snippet: Whistler & Blackcomb mountain is unmatched for resort skiing. I highly recommend for anyone who skis at any level.

text: Whistler is incredible and absolutely worth visiting! It looks like one of those little towns you see in snowglobes but in real life, and that’s just the start! A quick image search of Whistler will have you feeling as snug as a bug in a rug. I am in love with the simple, yet beautiful architecture of the town with the backdrop of Whistler Mountain and Tremor Mountain. If you visit during the winter, the snow-lined streets will leave you completely enchanted. But it’s not all looks. It’s a great destination for families, couples, and solo travelers alike! Summer or winter, rain or shine, there are plenty of activities here.
snippet: Whistler is incredible and absolutely worth visiting! It looks like one of those little towns you see in snowglobes but in real life, and that’s just the start! It’s a great destination for families, couples, and solo travelers alike! Summer or winter, rain or shine, there are plenty of activities here.

text: Whistler is a great place to see animals like eagles, marmots, deer, and bears, just to name a few. They are quite literally everywhere, and you’re sure to see several on your trip. Getting to Whistler is a breeze since it’s so close to Vancouver. This is a huge bonus if you’re flying in since you won’t have to spend hours sitting on a bus to actually start your vacation.
snippet: Whistler is a great place to see animals like eagles, marmots, deer, and bears, just to name a few. Getting to Whistler is a breeze since it’s so close to Vancouver. This is a huge bonus if you’re flying in since you won’t have to spend hours sitting on a bus to actually start your vacation.

text: A valley trail connects 99% of all areas within and around Whistler. This is a paved 2- lane trail system that is very pleasant and fast to get around on. More advanced trails head north to Pemberton and south to Squamish for those who may be more adventurous.
snippet: A valley trail connects 99% of all areas within and around Whistler. More advanced trails head north to Pemberton and south to Squamish for those who may be more adventurous.

text: Whistler, in British Columbia, Canada, is North America’s biggest ski resort  and in my opinion, it’s also one of the best. The whole place is spread across two mountains and three villages, with world-class terrain for skiing and snowboarding in winter, and hiking trails, mountain biking, and glacier-fed lakes in summer. The villages themselves are super walkable, full of boutique shops, great restaurants, and some amazing hotels.
snippet: The whole place is spread across two mountains and three villages, with world-class terrain for skiing and snowboarding in winter, and hiking trails, mountain biking, and glacier-fed lakes in summer. The villages themselves are super walkable, full of boutique shops, great restaurants, and some amazing hotels.

text: Personally, Guanajuato is my favourite place in Mexico. It's so three-dimensional, and pedestrian-friendly because major streets are underground. Most locals use the tunnels as shortcuts when convenient, but generally prefer walking on surface streets.
snippet: Personally, Guanajuato is my favourite place in Mexico. It's so three-dimensional, and pedestrian-friendly because major streets are underground.

text: The houses of Guanajuato tumble down the hills that surround it in every imaginable colour. There’s no subtle, complementary colour scheme—fuchsia pink mingles with pillar box red, saffron yellow, baby blue, and lime green. We like the city better for its discordance. Despite its beauty it’s not perfectly restored and retains a gritty realness—it’s a city where people live, work, study, and play, not a museum piece for tourists. This colonial city in the mountains of central Mexico was once a silver mining town and although it’s a UNESCO World Heritage Site, strangely it’s not very popular with foreign tourists, who prefer San Miguel de Allende just an hour away.
snippet: The houses of Guanajuato tumble down the hills that surround it in every imaginable colour. Despite its beauty it’s not perfectly restored and retains a gritty realness—it’s a city where people live, work, study, and play, not a museum piece for tourists.

text: Make sure to park your car in your hotel, as most offer free shuttles to the city; otherwise, park your car in a lot. It’s a compact, walkable city but the large student population means there’s plenty going on with many cafes, bars, street food stalls, markets, and art galleries. It was the perfect place to indulge our favourite slow travel activity of aimless wandering. It’s easy to get pleasantly lost in the cobblestone lanes that climb steeply from the centre, turning a corner to find yet another tiny plaza with locals chatting on benches shaded by manicured trees. It’s definitely a walking city as the narrow streets are a nightmare for cars and one of the city’s unique features is a network of underground tunnels that are used as roads.
snippet: It’s a compact, walkable city but the large student population means there’s plenty going on with many cafes, bars, street food stalls, markets, and art galleries. It was the perfect place to indulge our favourite slow travel activity of aimless wandering. It’s definitely a walking city as the narrow streets are a nightmare for cars and one of the city’s unique features is a network of underground tunnels that are used as roads.

text: During the rainy season (November–April) be prepared for occasional flash floods and traffic jams, and sometimes its almost as bad as Jakarta. Being simultaneously one of the most ancient and significant trading ports in Indonesia, a post Dutch-colonial city (the Dutch knew it as Soerabaja ), and having passed through brief Japanese control during WWII, Surabaya has a complex and patchwork heritage. It's not unusual to meet Surabaya locals with heritage from other parts of Indonesia, all parts of south-eastern China, the Middle East or Europe.
snippet: During the rainy season (November–April) be prepared for occasional flash floods and traffic jams, and sometimes its almost as bad as Jakarta. Surabaya has a complex and patchwork heritage. It's not unusual to meet Surabaya locals with heritage from other parts of Indonesia, all parts of south-eastern China, the Middle East or Europe.

text: Went there only for a short holiday visit but really impressed at how the city is run. There are so... many... nice... parks... that are really taken care of. Down to the small spaces, suddenly a park. Yes, even the road separators are lusciously green. I saw several Dinas Pertamanan trucks busy planting stuff around the city. Many old buildings still maintain the colonial architecture. Delicious and affordable food. I won't mind eating rawon and soto every other day. Jembatan Suramadu is awe-inspiring. I wish I had visited it during the night. The rivers/canals are relatively clean, and the locals seem to do their part to keep it so.
snippet: Went there only for a short holiday visit but really impressed at how the city is run. There are so... many... nice... parks... that are really taken care of. Delicious and affordable food. I won't mind eating rawon and soto every other day. Jembatan Suramadu is awe-inspiring. I wish I had visited it during the night. The rivers/canals are relatively clean, and the locals seem to do their part to keep it so.

text: Yes you should visit Surabaya. It is friendly city, it is not as big as Jakarta. There are several mall, and most of it have special theme children playground, with nice shops and good restaurant, I think kids will love the playground. Surabaya is very popular with their local food, such as Soto Ayam, Semanggi, Rawon, Nasi bebek , Soto Daging, Tahu Campur, Lontong Balap and others yummy food.
snippet: Yes you should visit Surabaya. There are several mall, and most of it have special theme children playground, with nice shops and good restaurant, I think kids will love the playground. Surabaya is very popular with their local food, such as Soto Ayam, Semanggi, Rawon, Nasi bebek , Soto Daging, Tahu Campur, Lontong Balap and others yummy food.

text: From Surabaya, you can take day trips to following places outside Surabaya: Mount Bromo, the most famous place to be visited from Surabaya and Pulau Madura, a big island nearby, famous for its bull race.
snippet: From Surabaya, you can take day trips to following places outside Surabaya: Mount Bromo, the most famous place to be visited from Surabaya and Pulau Madura, a big island nearby, famous for its bull race.

text: Nuuk is the most underrated place in Greenland, and I'm not sure why. Especially if you only have a few days, it is perfect as it is a mixture of outdoor activities and culture. You can see icebergs on a fjord tour from Nuuk, and the backcountry around the capital is more spectacular that than around Ilulissat in my opinion. Nuuk also has Northern Lights in Winter and usually has whales in Summer. You won't be disappointed with a few days in Nuuk. It's a great introduction to the country
snippet: Nuuk is the most underrated place in Greenland, and I'm not sure why. It is perfect as it is a mixture of outdoor activities and culture. Nuuk also has Northern Lights in Winter and usually has whales in Summer. You won't be disappointed with a few days in Nuuk. It's a great introduction to the country

text: I spent several days in Nuuk, the capital, which is home to around 20,000 people – just over a third of Greenland’s total population. It has an interesting mix of modern and traditional architecture, along with an array of restaurants and cafes, several museums, and even a shopping mall and cinema complex, all of which stands in stark contrast with the beautiful wilderness that surrounds the city. As I went in autumn, it wasn’t yet covered in snow and ice, but I was lucky enough to witness wonderful Northern Lights displays, both from Nuuk itself and from the sea.
snippet: It has an interesting mix of modern and traditional architecture, along with an array of restaurants and cafes, several museums, and even a shopping mall and cinema complex, all of which stands in stark contrast with the beautiful wilderness that surrounds the city. I was lucky enough to witness wonderful Northern Lights displays, both from Nuuk itself and from the sea.

text: A unmarked trek goes up Ukkusissat (Store Malene) and offers a view over Nuuk from above. Parts of this trail is on mountain sides with large boulders or steep climbs up loose rocks. Every part of town is easily accessible by bus.
snippet: A unmarked trek goes up Ukkusissat (Store Malene) and offers a view over Nuuk from above. Every part of town is easily accessible by bus.

text: I'm currently here on a trip and I can tell you it's great. Here's what I've found in the two days I've been here; it's small but has a lot more than you might expect, the views are amazing both from the hills outside and down on the sea, and I've had zero problem with only speaking English. The museum's and galleries are really good. There aren't many of any of these things but they're all really good. One point to note though is it feels very much like a city. Just a very small one. So culturally it doesn't seem that different to anywhere else although they don't have the same chains as everywhere else apart from seeing some Nordic chains I know) so the shops are really interestingly different) so there's no McDonald's but there is Hereford Beefstouw...
snippet: Here's what I've found in the two days I've been here; it's small but has a lot more than you might expect, the views are amazing both from the hills outside and down on the sea, and I've had zero problem with only speaking English. One point to note though is it feels very much like a city.

text: The city is sometimes accused of being rather soulless, with many high-rise blocks. Nuuk, along with most of the population centres of Greenland, has a tundra climate. Being on the coast helps moderate temperatures somewhat, and winter temperatures are not unlike those of northern Europe or central Canada.
snippet: The city is sometimes accused of being rather soulless, with many high-rise blocks.

text: In all my trips I have spent one night in Windhoek. Met friends, had dinner together. Caught up on some sleep and left first thing in the morning. I recommend as short a trip as possible.
snippet: In all my trips I have spent one night in Windhoek. Met friends, had dinner together. Caught up on some sleep and left first thing in the morning. I recommend as short a trip as possible.

text: Windhoek by itself is not that exciting of a city, especially if you are used to ones in Europe. There are some nice places to eat, museums and art galleries but nature is the real pull of visiting Namibia. There are some dams around Windhoek that are nice to visit but if your time and finances allow, I would recommend looking into some tours to see nature. Omaruru Game Lodge is a nice lodge that is close enough to justify a 2 night trip, for example.
snippet: There are some nice places to eat, museums and art galleries but nature is the real pull of visiting Namibia. There are some dams around Windhoek that are nice to visit but if your time and finances allow, I would recommend looking into some tours to see nature.

text: Most hotels and hostels are close to the city centre, so you can easily walk to most shops, restaurants, clubs and sights. After dark it is not safe to walk, though. Traffic in Windhoek generally flows freely, and driving is generally well ordered.
snippet: Most hotels and hostels are close to the city centre, so you can easily walk to most shops, restaurants, clubs and sights. After dark it is not safe to walk, though.

text: At night hookers are everywhere tourists may go, including the casinos, larger bars and restaurants, and on the street. Consider that the HIV prevalence rate still is around 15% nationwide, and presumably still higher than that among sex workers. Windhoek is generally safe for tourists but the usual precautions should be taken.
snippet: At night hookers are everywhere tourists may go, including the casinos, larger bars and restaurants, and on the street. Windhoek is generally safe for tourists but the usual precautions should be taken.

text: Kingston, Jamaica is vibrant streets pulse with reggae rhythms, while lush hills and beaches offer a serene escape, creating a captivating blend of energy and tranquility. This destination has a consistently warm to hot climate with little seasonal variation. The annual average temperature is 28.4°C, with monthly averages ranging from 26.8°C to 29.8°C.
snippet: Kingston, Jamaica is vibrant streets pulse with reggae rhythms, while lush hills and beaches offer a serene escape, creating a captivating blend of energy and tranquility.

text: Kingston is Jamaica at its most authentic, a sprawling city of contrasts spread between the east coast and the Blue Mountains. Kingston's diversity is reflected in its disparate neighborhoods, from the wealthy northern suburbs to the less affluent downtown. Travelers can discover the spirit of One Love at the Bob Marley Museum, or sample delicious ice cream at Devon House.
snippet: Kingston is Jamaica at its most authentic, a sprawling city of contrasts spread between the east coast and the Blue Mountains. Travelers can discover the spirit of One Love at the Bob Marley Museum, or sample delicious ice cream at Devon House.

text: Kingston is a small city with some outskirt communities. I traveled all over and felt extremely comfortable. To be honest, it felt safer than Negril. Most people in Kingston are working people with a purpose. I did not get hassled once in the 2 days I was there. I visited the "gully" home of Movado. We went out at night and did not get hassled once. I love Kingston and would definitely return. I made a few friends while I was there. People, like in the rest of Jamaica, are super nice.
snippet: Kingston is a small city with some outskirt communities. I traveled all over and felt extremely comfortable. Most people in Kingston are working people with a purpose. We went out at night and did not get hassled once. I love Kingston and would definitely return. People, like in the rest of Jamaica, are super nice.

text: I looove Kingston! I prefer it over Montego Bay. Less touristy and you get to be around the locals. Still easy to get to the tourists spots. Still got lotta things to do there. Night life is fire.
snippet: I looove Kingston! It has a lotta things to do there. Night life is fire.

text: At Nafplio, you have an amazing night time (city center at evening is just « great ») and during the daytime you can easily reach Spetzes or hydra by boat (the trip in ferries is, for me, the most chilling time to visit islands)
snippet: At Nafplio, you have an amazing night time and during the daytime you can easily reach Spetzes or hydra by boat (the trip in ferries is, for me, the most chilling time to visit islands)

text: Nafplio is absolutely gorgeous. A small and walkable old town, with lots of shops, taverns and food & drink places. Great in the morning, evening and night time alike. Beaches aren't amazing, but for example there is the 'sunken city' beach which is worth a visit. Take care as it's full of urchins (sign of clean waters). Not really a city, just a few Roman-era villas that are now under water. Only 1-2m depth, so easy to see snorkeling (just bring sea shoes or fins if someone is anxious about urchins, but also please try not to step on them and kill them).
snippet: Nafplio is absolutely gorgeous. A small and walkable old town, with lots of shops, taverns and food & drink places. Great in the morning, evening and night time alike. Beaches aren't amazing, but for example there is the 'sunken city' beach which is worth a visit.

text: Also one of the oldest still crossable arch bridges in existence. One of the best characteristics of Nafplio is that everything in the Old Town is within walking distance. In fact you can walk from one end to another in less than 15 min, and that's why you can see couples in love, happy kids, and large groups of friends everywhere in the town, at the port, in the alleys, always walking around and enjoying every part of the town.
snippet: One of the best characteristics of Nafplio is that everything in the Old Town is within walking distance. In fact you can walk from one end to another in less than 15 min!

text: You may get tired but the view is said to be worth it. We walked the paved road down to the crossing and went right down to "Karathona Beach". From "Karathona Beach" there is a walking path along the coast-line back to "Arvanithia Beach" and Nafplio.
snippet: You may get tired but the view is said to be worth it.

text: Supposedly has the best house, trance, dance, club music, Georgian and foreign DJs in Tbilisi. Drinks are a bit expensive, but worth it. Due to the countless budget options, prices have dropped significantly in the city.
snippet: Supposedly has the best house, trance, dance, club music, Georgian and foreign DJs in Tbilisi.

text: Tbilisi is large enough that you won’t get bored. The country is also small enough that you can easily do day tours to the stunning regions & countryside.
snippet: Tbilisi is large enough that you won’t get bored. The country is also small enough that you can easily do day tours to the stunning regions & countryside.

text: Absolutely beautiful. Tbilisi is particularly safe and fun, very walkable (though ubers and buses are unbelievably cheap), and has great food and many natural wonders a short drive away. I also went there to visit a friend who had moved; absolutely no regrets whatsoever. Go to the Baths. Pay attention to the fashion. Try the homemade wine. Talk to people. English also takes you a fairly long way in Tbilisi. Russian used to be more of a lingua franca, and even though almost everyone speaks it, they react more negatively to the language now and to Russian emigrees in general (at least, young people do).
snippet: Absolutely beautiful. Tbilisi is particularly safe and fun, very walkable (though ubers and buses are unbelievably cheap), and has great food and many natural wonders a short drive away. Go to the Baths. Pay attention to the fashion. Try the homemade wine. Talk to people. English also takes you a fairly long way in Tbilisi.

text: Either way, the king was inspired to found a city on the site, and the name of the city derives from the Georgian word tbili meaning "warm". Although the city has been destroyed and rebuilt 29 times, the layout of the Old Town is largely intact with narrow alleys and big crooked houses built around courtyards. Tbilisi experiences relatively cold winters and hot summers.
snippet: Although the city has been destroyed and rebuilt 29 times, the layout of the Old Town is largely intact with narrow alleys and big crooked houses built around courtyards. Tbilisi experiences relatively cold winters and hot summers.

text: Fruit farmers sell wine right on their farm or vineyard – make sure you also ask for Apple Wine (Hesse specialty) and New Wine (wine still in process of fermentation - sold from the barrel, bring a canister!) which you can sometimes drink in some 'wine-beergarden' right on site. Take a tram (5/5R) northbound to any place between Schriesheim and Lützelsachsen or a local train (S3/S4) southbound to Wiesloch – or (even better, if you have the time) S1 or S2 to Neustadt, where you will find yourself in an endless landscape of vine stocks. Maybe the most beautiful University Canteen in Germany and maybe also Heidelberg's cheapest beer garden.
snippet: Fruit farmers sell wine right on their farm or vineyard which you can sometimes drink in some 'wine-beergarden' right on site. Take a tram (5/5R) northbound to any place between Schriesheim and Lützelsachsen, where you will find yourself in an endless landscape of vine stocks.

text: More than 300 bars, pubs, clubs, discothèques and the like, from Bavarian style tourist restaurants with deer antlers on the walls to extremely left-wing student bars which reserve the right to refuse police officers entry to the bar.
snippet: More than 300 bars, pubs, clubs, discothèques and the like, from Bavarian style tourist restaurants with deer antlers on the walls to extremely left-wing student bars which reserve the right to refuse police officers entry to the bar.

text: Heidelberg is a small city with immense beauty and a highly desirable place to live, despite the millions of tourists that flock to the Schloss and wander the Altstadt each year. As a consequence, it has become expensive and upscale and driven out more alternative, progressive lifestyles. Yet it still has a youthful, romantic student flair, and while the student body is more preppy these days with Law and Med students versus the Hippie philosophers of the past, you can still have a great time for a few years.
snippet: Heidelberg is a small city with immense beauty and a highly desirable place to live, despite the millions of tourists that flock to the Schloss and wander the Altstadt each year. As a consequence, it has become expensive and upscale and driven out more alternative, progressive lifestyles. Yet it still has a youthful, romantic student flair.

text: You could almost be anywhere in Europe in Heidelberg; it has everything to cater to anyone, including a Frenchman who loves his Perrier, wine, and non-German cuisine. Which is great I guess. Don’t get me wrong – Heidelberg is a very pretty place and is worth a visit. But it just doesn’t feel very German to me.
snippet: You could almost be anywhere in Europe in Heidelberg; it has everything to cater to anyone, including a Frenchman who loves his Perrier, wine, and non-German cuisine. Heidelberg is a very pretty place and is worth a visit. But it just doesn’t feel very German to me.

text: Many of the cafes in Heidelberg set up outside tables when the weather is fair, and these are enjoyed by locals and tourists alike. A popular destination for summer cafe beer sipping and lounging is the Marktplatz, which is adjacent to the Heiliggeistkirche.
snippet: Many of the cafes in Heidelberg set up outside tables when the weather is fair, and these are enjoyed by locals and tourists alike. A popular destination for summer cafe beer sipping and lounging is the Marktplatz, which is adjacent to the Heiliggeistkirche.

text: So, even in summer, you should always bring a jumper just in case, and in the coldest months (May, June, July, August and September), pack layers. It is not unheard of for temperatures to drop below zero during winter, with Hobart's lowest temperature on record being a chilly -2.8°C, with sleet sometimes occurring and in the outer suburbs it even snows. Interestingly, Hobart has the second fewest daily hours of sunlight out of all Australian cities, at 5.9 hours on average for the year.
snippet: So, even in summer, you should always bring a jumper just in case, and in the coldest months (May, June, July, August and September), pack layers.

text: The Museum of Old and New Art (MONA) is among the best museums in Australia has and has fast become a Hobart icon. Allport Museum is a collection of the family library, art and colonial furniture of an extinct local family.
snippet: The Museum of Old and New Art (MONA) is among the best museums in Australia has and has fast become a Hobart icon.

text: It is a laid-back city where you might feel like you’ve gone back 20 years but that’s the beauty of the place. You get there and you find that the pace slows down and you can’t help yourself by falling into that groove which isn’t a bad thing, cruise and take your time with whatever you want to do as to the what and when you want to do it.
snippet: It is a laid-back city where you might feel like you’ve gone back 20 years but that’s the beauty of the place.

text: I happened to be there from mid December to mid January which seems to be the time when Hobart is most action packed, during this period there is a lot to entertain yourself with, festivals of all kinds plus the Sydney to Hobart yacht race finishes there then.
snippet: I happened to be there from mid December to mid January which seems to be the time when Hobart is most action packed, during this period there is a lot to entertain yourself with, festivals of all kinds plus the Sydney to Hobart yacht race finishes there then.

text: Nagasaki was my favourite part of Japan if I’m being honest. The weather was against us but I absolutely loved the observatory lookout, we walked up the mountain rather than catching the bus, we got tastes of the view on the way up but because of the weather we had no view from the top but it was absolutely incredible standing there and watching the clouds moving around us absorbing in the radio towers and how they would completely disappear. Very silent hill vibes. Also we had the best meal of the trip at this random Katsu place out by all the Mitsubishi factories.
snippet: Nagasaki was my favourite part of Japan if I’m being honest. It was absolutely incredible standing at the top of the mountain and watching the clouds moving around us absorbing in the radio towers and how they would completely disappear. Very silent hill vibes.

text: Although all of Japan celebrates O-Bon in August, Nagasaki puts a unique and deafening spin on the day of ancestor worship. Head down to the harbor for the main festivities, which involve far more alcohol and fireworks than is generally considered safe.
snippet: Although all of Japan celebrates O-Bon in August, Nagasaki puts a unique and deafening spin on the day of ancestor worship. Head down to the harbor for the main festivities, which involve far more alcohol and fireworks than is generally considered safe.

text: I really like Nagasaki. The trams are fun and make the city easy to navigate. The view of the bay from Glover's Garden is spectacular. The Museum is wonderful and there are some interesting temples along a street with a good view of Nagasaki.
snippet: I really like Nagasaki. The trams are fun and make the city easy to navigate. The view of the bay from Glover's Garden is spectacular. The Museum is wonderful and there are some interesting temples along a street with a good view of Nagasaki.

text: Nagasaki was extremely worth seeing. It is a gorgeous city with a beautiful harbor, one of the best night time views in Japan, and its history (Chinatown, Dejima, etc) was fascinating. Plus the food is really interesting and multicultural, as is the city's architecture (Meganebashi, etc.) I loved the time I spent in Nagasaki. But I am really interested in rangaku and foreign trade in Japan, so obviously that context colors how I see the city.
snippet: Nagasaki was extremely worth seeing. It is a gorgeous city with a beautiful harbor, one of the best night time views in Japan, and its history (Chinatown, Dejima, etc) was fascinating. Plus the food is really interesting and multicultural, as is the city's architecture (Meganebashi, etc.)

text: Utrecht is the Netherlands' fourth largest city, but undeservedly overlooked by many potential suitors seeking a long weekend getaway. Its compact medeival heart has a charm about it that will win you over the minute you arrive. Its cobbled streets, 200-year-old canals, wharf-side houses and quiet neighbourhoods are light years away from Amsterdam's hustle and bustle.
snippet: Utrecht is undeservedly overlooked by many potential suitors seeking a long weekend getaway. Its compact medeival heart has a charm about it that will win you over the minute you arrive. Its cobbled streets, 200-year-old canals, wharf-side houses and quiet neighbourhoods are light years away from Amsterdam's hustle and bustle.

text: Fun, safe, friendly city, close to nature, close to Amsterdam and Rotterdam, easy to get to know as an expat, great music venues, one of the best universities in Europe, historic city centre, lots of nice bars, decent place to raise kids.
snippet: Fun, safe, friendly city, close to nature, close to Amsterdam and Rotterdam, easy to get to know as an expat, great music venues, one of the best universities in Europe, historic city centre, lots of nice bars, decent place to raise kids.

text: For a more intimate experience, you could visit Touchstone, located next to the characteristic Utrecht canals. The Wilhelminapark, Park Lepelenburg and the Julianapark are nice places to chill out in summer.
snippet: For a more intimate experience, you could visit Touchstone, located next to the characteristic Utrecht canals. The Wilhelminapark, Park Lepelenburg and the Julianapark are nice places to chill out in summer.

text: The good thing about Utrecht is the balance between living and leisure. A lot of parcs. Nice canals. Perfect cycling architecture throughout the city. Nice old city center. The demolishing of the road through the city replacing it again with water is prove that the city really wants to keep the city liveable.
snippet: A lot of parcs. Nice canals. Perfect cycling architecture throughout the city. Nice old city center.

text: Utrecht is really lovely, and all we have seen it in is the rain, cold and winter gloom. I think it’s going to be absolutely fantastic when the weather improves and previous posters are spot on about the lack of car culture, plentiful great bars and restaurants, lots of boutique shops, gorg architecture… the place is just dripping with gorgeous ancient Northern European culture, it’s really quite something.
snippet: Utrecht is really lovely. The place is just dripping with gorgeous ancient Northern European culture, it’s really quite something.

text: We spent two full days exploring the temples, but wished we had one more day. The best time to visit Bagan depends on what you’re looking for: October – February has the best weather, with cooler temps (under 80 °F). These are the most popular months for tourists, especially in December and January. March – May has the hottest months, with temps of 90 °F (32 °C) or higher.
snippet: We spent two full days exploring the temples, but wished we had one more day. The most popular months for tourists are December and January.

text: Bagan, Myanmar, is a top-rated UNESCO World Heritage site known for thousands of stunning 11th–13th century temples, offering an "untouched" feel and world-class sunrise/sunset views, particularly via hot-air balloon. We highly recommend hiring an e-bike or guide to explore.
snippet: Bagan is a UNESCO World Heritage site known for thousands of stunning 11th–13th century temples, offering an "untouched" feel and world-class sunrise/sunset views, particularly via hot-air balloon. We highly recommend hiring an e-bike or guide to explore.

text: This place is amazing! I really wanted to be here and the expectations are fully met. A place that's striking. It takes a very long time to see everything.
snippet: This place is amazing! I really wanted to be here and the expectations are fully met. A place that's striking. It takes a very long time to see everything

text: The ancient city of Bagan (Pagan) is Myanmar’s most popular cultural site, and although I found it a lot more touristic than Mandalay or Shan State, it still remains a highlight of my recent trip. Every pagoda, temple and stupa is unique in its own way, but it’s still easy to get disoriented as you move through the pancake-flat landscape at ground level.
snippet: The ancient city of Bagan (Pagan) is Myanmar’s most popular cultural site, and although I found it a lot more touristic than Mandalay or Shan State, it still remains a highlight of my recent trip. Every pagoda, temple and stupa is unique in its own way!

text: People tend to rise late around Bagan, so touring early really emphasizes the sense of Bagan as "abandoned". Later in the day, particularly during the warm season, it may be uncomfortable to do this. In the dry season, bicycling the sandy paths connecting the more remote temples can be a harrowing exercise, but this is still the best way to get to where you want to go cheaply and freely.
snippet: People tend to rise late around Bagan, so touring early really emphasizes the sense of Bagan as "abandoned". In the dry season, bicycling the sandy paths connecting the more remote temples can be a harrowing exercise, but this is still the best way to get to where you want to go cheaply and freely.

text: Such a peaceful city! I went there at night and it felt so calm. Really really quiet town. It's also spotless, I didn't see any rubbish or homeless people or dangerous looking people, even though I've heard that from other people online...
snippet: Such a peaceful city! I went there at night and it felt so calm. Really really quiet town.

text: There is an ocean and a small beach where the good looking people hang on. Obviously only worthwhile in the summer. There are several parks in the center and a small canal. There are many small stores of the kind one can expect in a more diverse city than most of Scandinavia is.
snippet: There is an ocean and a small beach, so only worthwhile in the summer. There are several parks in the center and a small canal. There are many small stores of the kind one can expect in a more diverse city than most of Scandinavia is.

text: I love going to Malmö for day trips out of Copenhagen. The city is beautiful, the prices are good, it’s easy get to the city and back to Copenhagen. The people over there are kind and friendly. For me Malmö almost feels like the eastern part of Copenhagen. I would totally go and especially if you haven’t been to Sweden yet.
snippet: I love going to Malmö for day trips out of Copenhagen. The city is beautiful, the prices are good, it’s easy get to the city and back to Copenhagen.

text: In winter season, the same company runs a service to and from the ski resort Åre. Malmö's main train station, with a refurbished historic terminal building housing a small shopping centre, gastronomic options and other facilities.
snippet: In winter season, the same company runs a service to and from the ski resort Åre. Malmö's main train station, with a refurbished historic terminal building housing a small shopping centre, gastronomic options and other facilities.

text: Amsterdam is a large city and a major tourist destination, so you can visit it all year round. However, in winter the days are short (8 hours of daylight around Christmas between 08:45 and 16:30), and the weather may be too cold to walk around the city comfortably, let alone cycle.
snippet: Amsterdam is a large city and a major tourist destination, so you can visit it all year round. However, in winter the days are short (8 hours of daylight around Christmas between 08:45 and 16:30), and the weather may be too cold to walk around the city comfortably, let alone cycle.

text: While it would be possible to see Amsterdam's highlights in two days, I would recommend at least three days. It is a big city, especially if you venture just a little bit outside the tourist center, and there's plenty to keep you busy. Between museums, parks, coffee shops, and taking lots of pictures of the photogenic canals, you won't want to be rushed.
snippet: While it would be possible to see Amsterdam's highlights in two days, I would recommend at least three days. It is a big city, especially if you venture just a little bit outside the tourist center, and there's plenty to keep you busy.

text: The ambiance of Amsterdam alone is worth it. It’s a beautiful, unique city, so charming, and the people are lovely. Just sitting having a beer or coffee in the afternoon people watching is incredible. The Anne Frank house is a must visit, Vondelpark, the flower markets, the museums - totally worth it! Some believe it is boring or only focused on vices.
snippet: Some believe it is boring or only focused on vices but the ambiance of Amsterdam alone is worth it. It’s a beautiful, unique city, so charming, and the people are lovely. The Anne Frank house is a must visit, Vondelpark, the flower markets, the museums - totally worth it!

text: Amsterdam's culinary scene boasts a vibrant tapestry of flavours, offering a rich variety of dining experiences. The city unfolds as a treasure trove for those craving Asian cuisine, albeit catered to the milder local palate. Adventurous taste buds, fear not – it's not impossible to find spicy food.
snippet: Amsterdam's culinary scene boasts a vibrant tapestry of flavours, offering a rich variety of dining experiences.

text: New York City is one of the most famous cities in the world and easily one of the biggest tourist destinations out there. You’ve got Times Square, the Statue of Liberty, the Empire State Building, all the obvious landmarks that everyone knows. And honestly, no matter if you’re visiting from abroad or you’re American yourself, New York still has that wow factor when you see it in person.
snippet: You’ve got Times Square, the Statue of Liberty, the Empire State Building, all the obvious landmarks that everyone knows. And honestly, no matter if you’re visiting from abroad or you’re American yourself, New York still has that wow factor when you see it in person.

text: Summer is rough in NYC, especially mid-July through end of August (and possibly the first week or so of September). It's extremely humid and all the metal and blacktop in the city just absorbs and radiates heat and makes it way worse. May or October (or last week of September) are good times for visiting the city.
snippet: Summer is rough in NYC, especially mid-July through end of August (and possibly the first week or so of September). May or October (or last week of September) are good times for visiting the city.

text: If New York City is known as "the city that never sleeps," it makes complete sense that the nighttime is as hectic as the daytime full of attractions, activities, and entertainment for everyone and for every taste. Whatever you want to do, you will be spoiled with many options and choices. You want a romantic dinner in a good restaurant or some good live music in famous Clubs from Jazz & Blues Music to Rock & Roll and Pop, or perhaps a fun evening at the Theater to catch a classic Musical or an Off-Broadway Play, or maybe a Movie Theater with one of the largest IMAX screens in the world? You can have it all! Looking for breathtaking views? Then you can opt for an elegant cruise in the New York Bay, a cocktail & small bites in one of the popular Rooftops, a walk hand in hand in the most romantic and panoramic places or maybe a Limousine that takes you around the City ... Looking for more fun? What about a night at the Museum for an exclusive after-hours tour or maybe the world-renowned Opera at The Met followed by a classic Southern-style BBQ dinner ... As you can see the list goes on and on!
snippet: If New York City is known as "the city that never sleeps," it makes complete sense that the nighttime is as hectic as the daytime full of attractions, activities, and entertainment for everyone and for every taste. Whatever you want to do, you will be spoiled with many options and choices.

text: In general, common sense will keep you safe in Chicago: avoid unfamiliar side streets at night, stay out of alleys at night, know where you're going when you set out, stick to crowded areas, and keep a $20 bill on hand for cab fare as a bail-out option. Dress appropriately for the weather. Chicago's winter is famously windy and cold, so cover exposed skin and wear layers in the winter, but heat exhaustion is an equal risk in the summer months, especially July and August. That said, Chicago does have a few nice months of weather. May and September are pleasant and mild; April and June are mostly fine, although thunderstorms with heavy winds can also occur suddenly. Although there may be a slight chill in the air in October, it rarely calls for more than a light coat and some days that's not even necessary.
snippet: Chicago's winter is famously windy and cold, so cover exposed skin and wear layers in the winter, but heat exhaustion is an equal risk in the summer months, especially July and August. May and September are pleasant and mild; April and June are mostly fine, although thunderstorms with heavy winds can also occur suddenly.

text: Chicago is among the most diverse cities in America, and many neighborhoods reflect the character and culture of the immigrants who established them. Some, however, do more than just reflect: they absorb you in a place that, for several blocks at a time, may as well be a chunk of another country, picked up and dropped near the shores of Lake Michigan.
snippet: Chicago is among the most diverse cities in America, and many neighborhoods reflect the character and culture of the immigrants who established them.

text: Most neighborhoods, parishes, and service groups host their own annual festivals throughout the spring, summer, and fall. There are a few can't-miss citywide events, though. In the Loop, Grant Park hosts Taste of Chicago in July, and four major music festivals: Blues Fest and Gospel Fest in June, Lollapalooza in August, and Jazz Fest over Labor Day Weekend.
snippet: Most neighborhoods, parishes, and service groups host their own annual festivals throughout the spring, summer, and fall.

text: One of my favorite US cities! Metropolitan but not as intense as NYC, great cultural, music (Sweet Home Chicago!) and food scene. Easy to get to and easy to navigate. Always have a blast there.
snippet: One of my favorite US cities! Metropolitan but not as intense as NYC, great cultural, music (Sweet Home Chicago!) and food scene. Easy to get to and easy to navigate. Always have a blast there.

text: I stayed in chicago for 3 days/4 nights and I was outside for majority of my time there. I wish I had more time. Its very easy to travel around.... public transport there is tier 1. in general 10/10. Chicago is a highly recommended spot to visit by me. I want to go there again some time soon.
snippet: I stayed in chicago for 3 days/4 nights and I was outside for majority of my time there. I wish I had more time. Its very easy to travel around.... public transport there is tier 1.

text: If you’re in the area its definitely worth visiting. There are some decent restaurants (BBQ, Southern food, etc), bars, breweries and a few museums as well as a botanical garden. The caveat to this is if there is a particular event in the area. Something that lasts a weekend would be worthwhile.
snippet: If you’re in the area its definitely worth visiting. There are some decent restaurants (BBQ, Southern food, etc), bars, breweries and a few museums as well as a botanical garden. Something that lasts a weekend would be worthwhile.

text: Raleigh is suburban, as are almost all Sunbelt cities. It's suburban nature will bother any city purists and it bothered me at first, but it can get overstated online. Raleigh can be boring if you're accustomed to or prefer a larger city. Not the best nightlife, not the best museums, but the best parks, sporting events, walkability, recreation, restaurants, retail, downtown, urban neighborhoods, etc. It is a very, very strong parks and rec city. Ton of parks here, the best in NC's big cities, as a collective. I'd also say that while the depth and scale of Raleigh nightlife doesn't match Charlotte's, Glenwood is quite possibly the most lively nightlife district of anywhere in either city, so I'd argue that it's at least tied for the lead in premier nightlife district, in NC.
snippet: Raleigh is suburban, as are almost all Sunbelt cities. Raleigh can be boring if you're accustomed to or prefer a larger city. It is a very, very strong parks and rec city. Ton of parks here, the best in NC's big cities, as a collective. I'd also say that while the depth and scale of Raleigh nightlife doesn't match Charlotte's, it has one of the most lively nightlife district of anywhere in either city.

text: Raleigh is a nice place. I love living here. But, I wouldn’t call it a vacation destination. You could go to the museums (art, history, natural science), the various parks: (Umstead, Pullen, Dorothea Dix). You could go downtown or to Glenwood South if you like night life. There are plenty of good restaurants.
snippet: Raleigh is a nice place. But, I wouldn’t call it a vacation destination. You could go to the museums (art, history, natural science), the various parks: (Umstead, Pullen, Dorothea Dix). You could go downtown or to Glenwood South if you like night life. There are plenty of good restaurants.

text: Raleigh has two things that are world class: boba, and beers on tap. Milk Lab has been called the best boba shop ever, and Raleigh Beer Garden has more beers on tap than any bar in the world.
snippet: Raleigh has two things that are world class: boba, and beers on tap. Milk Lab has been called the best boba shop ever, and Raleigh Beer Garden has more beers on tap than any bar in the world.

text: Trondheim is definitely worth visiting. To make the most of your visit, explore this inviting city on foot to fully immerse yourself in its charm and to discover its highlights. Having lived in Norway over the years, in the pretty village of Skudeneshavn, we’ve been fortunate to road trip much of the country, visiting many of its iconic locations and witnessing its beauty for ourselves.
snippet: Trondheim is definitely worth visiting. To make the most of your visit, explore this inviting city on foot to fully immerse yourself in its charm and to discover its highlights.

text: This is the biggest church in Northern Europe, the only major Gothic cathedral in Norway and the pride of the city. Towering over the city centre at its southern edge, the majestic cathedral is the defining feature of Trondheim.
snippet: This is the biggest church in Northern Europe, the only major Gothic cathedral in Norway and the pride of the city. Towering over the city centre at its southern edge, the majestic cathedral is the defining feature of Trondheim.

text: The inhabitants like to call their city the historical, religious, and technological capital of Norway. Comparable to Scotland, the climate is oceanic and Trondheim is warmed by the Gulf Stream in the winter. Therefore the winters are much milder than you would expect at 63° north — temperatures of over +10°C can be encountered well into October.
snippet: The city is historical, religious, and the technological capital of Norway. The winters are much milder than you would expect at 63° north — temperatures of over +10°C can be encountered well into October.

text: Go skiing at Vassfjellet just outside Trondheim, in the season there's a bus service from Munkegata, and a Ski Shop with ski and snowboard rental service. Cross-country skiing is popular from November to April with hundreds of kilometres of tracks in Bymarka and Estenstadmarka.
snippet: Go skiing at Vassfjellet just outside Trondheim! Cross-country skiing is popular from November to April with hundreds of kilometres of tracks in Bymarka and Estenstadmarka.

text: Trondheim has food spots to suit every taste, though remember that eating out is generally very expensive, just like elsewhere in Norway.
snippet: Trondheim has food spots to suit every taste, though remember that eating out is generally very expensive, just like elsewhere in Norway.

text: Bruges offers a wide variety of attractions including historic landmarks, canals, museums, and scenic squares. Bruges is very nice, with lots of good food and art and pleasant places to walk.
snippet: Bruges offers a wide variety of attractions including historic landmarks, canals, museums, and scenic squares.

text: Ironically, the poverty that characterized Bruges in the 19th century is one reason the town's historic architecture is so well-preserved: there was usually simply no budget to modify the town on a large scale. However, in the 1890s two events revived interest in the city; on the cultural side the novel Bruges-la-Morte by Georges Rodenbach and on the economic side the construction of the Port of Zeebrugge, one of Western Europe's most important freight ports today. A canal from Zeebrugge to Bruges, the 12-km-long Boudewijnkanaal, was finished in 1905.
snippet: Ironically, the poverty that characterized Bruges in the 19th century is one reason the town's historic architecture is so well-preserved: there was usually…

text: Bruges is a trendy tourist destination at the moment, famous worldwide for it’s walkable city center and Old Europe charm. It is very walkable, but they also have horse-drawn carriages should you find yourself a bit lazy.
snippet: Bruges is a trendy tourist destination at the moment, famous worldwide for it’s walkable city center and Old Europe charm. It is very walkable, but they also have horse-drawn carriages should you find yourself a bit lazy.

text: If you are looking for a great place to eat in Bruges, avoid all of the restaurants in the central market square ("Grote Markt"). No matter how pleasant the view from there may look, they will offer you low-quality, overpriced food, served to you by waiters who are as rude and as slow as they are underpaid.
snippet: If you are looking for a great place to eat in Bruges, avoid all of the restaurants in the central market square ("Grote Markt"). No matter how pleasant the view from there may look, they will offer you low-quality, overpriced food, served to you by waiters who are as rude and as slow as they are underpaid.

text: Summer starts with a monsoon season that brings heavy rains in June and July, often leading the Han River to flood. Humidity is very high: the thermometer may read 30 °C (86 °F) during the day and 24 °C (75 °F) at night, but it can feel much hotter. Due to the proximity of Siberia, winters in Seoul can be very cold, colder than you would expect at these latitudes, with temperatures at times dropping below −10 °C (14 °F) in January and February, and wind-chill can make it feel even colder.
snippet: Summer starts with a monsoon season that brings heavy rains in June and July, often leading the Han River to flood. Due to the proximity of Siberia, winters in Seoul can be very cold.

text: This frantic pace of life is reflected everywhere – in Seoul's cutting-edge digital technology, in the millions of commuters rushing to work everyday in the world's third largest subway system, in one of the most vibrant nightlife scenes in the world, and in the thousands of high rises and apartment buildings still under construction. Considering all of this, one may be forgiven for forgetting that Seoul has a long history stretching far back into Korea's dynastic past. There is evidence for settlement in this area as far back as 18 BCE, but Seoul as the capital city of South Korea has a history dating back to the 14th century.
snippet: This frantic pace of life is reflected everywhere – in Seoul's cutting-edge digital technology, in the millions of commuters rushing to work everyday in the world's third largest subway system, in one of the most vibrant nightlife scenes in the world, and in the thousands of high rises and apartment buildings still under construction.

text: The coffee culture in Seoul is one of the biggest in the world. The generic corner shops may not be the best, but if you know where to go, the coffee bean sourcing and quality of the cup preparation can be world class.
snippet: The coffee culture in Seoul is one of the biggest in the world. If you know where to go, the coffee bean sourcing and quality of the cup preparation can be world class.

text: One of my favorite things about Seoul is how easy it is to experience Korean culture without booking tours or hunting things down. You see it in everyday life. People wearing hanbok around palaces, elders playing games in parks, cafés packed at all hours, and late-night meals being completely normal.
snippet: One of my favorite things about Seoul is how easy it is to experience Korean culture without booking tours or hunting things down. You see it in everyday life.

text: For a city this big, Seoul felt very budget-friendly. Food was inexpensive, public transportation was super cheap as well as grabs, and even accommodation felt reasonable compared to other major cities.
snippet: For a city this big, Seoul felt very budget-friendly. Food was inexpensive, public transportation was super cheap as well as grabs, and even accommodation felt reasonable compared to other major cities.

text: Ankara is worth visiting if your interests include modern Turkish history, museums, urban culture, and a quieter alternative to Istanbul. It’s not a romanticized Ottoman streetscape or coastal resort, but it rewards visitors who look for politics, architecture, food, and everyday Turkish life with concentrated, accessible experiences.
snippet: Ankara is worth visiting if your interests include modern Turkish history, museums, urban culture, and a quieter alternative to Istanbul. It rewards visitors who look for politics, architecture, food, and everyday Turkish life with concentrated, accessible experiences.

text: Although it never wrested the cultural crown from Istanbul, Ankara has a rich heritage in its museums, galleries and mosques, and tourists who just transit on the way to Cappadocia are missing an important chapter in the story of Turkey and Asia Minor. The city was already old when the ancient Greeks called it "the anchor", Аγκυρα, which to a sea-faring people indicated a base or home.
snippet: Ankara has a rich heritage in its museums, galleries and mosques.

text: Unless you are really interested in Turkish government, seeing Ataturk’s grave, or visiting your country’s embassy, I’d suggest skipping it. Spend time in Cappadocia or a small town on the Mediterranean Sea instead.
snippet: Unless you are really interested in Turkish government, seeing Ataturk’s grave, or visiting your country’s embassy, I’d suggest skipping it. Spend time in Cappadocia or a small town on the Mediterranean Sea instead.

text: Nights are cold but daytimes are mild and dry, unlike the wet spring months. Winters are cold and often snowy, though the snow is seldom heavy, and cold below -20°C is very rare.
snippet: Nights are cold but daytimes are mild and dry, unlike the wet spring months. Winters are cold and often snowy, though the snow is seldom heavy, and cold below -20°C is very rare.

text: At night, the area is lit up and food stalls are set up with a variety of delicious snacks. Northern Kyoto offers cherry-blossom scouts worthwhile experiences at Hirano Shrine and Kyoto Botanical Gardens, and a walk inside the large grounds of Daigo-ji in Southern Kyoto is certainly made memorable when all the blossoms are in full bloom. Although they are less well-known to foreign tourists, who tend only to focus their attentions on seeing cherry blossoms, for those with plans to visit Kyoto from mid-February through mid-March, plum blossom viewing makes for a great alternative.
snippet: At night, the area is lit up and food stalls are set up with a variety of delicious snacks. Northern Kyoto offers cherry-blossom scouts worthwhile experiences at Hirano Shrine and Kyoto Botanical Gardens, and a walk inside the large grounds of Daigo-ji in Southern Kyoto is certainly made memorable when all the blossoms are in full bloom.

text: Your hotel concierge may be able to make a reservation for you if you contact them well in advance, though only the most expensive luxury hotels have the necessary clout to do this. As with anywhere else in Japan, most fine dining establishments do not accept credit cards, and you will have to pay for your meal in cash. Consider staying at one of Kyoto's top ryokan; elaborate kaiseki dinners are an essential part of a stay in a ryokan, and there are numerous ryokan that are just as well known for their food as their lodging experience.
snippet: As with anywhere else in Japan, most fine dining establishments do not accept credit cards, and you will have to pay for your meal in cash.

text: Kyoto is particularly humid in summer, as the city is flanked by mountains. From about mid-June to the end of July is the rainy season, so most travelers avoid this time. The type of rain ranges from drizzles to off-and-on showers to downpours.
snippet: Kyoto is particularly humid in summer, as the city is flanked by mountains. From about mid-June to the end of July is the rainy season, so most travelers avoid this time.

text: Kyoto is a must if you go to Japan. Kyoto was the capital of Japan for a long time, and has many beautiful temples and shrines. Yes, it is very touristy, but still, I would recommend it. Kyoto represents the old Japan, and comparing modern Japan (Tokyo) and Kyoto will be fun!
snippet: Kyoto is a must if you go to Japan. Kyoto was the capital of Japan for a long time, and has many beautiful temples and shrines.

text: Kyoto was my absolute favourite city in Japan. I took advantage of my jetlag and would eat breakfast watching the sunrise on the river bank. Then I’d arrive at my first temple just as it opens so you have the place to yourself (and some random grannies). I spent 5 days in Kyoto and would walk almost 11km a day. That being said, I LOVE temple. I love the history and architecture and the different goshuin. I’m not a big partier and I hate clubs, so I didn’t care about the less than stellar night life.
snippet: Kyoto was my absolute favourite city in Japan. I’d arrive at my first temple just as it opens so you have the place to yourself. I love the history and architecture and the different goshuin. I’m not a big partier and I hate clubs, so I didn’t care about the less than stellar night life.

text: Tampa Riverwalk. Tampa Riverwalk has been nearly 30 years in the making, and finally connects the vibrant Tampa Heights district with the heart of Downtown, all the way to the Florida Aquarium in the Channel District. Along the 2.4-mile walkway are many parks, including the incredibly popular Curtis Hixon, the Tampa Performing Arts Center, the Amalie Arena where the Tampa Bay Lightning play, and multiple hotels, bars, and restaurants.
snippet: Tampa Riverwalk has been nearly 30 years in the making, and finally connects the vibrant Tampa Heights district with the heart of Downtown, all the way to the Florida Aquarium in the Channel District. Along the 2.4-mile walkway are many parks, and multiple hotels, bars, and restaurants.

text: Tropical systems and hurricanes may occasionally threaten the region in the late summer or fall.
snippet: Tropical systems and hurricanes may occasionally threaten the region in the late summer or fall.

text: It's cool to just walk around as there is a sidewalk by the river that runs through downtown, endless amounts of food, and it's just a cool place to be overall. A nice added bonus is that you're also 30 min away from downtown St. Pete, which I would recommend you checking out if you're into breweries/food.
snippet: It's cool to just walk around as there is a sidewalk by the river that runs through downtown, endless amounts of food, and it's just a cool place to be overall. A nice added bonus is that you're also 30 min away from downtown St. Pete, which I would recommend you checking out if you're into breweries/food.

text: Tampa’s historic Latin Quarter, Ybor City, is the real highlight of any visit here. It's famous for its lively, even often rowdy, bar and nightclub scene, but by day it’s a charming, calm spot to wander around, with Cuban artisans still hand-rolling cigars as they have since 1885 and a free-roaming wild chicken population. To make the most of it, join a guided outing with Ybor City Walking Tours and learn about how this fascinating neighbourhood became a magnet for immigrants from Cuba, Spain, Germany, Italy and Eastern Europe. Also, don’t miss a ride on the free historic wooden streetcar back from Ybor City to Downtown.
snippet: Tampa’s historic Latin Quarter, Ybor City, is the real highlight of any visit here. It's famous for its lively, even often rowdy, bar and nightclub scene, but by day it’s a charming, calm spot to wander around, with Cuban artisans still hand-rolling cigars as they have since 1885 and a free-roaming wild chicken population.

There are a lot of old buildings and history, but it doesn’t feel boring. Kazimierz was our favorite area because it felt more relaxed and less touristy. There were lots of places to eat and sit without feeling rushed. Overall, Krakow is a great place to visit. It feels safe, affordable, and easy to enjoy even if you don’t plan much. We’d definitely go back!

text: There are four definite seasons to Kraków - summer being hot and humid (average highs of 25°C) and winter always seeing Kraków under a blanket of snow with bitingly cold days (average lows of -3/-4°C). Due to little wind, Kraków suffers from high levels of air pollution from cars and coal stoves, which especially affects children and people with respiratory problems. Kraków is the most popular tourist destination in Poland and this supports much of the local economy.
snippet: Krakow summers are hot and humid and winters have bitingly cold days.

text: The Siq & The Treasury: One of Earth's Most Jaw-Dropping Reveals — Walking the Siq — the narrow, winding 1.2km slot canyon leading into Petra — is one of the most theatrical journeys in travel, its towering pink-sandstone walls narrowing overhead until they suddenly part to reveal the Treasury (Al-Khazneh) carved in perfect detail into the clifface. Travelers who have visited dozens of world wonders consistently say this moment is unlike anything else — not just visually stunning but emotionally overwhelming, a site that makes you genuinely struggle to comprehend that human hands carved it 2,000 years ago. The combination of ancient Nabataean engineering, rose-red geology, desert light, and sheer scale makes it a once-in-a-lifetime experience that photographs simply cannot prepare you for. heatheronhertravels+1
snippet: "The walk through the Siq to the Treasury is just breathtaking — the colors of the rock, the narrowing of the canyon walls, and then suddenly it just opens up and there it is. I was not prepared for how emotional it would feel. I've been to the Colosseum, the Pyramids, Machu Picchu — nothing compared to that first glimpse of the Treasury. I stood there for about 20 minutes just staring. Utterly magnificent." tripadvisor

text: The Monastery: The Harder Hike, the Greater Reward — While the Treasury is Petra's famous face, seasoned travelers insist that Ad Deir — the Monastery — reached by climbing over 800 rock-cut steps, is the true highlight of the entire site. Its facade is even larger than the Treasury, it sits in near-total solitude compared to the crowds below, and the hiking trail itself passes sweeping canyon viewpoints, Bedouin tea stalls, and ancient carved niches that make the climb feel like a journey through time. Those who push through the physical effort consistently say it's the single best thing they did in all of Jordan. earthtrekkers+1
snippet: "The Monastery is Petra's best kept secret — bigger than the Treasury, far fewer people, and the hike up is genuinely beautiful in its own right. Stop for tea with the Bedouin family halfway up — they've lived in these caves for generations. When you finally emerge in front of that enormous facade, alone or nearly so, it hits differently than the Treasury. More intimate. More ancient. More real. Don't even think about skipping it." earthtrekkers

text: Petra by Night: Lanterns, Stars & Oriental Music in the Desert Dark — Three nights a week, Petra transforms into something almost supernatural — the entire Siq lined with hundreds of candles in paper bags, the Treasury lit by soft lantern light against a star-filled desert sky, while traditional Bedouin music drifts through the canyon. Travelers describe it as one of the most atmospherically beautiful experiences in the world, a complete contrast to the daytime crush, where the silence and the light create a deeply meditative mood that stays with them long after they've left Jordan. tripadvisor+1
snippet: "Petra by Night was one of the most magical experiences of my life. Walking through the Siq in near darkness with only candlelight guiding you, then arriving at the Treasury glowing under lanterns with the stars above — I genuinely got goosebumps. The music echoing off the canyon walls made it feel like the ancient city was alive again. I'd go back to Jordan just to do this again." tripadvisor

text: Beyond the Postcard: Royal Tombs, Roman Theatre & Hidden Trails — Most visitors barely scratch the surface of Petra, turning back after the Treasury. But Petra is a vast archaeological city — beyond the Treasury lie a Roman colonnaded street, a Nabataean theatre carved entirely from rock, enormous Royal Tombs honeycombing the cliffs, an ancient church with intact Byzantine mosaics, and dozens of hidden hiking trails that deliver views of the entire rose-red valley from above. Travelers who give it two full days consistently say it transformed from a "tick on the bucket list" into the single most extraordinary place they've ever visited. responsibletravel+1
snippet: "Petra complex was so much more than just the treasury — you need at least two full days to see it properly. The Royal Tombs, the colonnaded street, the theatre — all of it is extraordinary. On day two we did the High Place of Sacrifice hike and looked down over all of it. I kept thinking: this is a whole ancient city. Most people only see the front door." responsibletravel

text: The Honest Reality: Vendor Pressure, Heat & Why It's Still Worth Every Step — Petra is not without challenges — persistent vendors offering camel and donkey rides, intense summer heat requiring careful planning, and the sheer physical demand of 15–20km of walking over two days all require preparation. Reddit travelers and bloggers are candid about the relentless sales pressure in the canyon, particularly near the Treasury. Yet almost universally, even the most skeptical travelers conclude it belongs on every bucket list — the sheer scale, antiquity, and beauty of Petra is simply too extraordinary to be dimmed by the chaos around it. reddit+1
snippet: "First, Petra's attractions are amazing. Just can't believe this was built here when it was — and they are still unearthing structures. Despite all the unpleasantness of constant pestering, I would still recommend seeing Petra if you are a lover of ancient ruins and beautiful landscapes. Jordan as a country overall was warm, diverse, and open-minded. The sheer antiquity of the place makes it special and I loved contemplating the fact that some of the earliest foundations of human civilization developed here." reddit+1
