text: Luanda felt modern and cosmopolitan in parts, with upscale restaurants, nightlife, and residential areas, but also had visible poverty, pollution, street children, and chaotic traffic. Public transportation was not practical and recommended using a taxi app or driver, which makes Luanda better for travelers who want structure and local support rather than independent wandering.
snippet: Luanda felt modern and cosmopolitan in parts, with upscale restaurants, nightlife, and residential areas, but also had visible poverty, pollution, street children, and chaotic traffic.

text: Luanda is a city of jaw-dropping contrasts: gleaming oil-funded skyscrapers, rooftop bars, and high-end expat restaurants sit within walking distance of crumbling colonial-era buildings where people lack basic plumbing. Tourist attractions are admittedly limited — there's the original Portuguese fort overlooking the waterfront, a small ethnographic museum with impressive traditional masks, and a day trip to the Museum of Slavery in an old colonial church. Day trips out of the city shine brightest: a two-hour boat ride at the mouth of the Kwanza River offers sightings of monkeys, crocodiles, and exotic birds. This is a destination for adventurous travelers who want raw, unfiltered urban Africa mixed with wildlife and colonial history, not a polished resort experience.
snippet: Luanda boasts an abundance of upscale restaurants, nightclubs, and residential complexes, yet it's striking to see areas with extreme poverty just steps away. The waterfront at sunset with the city skyline behind you is genuinely beautiful — don't miss it.

text: The Luanda Bay Waterfront (Marginal) is the city's social heartbeat — a popular promenade where locals and tourists walk, dine at waterfront restaurants, and watch stunning sunsets over the Atlantic. The Museum of Currency nearby is a quirky gem with old Angolan coins and monetary history. Luanda's nightlife is vibrant and unique: clubs play a hypnotic blend of Kuduro and Afrobeat with hip-hop undertones, and the crowd is stylish, social, and welcoming to foreigners. Bars like Twenty (with ocean views and great cocktails) and Tailor Cocktail Bar (run by a gin expert named Edson, beloved by both expats and locals) are consistently praised. If you love beach sunsets, lively music scenes, and socializing with welcoming locals, Luanda delivers.
snippet: The people in Angola are extremely social and friendly — this makes things very fun for a solo traveler. The waterfront at sunset with the city skyline behind you is genuinely beautiful so don't miss it.

text: New Orleans is unlike any other American city — it's a sensory overload in the best possible way. The French Quarter's iconic wrought-iron balconies dripping with Mardi Gras beads, the smell of beignets and chicory coffee from Café Du Monde at 2am, and the constant soundtrack of live jazz pouring out of every open door on Frenchmen Street create an atmosphere that feels more like a Caribbean port city than the American South. History is absolutely everywhere: the St. Louis Cathedral, the oldest continuously operating cathedral in the US, overlooks Jackson Square where artists, tarot readers, and street performers have gathered for centuries. The Garden District is perfect for a self-guided walking tour through antebellum mansions draped in Spanish moss, while Lafayette Cemetery No. 1 — with its above-ground tombs — is hauntingly beautiful and deeply tied to the city's French and Creole heritage. The food scene alone justifies the trip: a muffuletta from Central Grocery, a bowl of gumbo at Dooky Chase's, and a plate of chargrilled oysters at Drago's is a culinary holy trinity you'll be dreaming about for years.
snippet: New Orleans hits you all at once — the jazz, the food, the history — and before you know it, you've been sitting on a balcony on Frenchmen Street for four hours and you don't regret a single minute of it.

text: For a history lover, New Orleans is basically a living museum that also serves cocktails. The city's layered past — French colonial, Spanish rule, Creole culture, enslaved African traditions, and American expansion — is visible in nearly every neighborhood. The Whitney Plantation, about an hour west of the city, is one of the most powerful and sobering historical sites in the entire country, offering an unflinching look at the brutality of slavery through first-person slave narratives carved into memorials. Back in the city, the Historic Voodoo Museum in the French Quarter is quirky and intimate, giving real context to the African spiritual traditions that survived slavery and merged into Louisiana's unique folk culture. The Cabildo museum on Jackson Square holds the original Louisiana Purchase transfer documents — you're literally looking at the moment America doubled in size. Walking the Tremé neighborhood, the oldest African American neighborhood in the US, you feel the weight of jazz history, civil rights struggle, and resilience all at once. This city demands to be walked slowly, with a cold Abita beer in hand and zero agenda.
snippet: I came for the food and parties and left having learned more American history than I did in four years of high school — the Whitney Plantation alone changed something in me.

text: The food culture in New Orleans is so deep and specific that it deserves its own trip entirely. This is not just Southern food — it's a completely distinct culinary tradition shaped by French technique, West African ingredients, Spanish influence, and Creole ingenuity. Start your mornings at Café Du Monde for beignets (get the takeout window to skip the line) and work your way up to a dressed roast beef po'boy from Parkway Bakery & Tavern, which many locals consider the city's finest. The Central Business District and Warehouse District have spawned a newer generation of chef-driven restaurants: Compère Lapin blends Caribbean and Louisiana flavors brilliantly, and Herbsaint has been a benchmark of refined Creole cooking for decades. Don't skip the Vietnamese food either — the city has a large Vietnamese community, and the Versailles neighborhood (Village de L'Est) serves some of the most authentic pho and bánh mì in the South. New Orleans also has a serious cocktail heritage: the Sazerac (allegedly the world's first cocktail), the Ramos Gin Fizz, and the Vieux Carré were all invented here, and sipping one at the Sazerac Bar in the Roosevelt Hotel feels genuinely ceremonial.
snippet: I had a roast beef po'boy from Parkway and a bowl of gumbo from Dooky Chase's on the same afternoon and genuinely felt like I had cracked the code to life — the food here is on another level.

text: Most of the world's coral reefs biodiversity and rare species of marine life can easily be found in an archipelago about the size of Switzerland. While there are 612 islands, there are four major islands ("Raja Ampat" means "Four Kings") that have a population center: Waigeo, Misool (which is home to ancient rock paintings), Salawati, and Batanta. Its location in Papua, one of the furthest places you can get to in Indonesia, may give you a head shake.
snippet: Most of the world's coral reefs biodiversity and rare species of marine life can easily be found in an archipelago about the size of Switzerland.

text: This town is the starting point for exploration in the area, as it contains the district's airport and seaport. The best time to visit Raja Ampat is during the dry October–April period, which is by contrast the rainiest period in western and central Indonesia.

text: Hamilton Island is the kind of place that makes you question every life decision that led you to living somewhere without a coral reef in the backyard. Sitting in the heart of the Whitsunday Islands off the Queensland coast, it's one of Australia's most beloved resort destinations — and for good reason. The island serves as the ultimate launchpad for the Great Barrier Reef, with day trips to Hardy Reef and Heart Reef departing daily by boat or seaplane. The famous Whitehaven Beach, a 7km stretch of 98% pure silica sand so white it doesn't absorb heat, is a 30-minute boat ride away and consistently ranks among the top beaches in the world. On the island itself, the Passage Peak hike (about 45 minutes up a forested trail) rewards with a panoramic view over the Whitsundays that will genuinely stop your breath. Accommodation ranges from budget-friendly Reef View Hotel rooms all the way to qualia, an ultra-luxury adults-only retreat perched on the northern headland with private plunge pools and personal golf buggies — the preferred mode of transport island-wide. Whether you're a snorkeler, hiker, sailor, or someone who simply wants to do absolutely nothing in a beautiful place, Hamilton Island threads the needle between adventure and relaxation better than almost anywhere in the Pacific.
snippet: I booked Hamilton Island thinking it would be a typical resort trip and ended up snorkeling the Great Barrier Reef, hiking through the bush at sunrise, and sailing into a Whitsunday sunset all in the same day. It exceeded every expectation I had.

text: Hamilton Island has the most places to stay, restaurants and shops of all the Whitsunday Islands. Whereas many of the Whitsunday Islands only feature a single resort, Hamilton Island features many resorts and hotels. Infrastructure is on the north side of the island.
snippet: Hamilton Island has the most places to stay, restaurants and shops of all the Whitsunday Islands.

text: For sailing and watersports lovers, Hamilton Island is quite simply one of the best bases in the Southern Hemisphere. The Whitsunday passage between the islands is sheltered, warm, and consistently breezy — perfect conditions for beginners and experienced sailors alike. Hamilton Island Race Week, held every August, is one of the largest keelboat regattas in the Asia Pacific and draws hundreds of yachts from around the world, transforming the marina into a buzzing festival of boats, sun-bronzed sailors, and open-air parties. Even outside of race week, you can rent a bareboat catamaran with no prior experience and legally sail the Whitsundays with just a basic briefing — a level of freedom that still feels wild by most countries' standards.
snippet: We rented a catamaran with zero sailing experience, the briefing was about 20 minutes, and then we were just out there sailing between these jaw-dropping islands on our own. I still can't believe that's legal and I mean that in the best possible way.

text: Melbourne is the kind of city that rewards slow, aimless wandering more than any itinerary ever could. The laneways of the CBD — Hosier Lane with its rotating street art murals, Degraves Street lined with espresso bars and French-style cafés, Centre Place buzzing with lunch crowds — form a hidden city within the city that most visitors only scratch the surface of. The coffee culture here is world-famous and completely justified: Melbourne essentially invented the flat white and takes its espresso so seriously that a Starbucks famously failed and closed here. The National Gallery of Victoria on St Kilda Road is Australia's most visited art museum and perpetually underrated on the international stage, housing an extraordinary collection from ancient antiquities to contemporary Australian Aboriginal art. Weekend mornings at the Queen Victoria Market — fresh produce, sourdough, baklava, and the best free-range eggs you've ever bought — feel like a civic ritual that half the city participates in together. Melbourne is endlessly liveable, creative, and culturally layered in a way that takes multiple visits to fully appreciate.
snippet: Melbourne hit me differently to every other city I've visited in Australia. The laneways, the coffee, the art scene — it feels more like a European capital that somehow ended up in the Southern Hemisphere.

text: Melbourne's food scene is one of the most genuinely diverse and high-quality in the world, shaped by successive waves of immigration that have left permanent, delicious marks on the city. Lygon Street in Carlton is the heart of Melbourne's Italian community and has been serving handmade pasta and wood-fired pizza since the 1950s. Victoria Street in Richmond is lined with Vietnamese restaurants serving pho, banh mi, and sugar cane prawn skewers that rival anything you'd find in Hanoi. The Greek precinct on Lonsdale Street, the Lebanese strip on Sydney Road in Brunswick, and the Chinese BBQ restaurants of Chinatown all operate at a standard that reflects communities cooking the food they grew up with, not performing it for tourists. Beyond the ethnic precincts, Melbourne's chef-driven restaurant scene at places like Attica, Lûmé, and Tipo 00 has genuine international standing. Budget travelers eat extraordinarily well here too — a bowl of pho on Victoria Street or a banh mi from one of Richmond's Vietnamese bakeries will cost you under $15 and be absolutely outstanding.
snippet: I ate my way through Richmond, Carlton, and Chinatown in one weekend and genuinely couldn't tell you which meal was best. Melbourne's food scene is the most underrated in the world and it's not particularly close.

text: Chinese cuisine has a long tradition in Melbourne and a large number and range of quality restaurants exist. Many are in Chinatown in Little Bourke Street, CBD. They are also dotted through the inner and outer suburbs, with concentrations in Richmond, Footscray, and suburban Box Hill, Glen Waverley, Doncaster and Springvale.
snippet: Chinese cuisine has a long tradition in Melbourne and a large number and range of quality restaurants exist.

text: Italian cafes and restaurants are plentiful throughout Melbourne but are in the greatest concentration in Lygon Street, Carlton, just north of the CBD. Lygon Street is where Melbourne's coffee culture originated. Suburban Italian restaurants are often large and family-oriented and tend towards the pizza, pasta, seafood and steak formula.
snippet: Italian cafes and restaurants are plentiful throughout Melbourne but are in the greatest concentration in Lygon Street, Carlton, just north of the CBD.

text: Vegans looking to experience the city's coffee culture will find soy, oat, or almond milk at most cafes. Melbourne's Little Vietnams are in Footscray, North Richmond and Springvale out in the far eastern suburbs. The streets in these areas are lined with pho (noodle) shops and restaurants offering other Vietnamese favourites.
snippet: Vegans looking to experience the city's coffee culture will find soy, oat, or almond milk at most cafes.

text: It is in the Puebla Valley, surrounded by volcanoes and snow-capped mountains, slightly over 110 km (68 miles) south-east of Mexico City. It has been listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site because of its architecture. Puebla is also considered to be the "cradle of Mexican Baroque" in architecture and in the decorative arts, and one of the five most important colonial cities in Mexico.
snippet: Puebla snuck up on me completely. I came for a night on the way to Oaxaca and stayed four days. The architecture, the food, the volcano on the horizon — it just gets under your skin.

text: Puebla's food scene is the reason many Mexicans consider it the culinary capital of the country, and after eating your way through the city for a few days it is very hard to argue against that claim. Mole Poblano, the complex sauce of dried chilies, chocolate, and spices that is Mexico's most iconic dish, was invented here and the versions served in Puebla's family-run restaurants bear almost no resemblance to the jarred versions sold abroad. Chiles en Nogada — roasted poblano peppers stuffed with spiced meat and fruit, topped with walnut cream sauce and pomegranate seeds in the colors of the Mexican flag — is one of the most beautiful and historically meaningful dishes in the world and is best eaten here in season between July and September. The Mercado El Parian and the street food stalls around 5 de Mayo serve cemitas (Puebla's signature sandwich on a sesame roll with avocado, chipotle, and pulled meat) for a few pesos, and they are outrageously good.
snippet: I had Mole Poblano in three different restaurants in Puebla trying to find the definitive version and by the third bowl I realized the whole city is the answer. The food here is on a completely different level.

text: For history lovers, Puebla layers pre-Hispanic, colonial, and modern Mexican history in a way that is remarkably easy to absorb just by walking the streets. The Museo Amparo is one of the finest pre-Columbian art museums in Mexico, housed in a beautifully restored 17th-century hospital building, with pieces spanning Olmec, Maya, Teotihuacan, and Aztec civilizations presented with thoughtful, bilingual context. The nearby Fuertes de Loreto y Guadalupe hilltop forts are where Mexican forces famously defeated the French army on May 5th, 1862 — the real origin of Cinco de Mayo, which is actually a bigger deal in Puebla than almost anywhere else in Mexico.
snippet: For history lovers, Puebla layers pre-Hispanic, colonial, and modern Mexican history in a way that is remarkably easy to absorb just by walking the streets.

text: Richmond is one of the most historically complex and culturally fascinating cities on the East Coast, and it rewards travelers who come curious and open-minded. As the former capital of the Confederacy, the city carries a layered, sometimes uncomfortable history that it has been grappling with publicly and honestly in recent years — the removal of the Monument Avenue Confederate statues sparked a national conversation, and the boulevard is now being reimagined in a way that reflects the full breadth of Richmond's story. The American Civil War Museum at Tredegar Iron Works, set in an actual Civil War-era factory on the James River, is one of the finest history museums in the South, presenting Union and Confederate perspectives side by side with remarkable nuance. The African American History Trail connects sites across the city tied to slavery, resistance, and the Civil Rights Movement, including the Lumpkin's Jail site where enslaved people were held before auction at what was once the largest slave trading complex in North America. Richmond doesn't sanitize its past, and that honesty makes it one of the most thought-provoking American cities you can visit.
snippet: I came to Richmond expecting a quiet Southern city and left having had some of the most meaningful conversations about American history I've ever had. The city doesn't hide from its past and that makes it genuinely unforgettable.

text: Many of Richmond's fine restaurants may be found in the historic district downtown called Shockoe Bottom, in the Downtown area.
snippet: Many of Richmond's fine restaurants may be found in the historic district downtown called Shockoe Bottom, in the Downtown area.

text: Richmond's arts and music scene gives the city an identity that extends far beyond its Civil War reputation, and the creative energy here is palpable in almost every neighborhood. The Virginia Museum of Fine Arts (VMFA) is one of the best free art museums in the United States, with a permanent collection spanning ancient Egyptian artifacts, Fabergé eggs, American modernism, and a particularly strong South Asian and Himalayan art wing that most visitors don't see coming. The Institute for Contemporary Art at VCU — a striking Holl-designed building on the Virginia Commonwealth University campus — hosts rotating exhibitions that consistently attract national attention and are free to the public. Richmond has a long, proud history as a punk and indie rock incubator: the city produced GWAR, nurtured a thriving hardcore scene in the 1990s, and today supports a dense ecosystem of live music venues including The National, Richmond Music Hall, and the intimate Camel in the Fan District. The street art throughout Arts District, Manchester, and Church Hill rivals cities with far bigger creative reputations — entire building facades transformed by local and international muralists make walking these neighborhoods a constantly evolving gallery experience.
snippet: The VMFA alone would justify a trip to Richmond, and then you walk outside and realize the entire city is an art gallery. I spent a whole afternoon just photographing murals in Manchester and barely covered two blocks.

text: Bridgetown is the kind of Caribbean capital that manages to feel both genuinely historic and effortlessly alive at the same time, and that balance is rarer than it sounds in the region. As a UNESCO World Heritage Site, the city's colonial core — Broad Street, National Heroes Square, and the Nidhe Israel Synagogue (one of the oldest in the Americas, dating to 1654) — tells the story of British colonialism, Jewish merchant settlers, and African enslaved communities layered on top of each other in just a few walkable blocks. The Barbados Museum and Historical Society housed in a former British military prison is one of the finest small history museums in the Caribbean, with an honest and detailed account of the island's plantation economy and the lives of enslaved Africans. The Careenage, the inner harbor where wooden schooners and tourist catamarans bob alongside each other in the late afternoon light, is the city's social and visual centerpiece and one of the most photogenic waterfronts in the entire Caribbean. Bridgetown is compact, walkable, and layered enough to anchor several days of genuine exploration without ever feeling like you're retreading the same ground.
snippet: I spent two full days in Bridgetown just following my nose through the streets. The history here is deep and the city wears it openly in a way that made me want to keep asking questions.

text: In the 18th and 19th century the whole area was a military base, centred on the Savannah, the parade ground and sports fields, which are now the island's racetrack. There are many attractive old buildings here, with the chief sights being the George Washington House and the Barbados Museum east of the highway, and St Ann's Fort and Needham's Point to the west. All the buses and minibuses from Bridgetown towards Oistins and south-side hotels run this way.
snippet: There are many attractive old buildings here, with the chief sights being the George Washington House and the Barbados Museum east of the highway, and St Ann's Fort and Needham's Point to the west.

text: Bridgetown sits at the center of one of the most varied and accessible beach coastlines in the Caribbean, with dramatically different beach personalities just minutes apart by car or bus. The Platinum Coast running north from Bridgetown along the calm, clear west shore offers the quintessential Caribbean postcard: turquoise water, white sand, gentle surf, and luxury beach clubs at spots like Sandy Lane and Paynes Bay where you can rent a sun lounger and watch superyachts anchor offshore. In complete contrast, the southeast and east coasts around Bathsheba deliver wild Atlantic surf conditions, dramatic rock formations, and a raw, windswept beauty that feels nothing like the manicured west coast — the Soup Bowl at Bathsheba is one of the best surf breaks in the Caribbean and draws serious surfers from around the world. Carlisle Bay, right on Bridgetown's doorstep, is an underrated snorkeling destination with several shipwrecks in shallow, clear water that are easily accessible without scuba gear. The ability to switch between calm Caribbean luxury beaches and dramatic Atlantic surf coastline within a 45-minute drive is something very few islands in the region can offer.
snippet: For live music lovers, keep an eye out for performances by Barbados Tuk Bands or calypso shows. We spent one morning on a calm, glassy west coast beach and the next afternoon watching surfers get absolutely pounded at Bathsheba on the Atlantic side. It felt like two completely different islands and that contrast is exactly what makes Barbados special.

text: Oaxaca City is surrounded by some of the most dramatic and varied natural and archaeological landscapes in Mexico, making it one of the best-positioned cities in the country for day trips and multi-day excursions. Monte Albán, the great Zapotec hilltop city built around 500 BC and occupied for over a thousand years, sits just 9 kilometers from the city center and delivers sweeping views across the Oaxacan valley from its grand central plaza — arriving at opening time before the tour groups is strongly recommended and changes the experience completely. The Hierve el Agua mineral springs, about 70 kilometers east, have created extraordinary petrified waterfall formations cascading down a mountainside above an incredible valley panorama, with natural infinity pools at the top that you can actually swim in. The Sierra Norte mountain range north of the city offers serious hiking, mountain biking, and community-based ecotourism through the indigenous Zapotec villages of the Pueblos Mancomunados, where cloud forest trails connect communities at altitudes above 3,000 meters. For birders, the valley and sierra ecosystems around Oaxaca support an astonishing range of endemic and migratory species, and the city has become a quietly serious destination on the international birding circuit. Oaxaca gives you a world-class colonial city, a living indigenous culture, extraordinary food, and one of the most varied natural landscapes in Mexico all within an hour's drive in any direction.
snippet: We did Monte Albán at sunrise, Hierve el Agua in the afternoon, and ate mole negro in the Zócalo that evening. It was the best single day of travel I have ever had and I have been traveling seriously for over twenty years.

text: For anyone drawn to indigenous culture and living craft traditions, Oaxaca is one of the most important destinations in the entire Western Hemisphere. The state is home to 16 recognized indigenous groups, and their artistic and cultural output shapes everything from the textiles sold in the markets to the languages you hear spoken on the street. The Zapotec and Mixtec civilizations that predated the Spanish are not ancient history here — they are living culture, and the village craft cooperatives surrounding the city make that tangible. Teotitlán del Valle, 30 minutes east, produces some of the finest hand-woven wool rugs and tapetes in the Americas using natural dyes made from cochineal insects, indigo, and marigolds in a tradition stretching back over 2,000 years. San Bartolo Coyotepec is the home of the distinctive black clay pottery (barro negro) that has become synonymous with Oaxacan craftsmanship, and watching a master potter shape a vessel entirely by hand on a traditional wheel is deeply meditative. The Museo de las Culturas de Oaxaca inside the Santo Domingo complex houses the extraordinary Tomb 7 treasures from Monte Albán — gold jewelry, carved bone, and turquoise mosaics of staggering sophistication — and is one of the finest pre-Columbian collections in Mexico.
snippet: We spent three days just doing village day trips from Oaxaca and came back each evening having watched weavers, potters, and mezcal makers work in ways that are genuinely unchanged from centuries ago. It is the most culturally dense region I have ever visited.

(The extra M$22 or so are worth it: the first class seat fewer people, thus less crowded, more space, more comfortable.) Buses leave for Puebla approximately every half hour from both locations. Travel time from TAPO to Puebla's CAPU is usually around 2 hours, but this time may vary by up to a full hour depending on Mexico City traffic conditions at that time of day.

text: It is the capital of the State of Puebla. It was founded on April 16, 1531 as "La Puebla de los Ángeles". It was the first city in central Mexico founded by the Spanish conquistadors that was not built upon the ruins of a conquered Amerindian settlement.
snippet: It is the capital of the State of Puebla.

text: The family-run, small and intimate, Livit Immersion Center, offers Spanish language and cultural immersion programs and classes for all levels. Learn Spanish while exploring Puebla's fantastic colonial arquitecture, rich history and exquisite traditional cuisine. Spanish Institute of Puebla, 11 oriente No.
snippet: The family-run, small and intimate, Livit Immersion Center, offers Spanish language and cultural immersion programs and classes for all levels.

Richmond is on I-95 less than 2 hours south of Washington DC.

text: For the rest of its history it has simply been a constituency within the government of Barbados, though with the parliament, political base and big money concentrated here, many islanders might suspect that Bridgetown governs Barbados not the other way round. Barbados Airport ( BGI IATA ) is usually shown on departure boards as Bridgetown but it's 8 miles west in Southern Barbados. Most tourists are bussed straight to their beach hotels and don't arrive via the city.
snippet: For the rest of its history it has simply been a constituency within the government of Barbados, though with the parliament, political base and big money…

text: For live music lovers, keep an eye out for performances by Barbados Tuk Bands or calypso shows. The sound of steel pans is everywhere – it’s the soundtrack of the Caribbean. Here are a few tips for your night out in Bridgetown: - Most places don’t get busy until after 10 PM. - Some clubs have dress codes, so maybe leave the flip-flops at the hotel. - Always have a plan for getting back to your hotel safely.
snippet: For live music lovers, keep an eye out for performances by Barbados Tuk Bands or calypso shows.

text: Climate Bridgetown features a tropical savanna climate (Köppen Aw), with relatively constant temperatures throughout the course of the year. While fairly hot, Bridgetown is cooled somewhat by the trade winds that affect weather in Barbados in general. Bridgetown's record high of 33.1 °C (91.6 °F) in September 2005 and record low of 16.5 °C (61.7 °F) on 2 January 1984.
snippet: Climate Bridgetown features a tropical savanna climate (Köppen Aw), with relatively constant temperatures throughout the course of the year.

text: This is the longest established school in Oaxaca, with a very lovely campus, most appropriate to university students who are in Mexico for course credit. A great place for independent travellers to study, as well, as it provides an easy way to meet other travellers and local Oaxacans. Its program includes academic courses to teach Spanish grammar, workshops ranging from cooking classes to weaving to teach about Oaxacan culture, and an "intercambio" session, which links a Oaxacan student studying English with an Instituto student so they both may practice their languages.
snippet: This is the longest established school in Oaxaca, with a very lovely campus, most appropriate to university students who are in Mexico for course credit.

text: Oaxaca City, Mexico is though Oaxaca is warm all year round, the hottest months are March to May, before the start of the rainy season. Temperatures have reportedly been getting hotter and starting earlier over the years - for much of March 2024, daily highs were hanging around 34°C. Despite this, there is very little air conditioning, and it's basically unheard of in low- to mid-range restaurants and budget accommodations, which tend to be uncomfortably hot.
snippet: Oaxaca City, Mexico is though Oaxaca is warm all year round, the hottest months are March to May, before the start of the rainy season.

text: Traveled all the way from Lisbon to San Sebastián and it was absolutely worth it — breathtaking views and probably the best food I've ever had. The pintxos bars in the Old Town are incredible, and just wandering through la parte vieja is a treat on its own. La Concha beach at sunset is one of those images that just stays with you.
snippet: Breathtaking views and probably the best food I've ever had. The pintxos bars in the Old Town are incredible, and just wandering through la parte vieja is a treat on its own.

text: As a surfer, I came to San Sebastián in October specifically for the waves at Zurriola beach, and the city delivered on every front. The Gros neighborhood on that side of the river is younger, less polished, and far cheaper than the Old Town — exactly my kind of scene. I ended up staying a week longer than planned.
snippet: Zurriola beach draws serious surfers, while the Gros neighborhood serves up the same great food without the tourist markup.

text: Visiting in January meant I had Mount Igueldo almost entirely to myself, and the funicular ride up gave panoramic winter views over the bay that I'll never forget. Off-season San Sebastián has a quiet dignity to it — chefs are more relaxed, tables are easier to get, and the Michelin-starred restaurants actually have availability. The cold Atlantic air just makes the txakoli wine taste better.
snippet: January is the secret season for San Sebastián — fewer crowds, easier reservations, and spectacular stormy-sea views from Mount Igueldo.

text: My partner and I did a self-guided Basque cider house tour in the hills above the city, tasting straight from the barrels alongside salt cod omelettes and aged txistorra sausage — a completely different food experience from the pintxos scene in town. The agricultural landscape just outside San Sebastián surprised us both; rolling green hills and traditional farmhouses that feel a world away from the coast. If you only do one meal, make it a sagardotegi.
snippet: A traditional cider house outside the city offers a deeper, more rustic Basque food experience that rivals anything you'll find downtown.

text: San Sebastián's architecture alone warrants a visit — the Belle Époque buildings along the Paseo de la Concha, the covered market of La Bretxa, and the ornate city hall overlooking the bay form a remarkably elegant cityscape. I spent two mornings sketching and photographing the cast-iron lampposts and tiled facades without setting foot in a single bar. For a city so famous for food, it's surprisingly easy to fill days just absorbing the urban beauty.
snippet: Beyond the food scene, San Sebastián is an architectural gem — Belle Époque facades, grand promenades, and a covered market worth lingering in.

text: My wife and I spent a few days in Sedona and it was hands down the most beautiful place I've ever been. The red rock formations are otherworldly — Cathedral Rock, Bell Rock, and the hike up to Soldier Pass caves are must-dos. Keep in mind Sedona is quite touristy, but the scenery is absolutely breathtaking regardless.
snippet: Hands down the most beautiful place I've ever been. The red rock formations are otherworldly and the hiking doesn't get any better than this.

text: I went to Sedona alone in early March, which turned out to be perfect timing — the crowds were manageable and the desert wildflowers were just starting to bloom on the trail to Devil's Bridge. Watching the sunrise paint the red cliffs in shades of amber and violet from a quiet overlook is an experience that rewards early risers. The spiritual energy people talk about isn't nonsense; there's genuinely something calming about spending a day among those formations.
snippet: Early March in Sedona means blooming desert wildflowers and manageable crowds — ideal conditions for solo hiking with spectacular sunrise views.

text: Sedona surprised me as a foodie destination — the restaurant scene is far more sophisticated than I expected for a small Arizona town, with farm-to-table menus and locally foraged ingredients turning up everywhere. Tlaquepaque Arts Village is worth half a day for its galleries, studios, and a serious green chile margarita. The combination of world-class art and genuinely creative cooking makes Sedona much more than a hiking stopover.
snippet: Tlaquepaque Arts Village and a thriving farm-to-table restaurant scene make Sedona a legitimate foodie and arts destination, not just a hiking hub.

text: We visited Sedona in November with our teenage kids and found the vortex sites surprisingly accessible for non-hikers — Airport Mesa is a short walk and the views are extraordinary. The West Fork Trail along Oak Creek Canyon was pure autumn magic, with cottonwoods blazing yellow against the red canyon walls. Families will find the town itself very walkable and safe, with plenty of ice cream and souvenir shops to keep everyone happy between hikes.
snippet: Oak Creek Canyon in autumn is stunning — golden cottonwoods against red canyon walls, with trails that suit families of every fitness level.

text: Pink Jeep Tours was a game-changer for understanding Sedona's geology — our guide explained the different sandstone formations, the ancient sea beds, and the tectonic forces that shaped everything over millions of years. Going off-road into areas inaccessible on foot gave us perspectives you simply cannot get on a hiking trail. Even after years of visiting desert landscapes, Sedona's geological storytelling left me with a completely new appreciation for what I was looking at.
snippet: A guided jeep tour unlocks the geology behind Sedona's formations — an off-road perspective that transforms a beautiful landscape into a fascinating story.

text: I just finished a great 8-day solo trip to Benin — it was the perfect mix of a cultural trip and beach vacation. In Cotonou, motorcycle taxis known as zems are the primary way to get around; just look for someone in a yellow shirt. The day trip to Ganvié, a village built entirely on a lake, was the highlight of the whole trip.
snippet: A perfect mix of cultural trip and beach vacation. The day trip to Ganvié, a village built on a lake near Cotonou, was the highlight of the whole trip.

text: Cotonou's nightlife surprised me completely — the Cadjehoun neighborhood has open-air bars and live Afrobeats venues that get going well past midnight, drawing a young local crowd with zero interest in tourists. Street food from grilled fish stalls along the beach road fed me better than any restaurant for a fraction of the cost. West Africa's most underrated party city might just be Cotonou.
snippet: Cotonou's Cadjehoun neighborhood comes alive after dark with live music and street food that puts most West African capitals to shame.

text: As a textile and fashion enthusiast, the Grand Marché de Dantokpa was the undisputed highlight of my Benin trip — it's one of the largest open-air markets in West Africa, and the wax-print fabrics and handmade beadwork are extraordinary. A local tailor near the market made me a custom outfit in under 24 hours for less than ten euros. Bring cash, bring patience, and bring a big bag.
snippet: Dantokpa market is a textile lover's paradise — one of West Africa's largest open-air markets, where custom clothing can be made overnight for almost nothing.

text: Ouidah, just an hour from Cotonou, is one of the most historically significant and emotionally powerful places I've visited anywhere in the world — the Route des Esclaves and the Door of No Return on the beach are deeply moving memorials to the transatlantic slave trade. The Python Temple in town is genuinely unlike anything else, with sacred pythons moving freely across your shoulders if you're willing. No history buff should skip this day trip.
snippet: The day trip to Ouidah — with its slave trade memorials and sacred Python Temple — is one of the most historically profound experiences West Africa offers.

text: Cotonou in February during the Fête du Vodoun was an extraordinary cultural immersion — elaborately masked Zangbeto guardians parading through the streets, drumming that shook the whole neighborhood, and ceremonies that have continued unbroken for centuries. My Beninese host arranged for me to watch from a respectful distance, and even from there the energy was overwhelming. Timing your visit around a traditional ceremony transforms Cotonou from interesting to unforgettable.
snippet: Visiting during the Vodoun festival turns Cotonou into one of the world's great cultural spectacles — ancient masked ceremonies and drumming that you'll feel in your chest.

text: Spent 3 days in Sarajevo at the tail end of our Croatia trip and it is an incredible city — should be high on the list for people who love history and good food. I met really lovely people while I was there, and the food was incredible. The War Childhood Museum and the Old Town are genuinely moving experiences you won't find anywhere else in Europe.
snippet: An incredible city that should be high on the list for people who love history and good food. I met really lovely people and the Old Town is genuinely unlike anything else in Europe.

text: Sarajevo in winter, when snow settles on the Ottoman-era rooftops and the call to prayer echoes off the hills, is something out of a dream — the city feels ancient and alive at the same time. I took the gondola up Trebević Mountain for views over the snow-dusted city and found the ruins of the 1984 Olympic bobsled track right there in the pine forest, ghostly and beautiful. No other European capital I know carries so much layered history in such a compact space.
snippet: Winter in Sarajevo brings snow-covered Ottoman rooftops, mountain gondola rides, and the haunting ruins of the 1984 Olympic bobsled track — Europe at its most layered.

text: As a food traveler, Sarajevo absolutely delivered — cevapi at Zeljo in Baščaršija, burek fresh from a wood-fired pita shop at dawn, and the most extraordinary honey baklava I've eaten outside Istanbul. The coffee culture here is serious and unhurried; Bosnian coffee arrives with lokum and a glass of water, and nobody rushes you out. Budget well under €20 a day for eating and you'll still eat like royalty.
snippet: Cevapi, burek at dawn, and Bosnian coffee served with lokum — Sarajevo's food scene is one of Europe's most authentic and underpriced.

text: Cycling the river valley just outside Sarajevo's center, through neighborhoods largely untouched by the tourist trail, showed me a more ordinary and surprisingly beautiful side of the city — vegetable gardens, elderly men playing cards outside cafes, kids on bikes. The Yellow Fortress above the old town gave a panoramic sunset view that rivaled anything in Dubrovnik for a fraction of the crowds. Sarajevo rewards travelers who wander beyond the obvious sights.
snippet: Wander beyond the tourist center and Sarajevo reveals quiet riverside neighborhoods and rooftop fortress views that rival anything on the Adriatic coast.

text: I came to Sarajevo specifically to understand the siege of 1992–1995, and the city handles this heavy history with remarkable honesty and grace — the Tunnel of Hope Museum on the city's outskirts is essential, showing how residents survived nearly four years of encirclement. Walking the Sniper Alley boulevard with a local guide who lived through the siege turned abstract history into something deeply personal. It's a challenging visit emotionally, but one of the most important trips I've ever made.
snippet: The Tunnel of Hope Museum and Sniper Alley, best explored with a local guide who survived the siege, offer a history lesson found nowhere else in Europe.

text: Four days in Tirana and what an interesting country filled with history and nature! The food — especially stuffed eggplant — is delicious, and it really is a hidden gem with not that many tourists yet. Bunk'Art museum in the old Cold War bunker is genuinely fascinating, and the cable car up Mount Dajti gives stunning views over the whole city.
snippet: A hidden gem with not many tourists yet — the food is delicious and Bunk'Art museum in the old Cold War bunker is genuinely fascinating.

text: Tirana's street art scene caught me completely off guard — the city has invested heavily in transforming its communist-era grey blocks into enormous, vivid murals, and just wandering the Blloku district feels like walking through a curated outdoor gallery. This neighborhood was literally off-limits to ordinary Albanians until 1991, reserved exclusively for the communist elite, and that layered history makes every coffee shop and boutique feel like a small act of reclamation. Albania's capital is telling its own story and it's a compelling one.
snippet: Tirana's Blloku district — once reserved for communist elites — is now the city's most vibrant neighborhood, covered in murals and full of excellent cafes.

text: Hiking up to the Martyrs' Cemetery on the hill above Tirana at dusk, with the whole pastel-painted city spread out below and the Albanian Alps catching the last light on the horizon, was one of those unplanned travel moments that turns a good trip into a great one. The cemetery itself is sobering and beautifully maintained, offering context for the country's 20th-century story. A combined walk-and-history afternoon costs nothing and stays with you for a long time.
snippet: The hike to the Martyrs' Cemetery above Tirana delivers panoramic city and mountain views alongside quiet, meaningful insight into Albanian history.

text: Lake Ohrid day trips get all the attention, but the village of Krujë just 45 minutes from Tirana is a far more accessible and equally rewarding excursion — the castle of Gjergj Kastrioti Skanderbeg sits dramatically above the town, and the bazaar below it sells handmade copper work and embroidery directly from artisan workshops. Albania's national hero is inescapable here, and the pride locals take in that history is genuinely infectious. Half a day is enough, but take the full day if you can.
snippet: Krujë, 45 minutes from Tirana, combines Skanderbeg's hilltop castle with a living artisan bazaar — a compact, deeply Albanian half-day trip.

text: Tirana's restaurant scene is evolving fast, and the raki-and-meze culture at traditional tavernas — called mejhane — is one of the great underrated pleasures of Balkan travel. I spent three evenings trying different Albanian wines, including Kallmet and Shesh i Zi varieties that almost nobody outside the country has heard of, paired with roasted lamb and fermented white cheese. The bill each night was laughably low for the quality; Tirana is the best-value food city I've found in Europe.
snippet: Albanian wines like Kallmet, generous meze spreads, and roasted lamb at a traditional mejhane make Tirana one of Europe's most rewarding and affordable food cities.

text: Rio is one of the most visually striking places I've ever visited — a city beautifully wedged between beaches, mountains, and jungle. The biggest downside is safety; even if nothing bad happens, the constant awareness can take the edge off the experience. That said, it is absolutely a must-see for seasoned travelers, and the energy here is unlike any other city in the world.
snippet: One of the most visually striking places I've ever visited — a city beautifully wedged between beaches, mountains, and jungle. A must-see for seasoned travelers.

text: I came to Rio during Carnival as a solo traveler and the experience was overwhelming in the best possible way — blocos (street parties) throughout Santa Teresa and Lapa that run for days, samba schools with 4,000 members pouring past in feathers and sequins, and a city that simply forgets everything else exists for a week. Booking accommodation six months in advance is essential, but the energy makes every logistical headache worth it. There is no party anywhere on earth quite like this.
snippet: Rio during Carnival — with its neighborhood blocos and samba school parades — is the most exhilarating street festival on the planet, and worth every planning headache.

text: Hiking the Tijuca National Forest, the world's largest urban tropical forest, right inside the city limits of Rio was something I didn't expect to be doing on a city trip — but the trails to Pedra Bonita and the hidden waterfall at Cascatinha Taunay are spectacular and largely crowd-free. The forest sits just behind Christo Redentor and you can actually see the statue through the tree canopy on the way up. Rio's nature runs far deeper than its famous beaches.
snippet: Tijuca National Forest — the world's largest urban jungle — offers waterfall hikes and forest trails minutes from the city center, completely different from the beach scene.

text: Santa Teresa, the hillside bohemian neighborhood above Lapa, is where I found the Rio I actually wanted — crumbling belle époque mansions turned into artist studios, open-air samba bars on Friday afternoons, and a population of musicians, painters, and writers who have no interest in the tourist circuit below. The yellow bonde tram that rattles up from downtown makes it feel almost cinematic. Spend at least one full day up here and you'll see a completely different city.
snippet: Santa Teresa's hillside of artist studios, vintage mansions, and neighborhood samba bars offers a Rio experience that has nothing to do with Copacabana or Ipanema.

text: Going out on a sailboat for a sunset cruise around Guanabara Bay, with the Sugarloaf rising dramatically out of the water and the city lights beginning to flicker on, was the moment Rio locked itself into my memory permanently. Boat tours are easy to arrange from Gloria marina and cost far less than you'd expect. Seeing the city from the water gives you a completely different sense of its scale — this place is genuinely immense.
snippet: A sunset sailboat cruise around Guanabara Bay, with Sugarloaf reflected in the water and the city lighting up behind it, is Rio at its most spectacular.

text: Curitiba is almost perfect when it comes to urban mobility — I used the municipal buses, the tourist tram line, and Uber, and everything ran with impressive punctuality. The articulated buses and their distinctive tube stations are a wonderful experience in themselves. The scenic train ride down to Morretes through stunning mountain landscapes is a genuine highlight you shouldn't miss.
snippet: Curitiba is almost perfect when it comes to urban mobility, and the scenic train ride down to Morretes through the mountains is a highlight you shouldn't miss.

text: Curitiba's parks caught me completely off guard as a landscape architecture enthusiast — the Jardim Botânico with its iconic Art Nouveau greenhouse, Tanguá Park with its waterfall and tunnels, and Barigui Park where capybaras simply roam free among the joggers are all exceptional public spaces. The city treats green space as infrastructure, and the result is a South American capital that actually feels livable. I'd come back just to spend more time in these parks.
snippet: Curitiba's parks — including a botanical garden with a stunning Art Nouveau greenhouse and a park where capybaras roam free — make it one of South America's greenest and most livable cities.

text: As a food traveler I came to Curitiba for the German and Ukrainian immigrant food heritage, and the barreado — a slow-cooked beef stew served with manioc flour and banana — at a traditional restaurant in the Santa Felicidade district was unlike anything else in Brazil. The neighborhood itself is largely Italian-descended and the trattorias spill out onto the streets on weekend evenings with an energy that reminded me of São Paulo's immigrant neighborhoods. Curitiba's culinary diversity is one of its most underrated assets.
snippet: Curitiba's Santa Felicidade district, shaped by Italian immigrants, and the traditional barreado stew make this city a genuine culinary detour for any foodie in the south of Brazil.

text: Curitiba makes a surprisingly excellent base for exploring the coast — the road down to Paranaguá via Estrada da Graciosa is one of the most scenic drives in Brazil, switching back through Atlantic rainforest with sea views at every turn. From Paranaguá it's a short boat ride to Ilha do Mel, a car-free island with beautiful beaches that sees almost no international tourists. The whole itinerary is easy in a rental car and feels completely off the beaten path.
snippet: The drive down to Paranaguá via Estrada da Graciosa, and a boat to the car-free Ilha do Mel, make Curitiba an excellent coastal base that few international travelers discover.

text: The Wire Opera House — Ópera de Arame — is one of the most architecturally striking buildings I've encountered anywhere in Latin America: a transparent structure of steel tubes rising from a quarry lake, surrounded by forest. Curitiba has an unusual number of architectural landmarks for a city its size, including Oscar Niemeyer's Museu Oscar Niemeyer with its giant eye-shaped gallery. For anyone interested in design and urban planning, this city is a genuine pilgrimage.
snippet: Curitiba is an unexpected pilgrimage for design enthusiasts — from the transparent Wire Opera House built in a quarry to Oscar Niemeyer's eye-shaped museum downtown.

text: Phnom Penh gets a lot of mediocre reviews but it really deserves more credit. I visited the Royal Palace and the Tuol Sleng Genocide Museum — everything is close and a tuk-tuk via Grab is cheap and easy. The Tuol Sleng museum is an incredibly powerful experience that gives you real context for understanding Cambodia.
snippet: Phnom Penh deserves more credit than it gets. The Tuol Sleng Genocide Museum is an incredibly powerful experience that gives you real context for understanding Cambodia.

text: I spent three nights in Fès and found it absolutely fascinating, though the Fès medina is so complex that a professional guide is genuinely essential. The hassle and aggressive selling in the souks is real, but the architecture, the tanneries, and the sheer sensory overload of the old city are unlike anything else I've experienced. Overall it left a very positive impression on me.
snippet: I found Fès absolutely fascinating — the medina is complex enough to need a guide, but the tanneries and sensory overload of the old city are unlike anything else.

text: I visited Fès specifically to study traditional Moroccan zellige tilework and woodcarving, and the artisan quarter around the Nejjarine Museum delivered beyond any expectation — watching master craftsmen carve intricate geometric patterns into cedarwood using techniques unchanged for six centuries was genuinely moving. The wooden-ceilinged fondouks (historic merchant inns) scattered through the medina are some of the most beautiful architectural spaces in Africa. Come with time and curiosity, not just a checklist.
snippet: The artisan quarter of Fès, where master craftsmen still practice six-century-old woodcarving and zellige techniques, is one of Africa's great living craft traditions.

text: Staying inside the medina walls at a traditional riad rather than in the new city transformed my experience of Fès entirely — waking to the predawn call to prayer echoing through narrow stone lanes, eating msemen flatbreads with honey on the rooftop terrace, and watching the neighborhood's morning rhythms unfold from above felt like genuine immersion rather than tourism. The Bou Inania madrasa, just minutes on foot, has Quranic calligraphy and carved stucco that I spent nearly two hours photographing. Base yourself inside the medina and the city opens up completely.
snippet: Staying in a riad inside the medina walls — rising to the call to prayer and watching the city wake from your rooftop — is the only way to truly experience Fès.

text: I found Toronto to be a great city with plenty to do, great food, and friendly people. I spent 6 days there and could have stayed longer — took the ferry out to the islands, which was lovely, and Casa Loma was a surprisingly interesting visit. Kensington Market is diverse and vibrant with lots of smaller merchants and great street food.
snippet: A great city with plenty to do, great food, and friendly people — I spent 6 days there and could have stayed longer. Kensington Market alone is worth the trip.

text: We absolutely loved our trip to Lucca — it's admittedly smaller than Pisa or Bologna but there's enough to see and the food is excellent. The intact Renaissance walls you can walk or cycle on top of are genuinely unique, and just being in the Piazza dell'Anfiteatro felt magical. Visiting in spring or fall is highly recommended to avoid the summer crowds.
snippet: We absolutely loved Lucca — the intact walls you can cycle on top of are genuinely unique, and the food is outstanding. Visit in spring or fall to avoid the summer crowds.

text: Edmonton is a cool city — a bit on the gritty side but some great people live here. The river valley park system is surprisingly massive and beautiful, and the food scene has genuinely improved in recent years. If you're visiting, come in summer when the city really comes alive with festivals and the long golden evenings.
snippet: Edmonton is a cool city with great people. Come in summer when it really comes alive — the river valley parks are surprisingly beautiful and the festivals are fantastic.

text: Banff felt very safe and family-friendly, with the town essentially existing to help visitors explore the spectacular national park around it. We stayed in Canmore just outside the park — much cheaper and less crowded — and easily drove in for the hikes. Lake Louise, Moraine Lake, and the Icefields Parkway are genuinely among the most beautiful landscapes on earth.
snippet: Banff is set in one of the most spectacular national parks on earth. Stay in nearby Canmore to save money and avoid crowds, and drive in daily for the hikes.

text: I visited Banff in January for a ski trip and the mountains delivered conditions I've never experienced anywhere in Europe — light, dry powder and vast vertical drops at Sunshine Village and Lake Louise ski areas. The town itself is charming under snow, and watching elk wander down Banff Avenue in the blue winter twilight is surreal. Cold-weather Banff, with its hot springs and Northern Lights potential, is genuinely a different destination from the summer crowds.
snippet: Winter Banff — powder skiing at Sunshine Village, elk on the main street, and the chance of Northern Lights — is a completely different and equally spectacular experience from the summer crowds.

text: As a wildlife photographer, the Bow Valley Parkway between Banff and Lake Louise in early spring was the most productive wildlife corridor I've worked anywhere in North America — black bears foraging on the roadside slopes, bighorn sheep at Johnston Canyon, and a wolf sighting that I'm still not sure I fully believe happened. Early morning is everything here; by 9 AM the buses have arrived and the magic dissipates. Set an alarm and go.
snippet: The Bow Valley Parkway in early spring, before the tour buses arrive, is one of North America's premier wildlife corridors — bears, wolves, and bighorn sheep all on the same morning.

text: Johnston Canyon in autumn, when the larches on the surrounding slopes turn electric gold and the canyon walls are streaked with frozen waterfalls beginning to form, was one of the most beautiful two-hour hikes I've done anywhere. The upper falls are far less visited than the lower ones — worth the extra kilometer significantly. October in Banff is genuinely underrated and the lodges lower their prices just as the scenery hits its peak.
snippet: October in Banff brings golden larch season and frozen canyon walls — some of the most spectacular autumn scenery in North America at a fraction of the summer-crowd experience.

text: Renting a canoe on Emerald Lake in Yoho National Park, just across the provincial border from Banff, was the most peaceful two hours of my entire Canadian Rockies trip — the color of the water is almost artificially turquoise and the surrounding peaks reflect perfectly on calm mornings. Most visitors skip Yoho for Jasper, but the combination of Emerald Lake, Natural Bridge, and Takakkaw Falls makes it an essential detour. The crowds at Moraine Lake are real; the crowds at Emerald Lake are not.
snippet: Yoho National Park's Emerald Lake, just over the border from Banff, offers equally stunning turquoise water and towering peaks with a fraction of the crowd.

text: I stayed in Maputo and can tell you that Mozambique is great — the city has a laid-back Portuguese colonial atmosphere with excellent seafood restaurants along the waterfront. The Mercado Central is an overwhelming mix of sights, sounds, and smells unlike anything I'd experienced before. As a gateway to the beaches further north, Maputo is a solid and welcoming starting point.
snippet: Maputo has a laid-back Portuguese colonial atmosphere with excellent seafood along the waterfront. As a gateway to the beautiful beaches further north, it's a solid and welcoming starting point.

text: Maputo's live music scene is one of the most vibrant and underappreciated in Southern Africa — the Clube Naval hosts weekend marrabenta and afro-jazz nights that go until dawn, and the crowd is almost entirely local. I spent three evenings bar-hopping in the Polana neighborhood with a group of Mozambican teachers I met at my guesthouse, and those conversations turned a solo trip into something far richer. Don't underestimate how much a city's music scene can tell you about its soul.
snippet: Maputo's marrabenta and afro-jazz venues, packed with locals dancing until dawn, make it one of Southern Africa's most underrated music cities.

text: The neighborhood of Baixa, Maputo's lower city, contains one of the finest collections of colonial-era ironwork architecture in Africa — buildings supposedly designed by Gustave Eiffel's studio, with ornate cast-iron verandas and facades in varying states of beautiful decay. A slow morning walk with a camera through these blocks is endlessly rewarding for anyone who loves architecture. The Central Train Station alone, with its teal-and-cream painted ironwork, is worth the trip to Mozambique.
snippet: Maputo's Baixa district holds one of Africa's finest collections of colonial ironwork architecture — including the stunning teal-painted Central Train Station reportedly linked to Gustave Eiffel.

text: I hired a local fisherman for an early morning boat trip into Maputo Bay and the experience was extraordinary — dolphins in the harbor mouth, enormous dhow fishing vessels under full sail, and the city's skyline rising behind mangrove shoreline in the golden morning light. The seafood he brought back that evening, cooked simply with piri-piri over charcoal at a backyard restaurant, was among the best I've eaten anywhere in my life. Maputo from the water is a different city entirely.
snippet: A dawn boat trip into Maputo Bay — dolphins, traditional dhow fishing vessels, and piri-piri seafood cooked fresh that evening — is the city's best-kept secret.

text: Maputo's art scene genuinely surprised me — the Núcleo de Arte collective has been running since the 1930s and the artists working there in tin and recycled materials produce work that sells in galleries from London to New York. The Natural History Museum with its famous collection of elephant fetuses preserved by the last Portuguese king is bizarre, fascinating, and completely unlike any other natural history museum I've visited. Maputo has more cultural depth than most travelers give it credit for.
snippet: The Núcleo de Arte collective and a Natural History Museum unlike any other in the world give Maputo a cultural weight that most travelers rushing north toward the beaches completely miss.

text: I've been to Dar es Salaam eight times as a UK tourist and it's super safe — worst that can happen is getting overpriced at the market. Everywhere you go, whoever you meet, you'll get a warm 'Karibu' (welcome) — it's one of the most genuinely welcoming places I know. Use Uber or Bolt to get around easily and you'll have a great time.
snippet: I've been eight times as a UK tourist and it's super safe. Everywhere you go you'll get a warm 'Karibu' — it's one of the most genuinely welcoming places I know.

text: As a food traveler, Dar es Salaam's street food around the Kariakoo market neighborhood was the highlight of my Tanzania trip — mishkaki (grilled skewered meat), fresh chapati, and samaki wa kupaka (coconut-grilled fish) from roadside stalls that cost almost nothing and taste extraordinary. The Indian Ocean spice influence on Swahili cooking here is completely distinct from inland Tanzanian food, and every meal taught me something. Come hungry and arrive with small notes.
snippet: Dar es Salaam's Kariakoo street food scene — mishkaki, coconut-grilled fish, and fresh chapati — showcases a Swahili-Indian Ocean cuisine unlike anything you'll find upcountry.

text: The ferry from Dar es Salaam to Zanzibar is one of the great short sea crossings in the world — two hours on a modern fast ferry with the Tanzanian coast disappearing behind you and Stone Town's ancient skyline rising ahead. Most travelers treat Dar as a transit point, but a night or two in the Msasani Peninsula neighborhood, with its beach bars and excellent seafood restaurants on the Coco Beach strip, makes the city a genuine destination in its own right. The ferry departs multiple times daily so there's no reason to rush.
snippet: The Dar-to-Zanzibar ferry is a highlight in itself, and the Msasani Peninsula's beach bar scene turns what most travelers treat as a stopover into a genuine destination.

text: I visited Dar in April during the long rains, when everyone told me not to bother, and found a city that felt deeply, authentically itself — no tourist competition for restaurant tables, dramatic afternoon thunderstorms over the harbor, and Tanzanians going about their daily lives with zero interest in performing for visitors. The National Museum's collections on the Leakey fossil discoveries at Olduvai Gorge were exceptional and I had the galleries almost entirely to myself. Low season Dar es Salaam is one of East Africa's best-value city breaks.
snippet: Dar es Salaam in the rainy season is authentically itself — exceptional museums, empty restaurants, and a harbor thunderstorm light show that no postcard captures.

text: Spending a Sunday morning at the Mwenge Crafts Village watching local sculptors work ebony and mpingo wood into figures and furniture was unexpectedly absorbing — these artisans have supplied the same workshops for decades and are happy to show their techniques if you're genuinely curious rather than just shopping. The drive north through Dar's leafy embassy quarter to the Bongoyo Island marine reserve — reachable by a short water taxi — revealed a completely different, slower side of the city. Dar rewards anyone willing to spend more than a single overnight.
snippet: The Mwenge Crafts Village and a water taxi to Bongoyo Island's coral reef show a slower, more rewarding side of Dar es Salaam that one-night transit travelers never discover.

text: Hamilton is great for shopping and restaurants, and the ferry connections to the Royal Naval Dockyard and St. George make it an ideal base for exploring the whole island. We rented an electric microcar and it was a blast driving around discovering hidden beaches and the scenic railway trail. Bermuda is a dream — the pink sand beaches alone are worth every penny.
snippet: Hamilton is great for shopping and dining, and with ferries to the Dockyard and St. George it's the perfect base. Bermuda is a dream — the pink sand beaches alone are worth every penny.

text: Santiago exceeded my low expectations — it's not really a destination in itself but more of a hub, and still worth a few days for the food and wine alone. The metro is efficient, Uber is cheap, and the neighbourhoods of Bellavista and Lastarria are genuinely great to explore on foot. Chile has been rated the safest country in Latin America and that definitely comes through.
snippet: Santiago exceeded my low expectations — worth a few days for the food and wine alone. The metro is efficient and the neighbourhoods of Bellavista and Lastarria are genuinely great to explore on foot.

text: Santiago's craft wine bar scene in Barrio Italia is where I spent most of my evenings — small producers from the Maule, Itata, and Bio-Bio valleys making natural wines from old-vine País and Cinsault grapes that almost nobody outside Chile has heard of. The neighborhood itself, full of vintage shops, artisan workshops, and plant-filled restaurants, is easily the most livable and interesting part of the city. Santiago has quietly become one of South America's best wine destinations.
snippet: Barrio Italia's natural wine bars, pouring obscure old-vine País and Cinsault from Chile's lesser-known southern valleys, make Santiago a genuine pilgrimage for wine enthusiasts.

text: Taking the Sunday funicular up Cerro San Cristóbal for panoramic views of the city — with the snow-capped Andes as a backdrop so close they seem theatrical — is one of those simple pleasures that reminds you why Santiago surprises people. The view is best in winter and early spring when the air is clear and the mountains are fully white. Below the hill in Barrio Bellavista, an outdoor arts and antiques market fills the streets and the empanadas at the corner bakeries are exceptional.
snippet: Winter views from Cerro San Cristóbal, with the snow-covered Andes as a backdrop and Bellavista's Sunday market below, show Santiago at its most visually stunning.

text: Santiago was my base for a week of day trips that would have been impossible from any other South American city — skiing in the Andes at Valle Nevado in the morning and back for dinner in the city the same evening; wine tasting in the Maipo Valley; a weekend in the port city of Valparaíso. The efficiency of Chilean infrastructure makes these excursions genuinely easy, and the contrasts between desert mountain, coastal city, and wine country all within a few hours' radius are remarkable.
snippet: No other South American capital puts Andean skiing, ocean-side Valparaíso, and vine-covered wine valleys within the same day-trip radius as Santiago — its greatest underappreciated asset.

text: La Vega Central market in the early morning, when wholesale sellers are arriving and the smells of fresh cilantro, merkén chili, and pebre sauce ingredients fill the air, is one of the most viscerally Chilean experiences in the city. I arrived at 7 AM and shared a breakfast of cazuela broth and pan de molde with market workers for about $2 total. Santiago's markets, rather than its museums or plazas, are where the city's actual character lives.
snippet: La Vega Central at dawn — wholesale sellers, steaming cazuela broth, and the full aroma of Chilean spices — is the city's most authentic and least touristed morning ritual.

text: I did a solo weekend in Shanghai a few weeks ago and loved it — felt super safe and was easy to get around. The metro is incredibly clean and efficient, almost like Tokyo's. Fantastic museums, gorgeous ancient water towns easily reached just outside the city, and day trips to nearby Suzhou and Hangzhou make it one of the best-value big city trips in Asia.
snippet: I loved solo Shanghai — felt super safe, easy to get around, and the metro is incredibly clean and efficient. The nearby water towns and day trips to Suzhou make it one of the best-value big city trips in Asia.

text: The Bund at night, with its illuminated colonial banking facades facing Pudong's science-fiction skyline across the Huangpu River, is one of the great urban panoramas of the 21st century — but the real secret is to walk the river south to the less-photographed Shiliupu area, where old working docks and tea warehouses are quietly becoming galleries and studios. Shanghai's relationship between its colonial past and its ambitions for the future is constantly visible on this waterfront. No other city in the world occupies quite this tension so spectacularly.
snippet: Walk south from the Bund to the converted Shiliupu dock warehouses and you find a Shanghai where colonial history and contemporary art collide without tourist crowds.

text: Shanghai's food scene in Jing'an district, particularly the morning dumplings situation, is worth a trip alone — soup dumplings (xiaolongbao), pan-fried sheng jian bao with their crispy bottoms, and scallion-oil noodles from 30-year family operations tucked into residential alleyways. I spent three mornings eating my way through different lilong neighborhoods before 9 AM and barely scratched the surface. The density of genuinely exceptional food in this city is simply without equal.
snippet: Three mornings eating soup dumplings and pan-fried bao in Jing'an's residential lilong alleys barely scratches the surface of what Shanghai does with breakfast alone.

text: Spending an afternoon cycling through the French Concession's plane-tree-lined streets in autumn, when the leaves turn gold and filter the light down through a complete canopy overhead, is one of those experiences that makes you understand why Shanghailanders are so devoted to this city. The old Art Deco apartment buildings and lane houses converted into boutique coffee shops and independent bookstores give the neighborhood a living, inhabited elegance you won't find in any other Chinese megacity. Rent a shared bike and just wander — no map needed.
snippet: Cycling the French Concession's autumn plane-tree canopy, with Art Deco apartment buildings and lane house coffee shops on every corner, is Shanghai at its most quietly beautiful.

text: A solo visit to the Zhujiajiao water town, an hour's bus ride from central Shanghai, gave me the ancient canal-city experience that tourists pay far more for in Suzhou — stone arch bridges, candlelit dinner boats, and Ming-dynasty temple courtyards — but with a genuinely local weekend crowd rather than tour groups. Arriving on a Tuesday morning meant I had some of the narrow stone lanes entirely to myself. Shanghai's surrounding water towns are one of the region's best open secrets.
snippet: Zhujiajiao water town, an hour from Shanghai, delivers ancient stone bridges and candlelit canals on a budget and without the crowds that descend on more famous Suzhou.

text: Kathmandu is chaotic and magical in equal measure — Boudhanath Stupa, Pashupatinath Temple, and the old Durbar Square are all extraordinary, and the Thamel neighborhood is great for gearing up for a trek. Two days in the city is about right before heading out to Pokhara or into the mountains. If you're only in Nepal for the month, spend your extra days exploring beyond the capital.
snippet: Kathmandu is chaotic and magical in equal measure — Boudhanath Stupa and Pashupatinath Temple are extraordinary. Two days in the city is about right before heading out to Pokhara or the Himalayas.

text: As someone who came to Kathmandu purely for the food, I was completely unprepared for how good the Newari cuisine is — beaten rice with curried black lentils, buffalo meat dried and fried with ginger, and the specific variety of chhoyela spiced grilled meat that you find only in the Valley. The Newa Lahana restaurant near Patan Durbar Square was a revelation and cheaper than any street food back home. Nepal's culinary heritage is one of the world's great undiscovered food traditions.
snippet: Newari cuisine in Kathmandu Valley — chhoyela, beaten rice with black lentil curry, and buffalo preparations found nowhere else — is one of the world's most underrated food traditions.

text: Patan, the medieval city just across the river from Kathmandu proper, has a Durbar Square so perfectly preserved and architecturally dense that it makes Kathmandu's own square look almost secondary. The bronze casting and copper repoussé workshops in Patan's back lanes still produce work by traditional methods for temples across Asia, and watching artisans at work is free and entirely welcoming. Most visitors skip Patan for Bhaktapur and miss a living cultural center that rewards two full days.
snippet: Patan's Durbar Square and its living tradition of bronze casting workshops make it arguably the most architecturally and artistically significant of the three medieval Kathmandu Valley cities.

text: I arrived in Kathmandu during the Tihar festival — Nepal's Festival of Lights — and the city was transformed: oil lamps in every window, intricate rice-flour rangoli patterns on every doorstep, and a day dedicated specifically to honoring crows, dogs, and cows before the human celebrations begin. Sharing sel roti (ring-shaped fried rice bread) with neighbors during the Bhai Tika ceremony was a spontaneous moment of hospitality that no tour operator could have arranged. Time your visit around a festival and Kathmandu gives you everything.
snippet: Arriving during Tihar — Nepal's Festival of Lights — transforms Kathmandu into a city of oil lamps, rangoli patterns, and festival food shared freely with strangers.

text: The mountain flight from Kathmandu airport at sunrise, 35 minutes looking out a window seat directly at the full Himalayan arc from Dhaulagiri to Kanchenjunga with Everest unmistakable in the center, is one of the most extraordinary hours available to any traveler anywhere on earth. It costs about $200 and requires no fitness, no acclimatization, and no trekking permit — just a willingness to wake up early. For anyone who cannot trek, this is the alternative that changes how you think about scale.
snippet: The 35-minute sunrise mountain flight from Kathmandu — eye-level with the full Himalayan arc including Everest — delivers the most dramatic perspective in aviation for $200 and zero fitness required.

text: I've solo traveled to New Zealand three times and you'll have a blast — Auckland is a great starting point with K Road, the ferry to Waiheke Island, and easy access to the stunning Coromandel Peninsula. The scenery and people are absolutely spectacular and I always have a list of things I didn't get to, so I keep coming back. It's much more tropical than you might expect in the north.
snippet: Auckland is a great starting point — the ferry to Waiheke, K Road, and easy access to the Coromandel Peninsula are all wonderful. New Zealand's scenery and people are absolutely spectacular.

text: Auckland's volcanic geography is something no other city in the world can match — 53 extinct volcanic cones within the city limits, each with a different character and view. Hiking up Rangitoto Island, a dormant shield volcano that erupted just 600 years ago, across a field of jagged black lava to a crater rim view over the Hauraki Gulf was one of the best half-days of my entire New Zealand trip. The island is uninhabited and the ferry from downtown takes 25 minutes.
snippet: Rangitoto Island, a dormant volcano 25 minutes by ferry from downtown Auckland, offers a lava field hike to a crater rim view that no other city on earth can replicate.

text: Auckland's food scene has transformed dramatically in the last decade, driven by its Pasifika and Asian communities — I ate my way through the Otara Saturday market, where Samoan chop suey, Tongan umu-roasted pork, and Fijian kokoda raw fish coconut salad are all served from the same row of stalls for under NZ$10 each. This is genuine Pacific Island food culture, not a tourist approximation of it. No restaurant in the city center matches the authenticity or the value.
snippet: The Saturday Otara Flea Market serves some of the world's most authentic Pacific Island food — Tongan umu pork, Samoan chop suey, and Fijian kokoda — all under NZ$10.

text: The Titirangi neighborhood in the Waitākere Ranges, a 30-minute drive from the city center, is Auckland's most atmospheric secret — an arts-and-crafts village perched at the edge of kauri rainforest, with walking tracks dropping through giant trees to the black-sand surf beaches of the west coast.
