snippet: Nairobi's café culture in Karen and Westlands is unexpectedly world-class, with Kenyan highland coffee sourced fresh — and the Karen Blixen Museum makes for a lovely, calm half-day away from the city bustle.

text: We used Nairobi as the bookend to a Masai Mara safari and it worked perfectly — flying into Wilson Airport and catching a small bush plane to the Mara is an adventure in itself. The Carnivore restaurant, which has been serving roasted meats for decades, is still worth it for the experience, and the Kazuri Bead workshop in Karen where women make hand-painted ceramic beads was one of the most meaningful stops of the trip.
snippet: Flying from Nairobi's Wilson Airport on a bush plane to the Masai Mara, then bookending the trip with the Carnivore restaurant and the Kazuri Bead cooperative in Karen, makes for a perfectly structured Kenya visit.

text: What I didn't expect about Nairobi was the thriving art scene — the Nairobi Gallery and a cluster of commercial galleries around Westlands showcase some genuinely world-class East African contemporary art, mostly at very affordable prices. The Maasai Market that moves between different locations on different days of the week is chaotic and wonderful, and I came home with far more textiles than I planned.
snippet: Nairobi's contemporary art galleries in Westlands and the rotating Maasai Market showcase East African creativity in ways that most visitors completely miss — a pleasant surprise in a city often defined only by its wildlife access.

text: As a solo traveler doing a budget trip, I found Nairobi far more manageable than I'd been led to expect. Staying in a well-reviewed hostel in the Kilimani area, using Bolt to get around, and eating nyama choma and ugali at local spots rather than tourist restaurants kept costs very low. The Railway Museum near the city centre tells the colonial history of the Uganda Railway in a surprisingly gripping way and costs almost nothing to enter.
snippet: Staying in Kilimani, eating nyama choma at local spots, and visiting the underrated Railway Museum on a tight budget — Nairobi is far more accessible and manageable for solo travelers than its reputation suggests.

text: Panama City was such an incredible place to visit — lots of history, amazing food, and so much to do. Watching the ships pass through the Panama Canal locks is mesmerizing, and the Casco Viejo neighborhood with its colonial architecture and rooftop bars is genuinely world-class. I would absolutely recommend Panama as a solo trip destination; English is widely spoken and it's one of the safest countries in Latin America.
snippet: Panama City is such an incredible destination — Casco Viejo's colonial architecture and rooftop bars are world-class, and watching ships pass through the Canal locks never gets old. One of the safest and most underrated capitals in Latin America.

text: I absolutely loved Fukuoka — the culinary scene is distinct from the rest of Japan, the Hakata-style ramen is fantastic, and the Mentaiko is a must-try, along with the vibrant yatai street-food stall culture along the river. What I found most delightful was its musical and energetic atmosphere; each night the main river through Tenjin was filled with people playing music and singing until around 2 AM. Locals appeared more approachable for conversation compared to Tokyo — I genuinely wished I had more than three nights.
snippet: Fukuoka's yatai street-food stalls along the river at night are one of Japan's most unique experiences. The Hakata ramen is the best I've had anywhere, and locals are noticeably more approachable than in Tokyo.

text: Maseru itself isn't much as a city, although it was great to be able to walk around and not feel at all unsafe — unfortunately in parts of southern Africa that's not something you can always take for granted. The real draw of Lesotho is the highland landscape: the Sani Pass road is one of the most dramatic drives in Africa, and the mountain villages accessible from there feel like a world away from anywhere. Even a short trip across the border from South Africa gives you a taste of the 'Kingdom in the Sky.'
snippet: Maseru is a quiet, walkable capital, but the real reason to come is the dramatic highland landscape beyond — the Sani Pass drive is among the most spectacular in Africa. Lesotho punches well above its weight for adventure travelers.

text: I just got back from Mexico City and gave it an 8/10 for my first solo trip — great food, loved the Condesa area where I stayed, and the museums, castle, and day trip to Teotihuacán were all incredible. Mexico City is an amazing place with a beautiful culture, good and cheap food, amazing art, and tons of things to do. I felt safe everywhere I went, even at night in Roma and Condesa, and there are tons of great places for food and nightlife.
snippet: Mexico City is an amazing place — incredible food, world-class museums, and the Condesa/Roma neighborhoods are safe and vibrant at night. Teotihuacán as a day trip absolutely blew my mind.

text: Coyoacán was my favourite neighbourhood and I could have stayed there the entire trip — the cobblestoned streets, the Frida Kahlo museum (book well in advance), the market stalls selling tamales and freshly-squeezed juice, and the central square where families gather on weekends all feel authentically Mexican in a way that the trendier areas sometimes don't. It's a 20-minute metro ride from the centre and a completely different pace of life.
snippet: Coyoacán's cobblestones, the Frida Kahlo museum, and tamale stalls in the market feel more authentically Mexican than anywhere else in the city — just a 20-minute metro ride and a completely different world.

text: Three days wasn't enough to do justice to Mexico City's museum scene, which is legitimately world-class. The Museo Nacional de Antropología alone could absorb a full day — the Aztec Sun Stone, the Mayan galleries, the scale of the whole thing is staggering. Walking through Bosque de Chapultepec afterward to decompress, then eating tacos al pastor from a late-night cart on the way back to the hotel, felt like the perfect Mexico City evening.
snippet: The Museo Nacional de Antropología is staggering in scale — the Aztec and Mayan galleries alone need a full day — and the walk through Bosque de Chapultepec afterward is the perfect decompression.

text: I took the metro everywhere in Mexico City and it was genuinely one of the best urban transit experiences I've had anywhere — fast, frequent, and incredibly cheap. The architecture in the Centro Histórico around the Zócalo is monumental and free to simply walk through, and the murals inside the National Palace by Diego Rivera are among the finest public artworks I've ever seen. The sheer scale and ambition of Mexico City keeps catching you off guard.
snippet: Diego Rivera's murals inside the National Palace are among the finest public artworks anywhere, and the Centro Histórico's monumental architecture is free to simply walk through — the scale of Mexico City keeps catching you off guard.

text: As a craft-beer enthusiast I was amazed by how well Mexico City has developed a local brewing scene over the past decade — there are excellent taprooms in Doctores and Cuauhtémoc that feel completely off the tourist trail. The Sunday morning antiques and vinyl market at El Chopo in Tepito attracts the city's alternative community and is unlike anything else I found in CDMX. You need at least a week to get past the surface.
snippet: Craft beer taprooms in Cuauhtémoc and the Sunday alternative market at El Chopo are the kind of authentic, off-trail experiences that reveal why Mexico City really needs at least a week to explore properly.

text: Doha and Qatar rank among the safest destinations for a solo traveler — I could leave my bags and laptop anywhere without worry, and taxis and hotels are reliably secure. Souq Waqif is the main highlight and genuinely interesting for a few hours of wandering, and the Museum of Islamic Art's architecture is extraordinary. It's a modern, polished city that works well as a stopover, though a couple of days is enough before the lack of street-level culture starts to feel limiting.
snippet: Doha is one of the safest cities in the world and Souq Waqif is a genuinely atmospheric place to spend an afternoon. The Museum of Islamic Art is architecturally stunning — worth visiting even if museums aren't usually your thing.

text: Astana has undergone several name changes but what hasn't changed is how genuinely fascinating it is — the architecture is futuristic and EPCOT-esque, and I found plenty of lively public spaces and parks despite expecting a ghost town. The people in Astana are highly educated, often speak multiple languages, and were genuinely curious about my visit — the city feels remarkably modern and safe. The Bayterek Tower, Grand Mosque, and Khan Shatyr entertainment complex are all worth your time.
snippet: Astana's futuristic architecture, immaculate parks, and genuinely welcoming locals make it one of Central Asia's most surprising cities. It's clean, safe, and remarkably modern — nothing like the Borat stereotype.

text: Luang Prabang is on the UNESCO World Heritage list for good reason — the architecture is lovely, the streets are very walkable, and there's a lot of little things to see and do without it feeling overwhelming. I spent three nights here and could frankly have stayed longer; it's my favourite town in Laos and one of the most stunning in all of Southeast Asia. Kuang Si Waterfall is my favourite waterfall anywhere — the lower tiers are swimmable and there's a cave you can duck behind the water screen.
snippet: Luang Prabang is one of the most stunning towns in Southeast Asia — walkable, UNESCO-listed, and utterly charming. Kuang Si Waterfall is the best waterfall I've ever swum in, and the monk alms ceremony at dawn is deeply moving.

text: Waking up before 5am to watch the tak bat — the monks' silent alms-giving procession through the mist — was one of the most quietly moving experiences of my Southeast Asia travels. The whole town seems to hold its breath in those early morning minutes. By 7am the bakeries and coffee shops on the main peninsula were open, serving French-influenced pastries and proper Lao coffee that set the tone for a genuinely unhurried day.
snippet: Watching the monks' silent alms procession through the pre-dawn mist is one of the most quietly moving experiences in all of Southeast Asia — and the French-influenced bakeries open at 7am are worth waking up for.

text: I spent four days kayaking and cycling around the villages near Luang Prabang and found the surrounding countryside even more beautiful than the town itself. The boat trip upstream to the Pak Ou Caves — thousands of Buddha images in two riverside grottoes — is a half-day well spent, and stopping at a Hmong village en route showed a way of life completely different from anything in the city. The pace of rural Laos is something genuinely special.
snippet: The boat trip to Pak Ou Caves with thousands of river-facing Buddha images, followed by cycling through Hmong villages in the surrounding countryside, reveals a Laos even more beautiful than Luang Prabang's famous streets.

text: As a solo female traveler I found Luang Prabang one of the safest and most relaxed places I've visited anywhere. The night market along the main street is manageable and low-pressure compared to markets in Thailand or Vietnam, and the Lao silk and textile work on sale is genuinely beautiful — I bought a handwoven silk scarf that remains one of my favourite possessions. The whole town feels like it operates at about half the speed of the rest of the world.
snippet: Luang Prabang's night market is low-pressure and genuinely beautiful — the handwoven Lao silk on sale is some of the finest textile work in Southeast Asia, and the whole town operates at blissfully half the speed of the rest of the world.

text: What makes Luang Prabang unusual is how the French colonial and Lao Buddhist traditions have fused into something completely their own. We ate at a small restaurant run by a Lao family that served a set menu of local dishes — laap, mok pa, sticky rice — for about $8 per person, and it was extraordinary. The Royal Palace Museum gives a fascinating insight into the last days of the Lao monarchy before 1975.
snippet: An $8 set menu of laap, mok pa, and sticky rice at a family-run restaurant, followed by the Royal Palace Museum's fascinating account of the last Lao monarchy — Luang Prabang's French-Buddhist fusion is unlike anywhere else in the region.

text: Oaxaca was one of the best places I've ever been — I could easily have spent a full week there and still felt rushed. The food scene is extraordinary with mole, tlayudas, and mezcal bars, and the surrounding Valley of Oaxaca with its Zapotec ruins at Monte Albán gives the region incredible historical depth. If you can time it for Día de los Muertos at the end of October, the celebrations are unlike anything else in Mexico.
snippet: Oaxaca was one of the best places I've ever been — the food, mezcal, and colonial architecture are extraordinary. Time it for Día de los Muertos and you'll witness celebrations unlike anything else in the country.

text: Kaunas and Vilnius were both stunning — the Lithuania Aviation Museum was a standout, and we had a fantastic hike in the area. Kaunas is worth a visit if you're interested in military, WWII, or Holocaust history, and walking the main street from Old Town to St. Michael's Church (the Soboras) is lovely. A half-day feels about right for the city itself, though the surrounding region rewards more exploration.
snippet: Kaunas is a quietly lovely Lithuanian city with a walkable old town and excellent WWII and Modernist architecture. Half a day is enough to cover the highlights, but the city has a relaxed charm that makes it easy to linger.

text: I came to Kaunas largely for the interwar modernist architecture and wasn't disappointed — the city was the Lithuanian capital between the wars and the resulting concentration of functionalist and art deco buildings is genuinely remarkable for a city this size. The architecture walking tour from the tourist office is free and extremely well put together. Laisvės Alėja, the long pedestrian boulevard, is one of the finest urban promenades in the Baltics.
snippet: Kaunas's interwar modernist architecture from its time as the Lithuanian capital is genuinely remarkable — the free walking tour is excellent, and Laisvės Alėja is one of the finest pedestrian boulevards in the entire Baltic region.

text: We visited Kaunas as a day trip from Vilnius and wished we'd allowed more time — the Ninth Fort memorial on the edge of the city is a deeply important and sobering site commemorating those killed during the Nazi occupation, and it deserves proper attention rather than a rushed hour. The Devil Museum's collection of thousands of devil figurines from Lithuanian folk tradition is strange, memorable, and completely unique.
snippet: The Ninth Fort memorial is a sobering and deeply important site that deserves proper time — and the completely unique Devil Museum, with thousands of folk-tradition figurines, is one of the strangest and most memorable museums in Lithuania.

text: The food and bar scene in Kaunas has quietly become one of the most interesting in the Baltics — there are several excellent craft beer bars and farm-to-table restaurants around the Old Town that draw a local rather than tourist crowd. I had a very good cold smoked duck with local rye bread at a tiny wine bar near the Town Hall Square that rivalled anything I'd eaten in Vilnius. The city has a relaxed, studenty energy that Vilnius sometimes lacks.
snippet: Kaunas's craft beer bars and farm-to-table restaurants near the Old Town draw a local crowd and have quietly become some of the most interesting dining in the Baltics — the city has a relaxed, studenty energy that Vilnius sometimes lacks.

text: Doing the bike path from Kaunas along the Nemunas River on a clear autumn afternoon was one of those unexpected travel highlights that doesn't appear in any guidebook. The confluence of the Nemunas and Neris rivers is genuinely beautiful, and the wooden hillside churches in the surrounding villages add a quiet spiritual quality to the countryside. Kaunas reveals itself through slow movement rather than checklist sightseeing.
snippet: Cycling the Nemunas River path on a clear autumn afternoon, past the river confluence and the wooden hillside churches in surrounding villages, is an unhurried highlight that no guidebook adequately captures.

text: Podgorica is honestly the most low-key European capital I've come across — there isn't a great deal to see or do in the city itself. But Montenegro as a whole is super underrated, easy to travel, safe, and full of natural beauty: Kotor Bay is breathtaking, Budva has a great vibe, and the Durmitor mountains are spectacular. Use Podgorica as a transit hub rather than a destination and you'll have a fantastic trip.
snippet: Podgorica doesn't have a lot to offer as a city on its own, but Montenegro overall is fantastic — Kotor Bay and the Durmitor highlands are some of the most beautiful landscapes in Europe.

text: Kuching turned out to be a fantastic city — it's clean, has an incredible food scene, and is relatively easy to navigate using Grab. I ended up staying the entire month instead of just two weeks. The orangutan sanctuary at Semenggoh, Bako National Park, and the lively waterfront with its music and fountains gave my days real purpose. In my opinion Kuching is significantly more charming than Kota Kinabalu — if you're doing Borneo, make sure it's on your list.
snippet: Kuching surprised me completely — clean, charming, with an incredible food scene and easy access to orangutans and Bako National Park. It's the most underrated city in Malaysia by a wide margin.

text: Kuching is one of the best eating cities in Southeast Asia and it's almost entirely overlooked. Sarawak laksa at a kopitiam at 7am is the finest breakfast I've had anywhere in the region, and the top floor of the weekend open-air market in Satok lets you eat your way through dishes you won't find outside Sarawak. The city's Chinese, Malay, Iban, and Bidayuh culinary traditions all coexist on the same streets.
snippet: Sarawak laksa at a kopitiam at 7am is arguably the finest breakfast in Southeast Asia — Kuching's layered Malay, Chinese, Iban, and Bidayuh food culture is almost entirely overlooked by travelers heading straight for Bali or Bangkok.

text: We spent three days doing the Bako National Park experience — taking the boat from the jetty, hiking the coastal trails, and sleeping in the park accommodation overnight to catch proboscis monkeys at dusk along the mangrove boardwalk. It's genuinely wild and the biodiversity felt more concentrated than anywhere I've hiked in Borneo. Kuching itself served as a comfortable and interesting base before and after.
snippet: Staying overnight in Bako National Park to catch proboscis monkeys along the mangrove boardwalk at dusk, then returning to Kuching's excellent food scene, is the perfect Borneo city-and-nature combination.

text: The Sarawak Museum is one of the finest natural history and ethnographic museums in Southeast Asia and costs almost nothing to enter — the longhouse reconstructions and indigenous Dayak artefact collection are extraordinary. The waterfront along the Sarawak River is very pleasant in the evening, and the Cat Museum — Kuching means 'cat' in Malay — is exactly as delightfully absurd as it sounds.
snippet: The Sarawak Museum's indigenous Dayak collection and longhouse reconstructions are among the finest ethnographic displays in Southeast Asia — and yes, the Cat Museum is as delightfully absurd as it sounds.

text: I visited Kuching during the Rainforest World Music Festival held at the Sarawak Cultural Village and it transformed an already excellent destination into something extraordinary. International musicians performing alongside Dayak longhouse traditions, all within reach of the jungle — the programming was genuinely world-class. The Cultural Village itself, with its reconstructed traditional dwellings from different Sarawak communities, is worth visiting any time of year.
snippet: The Rainforest World Music Festival at the Sarawak Cultural Village — international performers alongside Dayak longhouse traditions in the jungle — is genuinely world-class and transforms an already excellent destination into something extraordinary.

text: Port Louis was fine — other than the Central Bazaar and the waterfront there wasn't much else to do besides taking in the culture and city life. Most travelers base themselves elsewhere on the island and make Port Louis a day trip, which makes sense. The real magic of Mauritius is outside the capital: the southwestern corner has stunning cliffs, lagoons, and the dramatic landscapes that make the island famous.
snippet: Port Louis is best done as a day trip — the Central Bazaar is lively and the waterfront pleasant, but the real beauty of Mauritius is in the surrounding beaches and lagoons beyond the city.

text: Beira can feel a bit rough, especially at night, but it's not something to overly worry about if you stay in the Macuti district and stick to sensible areas. The city has a melancholy, weathered beauty — the drive through Ponta Gea along Avenida Eduardo Mondlane and the grand municipal squares give a glimpse of its colonial past. It's primarily a transit city for heading into Gorongosa National Park, which is Mozambique's most extraordinary wildlife destination.
snippet: Beira has a weathered colonial charm and is the gateway to Gorongosa National Park — one of Africa's great wildlife comeback stories. Stay in the Macuti district, take sensible precautions, and it's a perfectly manageable base.

text: I spent a few days exploring the Dhofar Mountains around Salalah and it has quickly become my new favourite region of any country I've visited. During the Khareef rainy season the mountains are vibrant with lush greenery and waterfalls, and during my four days I encountered only four other tourists at any given viewpoint or beach. The scenery is absolutely breathtaking — stunning cliffs, verdant hills, pristine beaches — unlike anything I've experienced before.
snippet: The Dhofar Mountains around Salalah are unlike anything I've seen elsewhere — during the Khareef season the cliffs and waterfalls are breathtakingly green. We had entire beaches entirely to ourselves and I loved every minute of it.

text: Pretoria is actually quite nice — safe and clean in the right areas, and I absolutely loved it so far. The jacaranda-lined streets in spring are legendary, and the Voortrekker Monument, Union Buildings, and the Smuts Museum give the city real historical depth. Stay in areas like Hatfield or Lynnwood, use Uber to get around, and you'll find a well-developed, orderly city that makes a pleasant contrast to Johannesburg's intensity.
snippet: Pretoria is safe and clean in the right areas and genuinely pleasant to explore. The Union Buildings grounds are beautiful, and the city's jacaranda trees in bloom are one of South Africa's most iconic sights.

text: Monterrey is basically Texas-lite — a wealthy, industrial city with outstanding food, great nightlife in San Pedro Garza García, and some impressive hiking in the mountains surrounding it. Parque Fundidora is a fantastic urban park and Barrio Antiguo has captivating architecture alongside a vibrant restaurant and bar scene. It's not a typical tourist city, which means fewer crowds and a more authentic experience than you get in Mexico's heritage towns.
snippet: Monterrey is a wealthy, modern city with incredible food and some of the best mountain scenery in northern Mexico. Parque Fundidora and Barrio Antiguo are the highlights — get Cabrito (BBQ goat) while you're there.

text: The hiking around Monterrey is seriously underrated — the Cañón de la Huasteca, a dramatic canyon just 20 minutes from downtown, and the Cerro de la Silla peak that defines the city's skyline both deliver proper elevation and views without the need for a guide. I rented a car for two days and covered terrain that felt genuinely remote while staying close to a fully functioning major city. For hikers, Monterrey is one of Mexico's best-kept secrets.
snippet: Cañón de la Huasteca, a dramatic canyon just 20 minutes from downtown, and the hike up Cerro de la Silla make Monterrey one of Mexico's most underrated destinations for serious hikers.

text: As a business traveler who extended my trip for a weekend, I was surprised by how well Monterrey works for leisure. San Pedro Garza García has some of the best contemporary restaurants in northern Mexico, the Museo de Arte Contemporáneo (MARCO) has a genuinely impressive permanent collection, and the local beer culture — this is the home of Cuauhtémoc Moctezuma brewery — means excellent craft and lager options everywhere.
snippet: San Pedro's contemporary restaurant scene, MARCO's impressive art collection, and the local brewing culture rooted in Cuauhtémoc Moctezuma make Monterrey a genuinely rewarding weekend extension for business travelers.

text: I moved to Monterrey for work and learned it quickly — the city is divided into very distinct zones and understanding which you're in matters a lot for safety and enjoyment. The regiomontano character is proud and direct, which I grew to deeply respect, and the local cuisine — cabrito al pastor, machacado con huevo, pan de pulque — is completely its own tradition rather than generic Mexican food. It's a city that rewards actually living in it.
snippet: Regiomontano identity is proud and distinct, and the local cuisine — cabrito, machacado con huevo, pan de pulque — is a completely separate culinary tradition from the rest of Mexico, one that rewards actually living in the city rather than just passing through.

text: We visited in late autumn specifically for Día de los Muertos and found Monterrey's celebrations more family-oriented and less performative than what you see in Mexico City or Oaxaca. The ofrendas in the Barrio Antiguo churches were genuinely moving, local families were welcoming to respectful visitors, and the cathedral lit up at night with marigold offerings all around it was one of the most beautiful scenes of my entire trip to Mexico.
snippet: Monterrey's Día de los Muertos celebrations in Barrio Antiguo are more family-oriented and less touristy than Mexico City's — the cathedral surrounded by marigold ofrendas at night was one of the most beautiful scenes of my entire Mexico trip.

text: I had expected a dreary, crumbling city, but Chisinau features lovely green parks, wide avenues, quaint coffee shops, and craft beer pubs — it felt safe, clean, and inviting. Chisinau is really compact and very safe; spending the day walking around, seeing the Soviet Victory Memorial, Parliament building, and churches is easy. I would absolutely return — good wine in Moldova is a serious underrated pleasure, and the cave monastery at Orhei Vechi is a stunning day trip.
snippet: I expected a dreary post-Soviet city but Chisinau has lovely green parks, good coffee shops, and great wine bars. It's compact, very safe, and far more charming than its reputation suggests.

text: Paramaribo has some old colonial-style architecture and a fascinating mix of cultures that you won't find anywhere else — Dutch colonial, Creole, Javanese, Hindustani, and Indigenous all layered together on the streets of one small capital. The historic inner city is a UNESCO World Heritage site and very walkable. The beautiful places are in the interior jungle though — tours there are expensive but the Central Suriname Nature Reserve is truly extraordinary.
snippet: Paramaribo's UNESCO-listed historic inner city has a colonial Dutch charm unlike anywhere else in South America. The real draw is the interior jungle — expensive to reach but the wildlife is extraordinary.

text: Walking the UNESCO-listed inner city of Paramaribo early in the morning before the heat sets in is a genuinely peaceful experience — the wooden colonial buildings, the mix of mosques and synagogues standing side by side, and the riverside promenade give the capital a quiet dignity that surprised me. Fort Zeelandia on the waterfront has a complicated colonial history now explored in a well-presented museum that doesn't shy away from the difficult parts.
snippet: The wooden colonial city at dawn, a mosque and synagogue standing peacefully side by side, and Fort Zeelandia's honest colonial history museum — Paramaribo's inner city has a quiet dignity that consistently surprises first-time visitors.

text: The food in Paramaribo is one of the most genuinely multicultural eating experiences I've had anywhere. In a single afternoon I ate roti with potato curry at a Hindustani spot, then pom — a Sephardic-influenced casserole that became an Afro-Surinamese staple — at a market stall, then an Indonesian nasi goreng at a Chinese-run warung. This culinary layering doesn't exist at this density anywhere else in South America.
snippet: Hindustani roti, Afro-Surinamese pom, and Indonesian nasi goreng from three different stalls in a single afternoon — Paramaribo's culinary layering doesn't exist at this density anywhere else on the South American continent.

text: We booked a four-day jungle tour from Paramaribo into the interior and it remains one of the most extraordinary experiences of my life. Staying in an Amerindian village on the Marowijne River, swimming in brown jungle water, and waking to absolute darkness and birdsong — the Central Suriname Nature Reserve is a genuine wilderness. The tours aren't cheap but the money goes directly to local guides and communities.
snippet: A four-day jungle tour into the Central Suriname Nature Reserve, staying in an Amerindian village on the Marowijne River and waking to birdsong in genuine wilderness, is among the most extraordinary experiences South America offers.

text: Suriname was the most surprising country of my South America loop and Paramaribo set the tone immediately upon arrival. The Dutch spoken on the streets, the Chinese supermarkets alongside Javanese food stalls, the Creole music drifting out of a bar near the Independence Square — it felt like five countries compressed into one capital city. The Surinamese people were among the most genuinely welcoming I encountered anywhere on the continent.
snippet: Dutch on the streets, Chinese supermarkets beside Javanese food stalls, Creole music near Independence Square — Paramaribo compresses five cultural worlds into one capital city and was the most surprising stop in my entire South America loop.

text: Rabat provided a striking contrast to the chaotic medina of Fez — it's more organized and relaxed, making it a welcome respite. The stunning Kasbah of the Udayas is perfect for sunset viewing, and the Chellah ruins combining Roman-era and medieval Islamic architecture set in lush greenery by the river are genuinely impressive and uncrowded. One to two days here feels right; it's easy to appreciate the city's elegant pace before moving on.
snippet: Rabat is a peaceful contrast to Fez or Marrakech — the Kasbah of the Udayas at sunset is beautiful, and the Chellah ruins are one of Morocco's most atmospheric and least-visited sites.

text: Arequipa is the highly underrated city of Peru — an absolutely stunning hidden gem with beautiful colonial architecture, great food, and Mt. Misti volcano looming over everything. I loved the queso helado (especially fresh-spun) and stuffed rocoto peppers, and had a little apartment with a rooftop view of the volcano while cooking. It's more laid back than Cusco and has more colonial sights than Lima, and the day trip to Colca Canyon to watch condors soaring overhead was unforgettable.
snippet: Arequipa is the hidden gem of Peru — stunning white volcanic-stone colonial architecture, incredible food, and Mt. Misti towering above the rooftops. A day trip to Colca Canyon to watch condors is unmissable.

text: We spent four days in Arequipa eating our way through the city and barely scratched the surface — picanterías, the traditional local restaurants serving slow-cooked stews, are a genuine culinary institution you won't find in Lima or Cusco. The Monasterio de Santa Catalina, a walled city within a city that functioned for centuries as an enclosed convent, is one of the most atmospheric places I've visited in all of South America.
snippet: Arequipa's traditional picanterías serving slow-cooked stews are a culinary institution unique to this city, and the Monasterio de Santa Catalina — a walled city-within-a-city once an enclosed convent — is one of the most atmospheric places in South America.

text: Arequipa at 2,300 meters is a sensible acclimatization stop before heading to Cusco or the Colca Canyon, and I found the altitude gentle enough to walk everywhere without discomfort. The Plaza de Armas surrounded by sillar volcanic white stone buildings glowing in the late afternoon sun is one of the most beautiful central squares in Latin America. The city's self-contained identity — they half-jokingly call it the República de Arequipa — gives it a confidence and pride that's palpable.
snippet: The Plaza de Armas surrounded by glowing white sillar volcanic stone buildings at golden hour is one of Latin America's most beautiful central squares — and Arequipa's fierce local identity, joking about the 'República de Arequipa,' gives the whole city a palpable confidence.

text: I came to Arequipa as a base for trekking the Colca Canyon — at twice the depth of the Grand Canyon it's one of the most dramatic landscapes I've ever stood in, and the condors riding the morning thermals above us were enormous and close enough to count feathers. Getting back to Arequipa's comfortable restaurants and good pisco bars after two days of camping made the city feel like a luxury reward.
snippet: Colca Canyon at twice the depth of the Grand Canyon, with condors riding morning thermals close enough to count their feathers, then returning to Arequipa's pisco bars and comfortable restaurants — this two-day loop is an essential Andean experience.

text: What struck me about Arequipa from a design perspective is how well preserved the colonial streetscape is — the entire historic centre is a UNESCO site and it earns that designation. The carved sillar stone facades of the churches and mansions are intricate in a way that photographs don't capture, and hiring a local architecture guide for a half-day walk through the Centro Histórico was one of the best-value decisions I made in Peru.
snippet: The UNESCO-listed Centro Histórico's intricately carved sillar stone church facades are something photographs don't adequately capture — hiring a local architecture guide for a half-day walk through it was one of the best-value decisions I made in Peru.

text: Chiang Rai is amazing if you're into artsy and spiritual places — the White Temple, Blue Temple, and Wat Huay Pla Kang complex are all magnificent sights absolutely worth visiting, especially while they're still relatively uncrowded. There's also a stunning royal garden and palace on a mountain to the north that most visitors miss entirely. The town itself is small and quiet, which actually makes it a pleasant break from the pace of Chiang Mai.
snippet: Chiang Rai's White Temple and Blue Temple are among the most visually spectacular religious buildings in Southeast Asia — genuinely unmissable. The town is small and laid-back, which makes it a great breather between busier destinations.

text: Maastricht is a beautiful town and I might be biased since I live here — it's in the south and 3–4 hours from Amsterdam by train, but it's easy to also visit Germany or Belgium as a day trip from here. The underground caves and tunnels beneath the city are fascinating to explore, and the lively Vrijthof square with its cafes and restaurants has a distinctly un-Dutch, almost Belgian or French character. Deserves at least a full two days to explore properly.
snippet: Maastricht is beautiful and has a distinctly non-Dutch feel — the café culture, underground tunnels, and proximity to Belgium and Germany give it a unique character. Two days here is time very well spent.

text: Christchurch is a city in fascinating transition — the rebuild after the 2011 earthquake has produced some genuinely creative architecture and street art, and the Botanic Gardens and Riverside Market are lovely places to spend a morning. The Cardboard Cathedral is a remarkable piece of design, and punting on the Avon River through the gardens feels wonderfully timeless despite everything the city has been through. It makes a fantastic launch point for exploring the South Island's extraordinary landscapes.
snippet: Christchurch's post-earthquake rebuild has created a surprisingly creative and artsy city — the Cardboard Cathedral and inventive new architecture are worth seeing. It's also the perfect gateway to the South Island's stunning landscapes.

text: As an architect I found Christchurch one of the most fascinating urban case studies I've ever walked through. The earthquake sequence accelerated a kind of urban experimentation that established cities never permit — shipping-container malls, temporary pavilions that became permanent, community-designed parklands on vacant lots. It's a city actively thinking about what it wants to be, and that energy is palpable in every neighbourhood.
snippet: Christchurch is one of the most fascinating urban case studies in the world — post-earthquake experimentation has produced shipping-container malls, community-designed parks on vacant lots, and a city actively reinventing itself in real time.

text: We based ourselves in Christchurch for five days and used it as a launchpad into the surrounding Canterbury region — the drive through the Rakaia Gorge to the alpine village of Methven, the whale-watching at Kaikōura up the coast, and the wine region of Waipara all fit easily into day trips. The International Antarctic Centre near the airport is a brilliant half-day for anyone interested in polar exploration.
snippet: Christchurch's position gives easy access to whale-watching at Kaikōura, alpine scenery at the Rakaia Gorge, and the Waipara wine region — and the International Antarctic Centre near the airport is a brilliant half-day for any polar-exploration enthusiast.

text: The mountain biking trails in the Port Hills above Christchurch are world-class and almost unknown outside New Zealand. I rented a bike from a shop in Sumner — the relaxed coastal suburb on the southern edge of the city — and spent a full day on the ridgeline tracks with views over the Pacific and back toward the Southern Alps. Coming back down into Sumner for fish and chips on the beach made for a perfect New Zealand afternoon.
snippet: The Port Hills mountain bike trails above Christchurch are world-class and almost entirely unknown outside New Zealand — ride the ridgeline with Pacific and Southern Alps views, then descend to Sumner for fish and chips on the beach.

text: Visiting Christchurch with my family including young children worked better than expected — the Margaret Mahy Playground on the Avon River is genuinely one of the most impressive free public playgrounds I've encountered anywhere in the world, and the gondola up to the Summit Road on the Port Hills gave the kids a panoramic view they still talk about. The city has invested heavily in free, publicly accessible infrastructure and it shows.
snippet: The Margaret Mahy Playground on the Avon River is one of the finest free public playgrounds in the world, and the gondola ride up to Summit Road for panoramic views over the city and coast makes Christchurch an outstanding family destination.

text: I absolutely loved Tromsø — it's more expansive than I initially expected, and the northern lights tour was the most surreal experience of my life: the sky was illuminated with vibrant greens dancing with shades of pink, leaving everyone in awe. The locals were incredibly friendly and the cable car up Fjellheisen gives one of the most stunning views I've ever seen, especially at night. I'd love to return in summer to experience the all-night sunshine and the hills in bloom.
snippet: The northern lights above Tromsø were the most surreal thing I've ever witnessed — greens and pinks dancing across the entire sky. The locals are incredibly welcoming, and the cable car view at night is breathtaking.

text: I visited Tromsø in summer specifically to experience the midnight sun, and it delivered something genuinely disorienting and magical — hiking at 11pm in broad daylight, the fjords reflecting gold at what felt like mid-afternoon, and a strange freedom that comes from a world without imposed bedtimes. The Arctic-Alpine Botanical Garden is the world's northernmost botanical garden and was in full dramatic bloom during my June visit.
snippet: Hiking at 11pm in full broad daylight with fjords reflecting gold, and the world's northernmost botanical garden in dramatic June bloom — Tromsø's midnight sun summer is as disorienting and magical as the northern lights in winter.

text: Tromsø has developed a genuinely impressive craft-beer and restaurant scene that punches well above the expectations you'd have for a small Arctic city. The Mack Brewery is the world's northernmost brewery and still going strong, and several younger craft operations have opened in the last few years that are doing interesting things with local foraged ingredients like cloudberry and Arctic herbs. Whale-watching boat trips from the harbour in winter when orcas and humpbacks follow the herring are unforgettable.
snippet: Whale-watching from Tromsø harbour in winter when orcas and humpbacks follow herring into the fjords, combined with world's-northernmost brewery visits and craft beer made with foraged Arctic herbs, makes this small city far richer than it looks on paper.

text: We drove out of Tromsø along the E8 toward Skibotn and the mountain scenery — fjords, snowy peaks, the occasional reindeer crossing the road — made a two-hour drive one of the most memorable of my life. The Sámi culture visible in the region, including the reindeer herding communities, adds a dimension to Tromsø that most visitors who just stay in the city centre completely miss. Renting a car for a day is essential.
snippet: Driving the E8 toward Skibotn with fjords, snowy peaks, and reindeer crossing the road — and encountering Sámi reindeer-herding culture in the surrounding region — reveals a Tromsø far beyond what city-centre visitors ever see.

text: As a solo traveler who arrived without a guided tour, I found the free-standing northern lights experience in Tromsø entirely achievable — I downloaded a KP-index app, rented a car, drove 40 minutes out of the light pollution, and sat on the hood in minus fifteen with a thermos of coffee watching the aurora erupt overhead for two hours. The city itself is small enough to walk everywhere, and the Polaria Arctic science centre is an excellent rainy-day museum.
snippet: Renting a car, driving 40 minutes from Tromsø's light pollution, and watching the aurora erupt overhead for two hours from a car hood with a thermos of coffee — the DIY northern lights experience is entirely achievable and deeply satisfying.

text: Granada is more laid back and chill — I really liked the feel of the city, especially the Spanish colonial architecture with lush, shady inner courtyards that you step into off the hot outer street. The town almost seemed like a very old movie set in the best possible way, and the colorful cathedral and lakefront are lovely. It's a compact, walkable city that pairs well with a day trip out to the Mombacho volcano or the Isletas on Lake Nicaragua.
snippet: Granada has a beautiful, laid-back colonial charm — the colorful cathedral, shady inner courtyards, and lakefront make it one of Central America's most photogenic cities. Pair it with a day trip to Mombacho volcano for the full experience.

text: Old San Juan is where I spent most of my time and it's absolutely charming — the cobblestoned streets, pastel buildings, and ocean views from the city walls could keep you busy for days. The locals are warm and welcoming, and most have a good grasp of English; sprinkle in a bit of Spanglish and you'll have a great time. La Placita Santurce at night is one of the most fun outdoor bar scenes I've experienced anywhere.
snippet: Old San Juan's cobblestoned streets, pastel colonial buildings, and ocean views are endlessly photogenic. La Placita Santurce at night is one of the most vibrant outdoor bar scenes in the Caribbean.

text: Salt Lake City has beautiful mountains on all sides and makes an outstanding base for exploring some of the best national parks in the American West — Zion, Bryce Canyon, and Arches are all within range. The downtown itself has good restaurants, interesting museums including the Clark Planetarium, and the Capitol building with Ensign Peak just to the north offers a great free hike with panoramic city views. You will definitely need a car to see the surrounding nature, but the city itself is very manageable on public transit.
snippet: Salt Lake City's mountains are stunning on all sides and it's the perfect base for Utah's incredible national parks. The city itself has great food, an interesting history, and free public transit downtown.

text: I spent a week in Salt Lake City focused entirely on skiing, and the access from the city is genuinely extraordinary — multiple world-class ski resorts within 40 minutes of downtown, including Alta and Snowbird in Little Cottonwood Canyon, with some of the deepest and lightest powder I've skied anywhere in North America. Coming back into the city for excellent restaurants and craft cocktail bars after a day on the mountain felt like the ideal winter rhythm.
snippet: Multiple world-class ski resorts including Alta and Snowbird within 40 minutes of downtown, then excellent restaurants and craft cocktail bars back in the city — Salt Lake City offers one of the best ski-and-city combinations in North America.

text: The Mormon cultural and architectural heritage is genuinely interesting even for secular visitors — Temple Square in the centre of the city is beautifully maintained and the Tabernacle with its extraordinary acoustics is worth attending a free choir rehearsal. The Church History Museum adjacent to it tells the entire saga of the Latter-day Saint movement in a surprisingly even-handed and compelling way. Whatever your views on religion, the story of this city is remarkable.
snippet: Temple Square's beautifully maintained architecture, the Tabernacle's extraordinary acoustics during free choir rehearsals, and the Church History Museum's compelling account of the LDS movement make Salt Lake City's religious heritage genuinely fascinating for any visitor.

text: The Natural History Museum of Utah on the University of Utah campus is one of the finest in the western United States — the dinosaur collection is exceptional, the Utah geology displays are beautifully designed, and the building itself by architect Ennead sits into the hillside in a way that frames views of the Wasatch Range from inside. The adjacent Red Butte Garden in autumn was one of the most beautiful free-to-enter spaces I found in the whole region.
snippet: The Natural History Museum of Utah on the University campus is one of the finest natural history museums in the American West — the dinosaur collection is exceptional and the building itself frames stunning Wasatch Range views from inside.

text: We did a road trip that started and ended in Salt Lake City and the logistics worked perfectly — the car rental options are plentiful, the roads south into canyon country are well-maintained and clearly signed, and having a proper city with good food and accommodation as a bookend to days of remote national park driving made the whole trip feel very balanced. The Great Salt Lake itself, though dramatically reduced in recent years, is still a stark and otherworldly landscape worth seeing.
snippet: Using Salt Lake City as a road-trip anchor for canyon country in both directions is near-perfect logistically — and the Great Salt Lake, stark and dramatically shrunken but still otherworldly, is worth a detour before you leave.

text: Asunción is charming and a bit of an underdog city — the museums, old colonial buildings, and the Costanera waterfront make for a pleasant few days, and Paraguayan people are genuinely very kind. The traditional food scene — chipas, mbeju, vori vori soup — is worth exploring, and Lido Bar is the place to do it. Use Uber to get around rather than walking in unfamiliar areas, especially downtown, and you'll have a comfortable and surprisingly rewarding visit.
snippet: Asunción is an underdog capital with genuine charm — the Costanera waterfront, old museums, and traditional food scene are worth a few days. Paraguayans are remarkably kind and the city feels far more relaxed than its neighbors.

text: Asunción has an easy unhurried pace that I found deeply restorative after weeks of faster-moving South American capitals. The Costanera along the Paraguay River is excellent for evening walks and the views of the wetlands from the waterfront are unexpectedly wild for a capital city. The Palacio de los López — the presidential palace on the riverfront — is a beautiful building with a dramatic history that local guides bring alive.
snippet: The Costanera's evening views over wild Paraguay River wetlands and the story-laden Palacio de los López give Asunción a distinctive, unhurried character that makes it a genuinely restorative stop between faster-moving South American capitals.

text: I spent a week in Asunción specifically to study the city's unique architectural heritage — the Spanish colonial buildings in the historic centre coexist with ornate Italian-influenced 19th-century civic buildings and some surprisingly good modernist work from the mid-20th century. The Casa de la Independencia museum is one of South America's most intimate and atmospheric independence-era sites. Architecture enthusiasts could fill several days here without any effort.
snippet: Spanish colonial buildings beside ornate 19th-century Italian-influenced civic architecture and mid-century modernist work — Asunción's layered urban heritage is a genuinely rewarding study for architecture enthusiasts, anchored by the intimate Casa de la Independencia museum.

text: As the most affordable capital city in South America, Asunción surprised me with the quality of what the low prices deliver. A long lunch of grilled river fish with sopa paraguaya bread at a riverside restaurant cost next to nothing and was excellent, the craft beer bar scene near Villa Morra has grown quickly and is genuinely impressive, and a taxi across the entire city costs about the same as a single bus ride in Buenos Aires.
snippet: Grilled river fish with sopa paraguaya bread for next to nothing, a genuinely good craft beer scene in Villa Morra, and taxi fares cheaper than a Buenos Aires bus ride — Asunción delivers remarkable quality at South America's most affordable capital-city prices.

text: We visited Asunción as a gateway to the Chaco and the Jesuit Mission ruins at Trinidad and Jesús, which are within day-trip distance and completely worth it. The Trinidad mission ruins are a UNESCO World Heritage site and the most impressive pre-modern architecture in the entire country — standing in the semi-circular church nave open to the sky, listening to nothing but birds, was one of the most memorable moments of our South America trip.
snippet: The UNESCO Jesuit mission ruins at Trinidad — a semi-circular church nave open to the sky with only birdsong around it — are among the most memorable pre-modern architectural spaces in South America and an easy day trip from Asunción.

text: Manila is a city of stark contrasts — the Spanish colonial streets of Intramuros are genuinely charming, but traffic is absolutely horrendous, often moving at bicycle speed even with Grab. The malls in BGC and Makati are immense and very modern, but wandering as a pedestrian outside the upmarket districts is uncomfortable. Most travelers I met agreed: Manila is more of a stopover than the main event — get your bearings, eat at Binondo, then head to the islands.
snippet: Manila is a city of stark contrasts — Intramuros is genuinely charming, but traffic is absolutely horrendous and wandering as a pedestrian outside the upmarket districts is uncomfortable.

text: Bern is central, charming, and remarkably easy to use as a base for the rest of Switzerland. The old town is genuinely gorgeous — the medieval arcades, the clock tower, and the cathedral are all worth your time. It's not the flashiest Swiss city, but it rewards slow walkers who just want to soak up the atmosphere without the crowds of Zurich or Lucerne.
snippet: Bern's old town is genuinely gorgeous — the medieval arcades and the cathedral are worth your time, and it's a perfect base for the rest of Switzerland.

text: The six kilometres of covered arcades running through Bern's Old Town made walking around the city in winter rain completely effortless and rather magical — you can cross half the city without ever getting wet. The Zytglogge astronomical clock's mechanical figures performing at the top of the hour drew a small crowd, but the bears in the BernMobil terraced enclosure down by the river were the unexpected highlight for my kids.
snippet: Six kilometres of covered medieval arcades mean you can walk half of Bern in a rainstorm without getting wet — and the bears in the riverside BernMobil enclosure, Bern's namesake animals, are a genuine delight.

text: Bern makes an extraordinary day-trip base — I took morning trains to Grindelwald and the Jungfraujoch, then came back for dinner at a fondue restaurant in the Old Town, and the whole thing cost less and felt far more civilised than staying in the Bernese Oberland resorts. The Paul Klee Centre on the edge of the city, housed in a remarkable wave-like building by Renzo Piano, is one of the finest single-artist museums I've visited in Europe.
snippet: A morning train to Grindelwald and Jungfraujoch, back to the Old Town for fondue in the evening — and the Paul Klee Centre in its Renzo Piano wave-building makes Bern an extraordinary day-trip hub and a city worth properly exploring in its own right.

text: As a food and market enthusiast I was very happy in Bern. The Tuesday and Saturday markets along the Bundesgasse sell Bernese alpine cheeses, smoked meats, and late-season vegetables from surrounding farms, and the Kornhauskeller restaurant built into a Baroque former granary vault is one of the most dramatic dining rooms in Switzerland. The city is expensive but not outrageously so compared to Zurich or Geneva.
snippet: Tuesday and Saturday markets along the Bundesgasse selling alpine cheese and smoked meats, then dinner in the Kornhauskeller's Baroque vaulted granary — Bern's food experience is quietly excellent and notably more affordable than Zurich or Geneva.

text: What I love about Bern as a political capital is how utterly unassuming it is — the Swiss Federal Palace sits at one end of the Old Town and you can walk right up to it, and the Parliament sessions are actually open to the public to attend from the gallery. The Einstein Museum dedicated to the years Einstein spent in Bern developing the special theory of relativity is a genuinely engaging science biography as much as a physics exhibit.
snippet: Parliament sessions open to the public gallery, the Einstein Museum tracing the special theory of relativity to its Bern origins, and a federal palace you can simply walk up to — Bern wears its status as a capital city with the most charming lack of pretension.

text: Catania has a genuine, lived-in charm that feels more authentically Sicilian than the tourist-polished cities. The fish market is remarkable — frigid volcanic water flows beneath it from Etna — and the city comes alive after 9 PM. Yes, parts of it are rough around the edges and not especially clean, but I found that added to its character rather than detracting from it.
snippet: Catania has a genuine, lived-in charm that feels authentically Sicilian, and the city absolutely comes alive after 9 PM around the baroque streets and fish market.

text: As a base for exploring eastern Sicily, Catania is absolutely ideal — Etna's summit craters are an hour away, Taormina is a 40-minute train ride, and the Alcantara Gorge where the river cuts through volcanic basalt columns is one of the most visually striking landscapes I found in all of Italy. The city itself rewards late evenings when the outdoor tables fill and the streets around Via Etnea become genuinely festive.
snippet: Catania's position makes eastern Sicily immediately accessible — Etna an hour away, Taormina 40 minutes by train, and the Alcantara Gorge's basalt river canyon one of Italy's most striking landscapes.

text: I spent a week in Catania in early spring focused on street food and came away thinking it rivals Palermo — arancini stuffed with pistachio and ricotta, sciusceddu soup eaten at a plastic table in the market, and granite for breakfast at a bar near the Elephant Fountain like a local. The volcanic stone baroque streetscape in the historic centre, rebuilt after the 1693 earthquake, is dramatic and beautiful in a way that Catania doesn't always get credit for.
snippet: Pistachio-and-ricotta arancini, sciusceddu soup in the market, granite for breakfast at the Elephant Fountain — Catania's street food culture rivals Palermo, and the volcanic-stone baroque centre rebuilt after 1693 is more beautiful than the city usually gets credit for.

text: The proximity to Etna transforms Catania from a regular city break into something much more interesting for outdoors-minded travelers. I joined a small guided group for a dawn hike to the main summit craters and the experience of walking on cooling lava fields with sulfurous steam venting around you, watching the sun rise over the Ionian Sea below, is not something you forget easily. Catania's gritty energy feels exactly right when you return dusty and elated in the afternoon.
snippet: A dawn hike to Etna's summit craters — walking on cooling lava fields with sulfurous steam venting, watching the sun rise over the Ionian Sea — is the kind of experience that transforms Catania from a city break into something genuinely unforgettable.

text: Catania has a small but growing contemporary art scene centred around the area near Via Crociferi that I found surprisingly compelling for a city this size. Several independent galleries showing young Sicilian artists occupy converted baroque buildings, and the photography exhibitions I stumbled into during an afternoon wander were of a quality I'd expect in Rome or Milan. Coming back to the city at dusk with the golden light on the lava stone buildings is a moment I'd pay good money to repeat.
snippet: Independent galleries showing young Sicilian artists in converted baroque buildings near Via Crociferi are a genuine surprise — and returning to the city at dusk with golden light on black lava stone is a moment worth travelling for on its own.

text: Davao is a perfectly comfortable city to base yourself in, but most of the real highlights require getting out of town. The Philippine Eagle Center is more than just a zoo — it's a fascinating conservation hub for the national bird. I rented a car one afternoon and drove to the Malagos Chocolate factory and a contemporary art gallery all in the same area, which was a surprisingly rewarding day.
snippet: The Philippine Eagle Center is more than a zoo — it's a fascinating conservation hub — and the Malagos area packs the Eagle Center, a chocolate factory, and a contemporary art gallery all within reach.

text: Porto is effortlessly charming — a little gritty in the best way, packed with history, and genuinely unforgettable to explore on foot. We much preferred it to Lisbon; there's a warmth and authenticity here that felt less packaged for tourists. The riverfront, the azulejo-covered churches, and the wine cellars in Vila Nova de Gaia kept us busy for days and we still didn't see everything.
snippet: Porto is effortlessly charming — a little gritty in the best way, packed with history, and we much preferred it to Lisbon for its warmth and authenticity.

text: São Miguel in the Azores is one of the most stunning places I've ever visited. The people are warm and welcoming, the food is top-notch, the natural thermal pools are incredibly soothing, and the landscapes are simply breathtaking. I did an insane amount of hiking and loved every minute — Furnas, Sete Cidades, Terra Nostra — it felt like a paradise for nature lovers and had almost zero of the overtourism you find in mainland Portugal.
snippet: São Miguel in the Azores felt like a paradise for nature lovers — the thermal pools, volcanic lakes, and lush hiking trails are stunning, with none of the overtourism you find on the mainland.

text: We island-hopped between Faial, Pico, and São Jorge over ten days and found each one surprisingly distinct in character. Pico's UNESCO-listed vineyard landscape — thousands of low black basalt walls protecting vines from Atlantic wind — is unlike any wine region I've seen anywhere, and the Pico wines are worth seeking out. Faial's CaldeiraVolcano crater trail and the marina at Horta covered in yacht murals from sailors who've crossed the Atlantic gave the island an end-of-the-world adventurousness.
snippet: Pico's UNESCO basalt vineyard walls protecting Atlantic vines, Faial's Caldeira crater trail, and the marina at Horta covered in yacht murals from Atlantic crossings — island-hopping the western Azores is endlessly varied and quietly extraordinary.

text: I came to the Azores specifically for whale watching and it delivered beyond any expectation — sperm whales are resident year-round and the operators use traditional vigias (lookout posts) on hilltops to spot them exactly as whalers did in the 19th century. Swimming with wild dolphins in open Atlantic water in the afternoon after the whale trip was the most exhilarating wildlife experience of my life. The marine life density here is unmatched in European waters.
snippet: Sperm whales spotted from traditional hilltop lookout posts, then swimming with wild dolphins in open Atlantic water the same afternoon — the Azores offers marine wildlife encounters that are unmatched anywhere in European waters.

text: I went to Terceira island in the Azores for the June Sanjoaninas festival and it turned out to be the most joyful travel experience I had all year. The entire island seems to participate — street bulls running in wooden-fenced lanes through the villages, brass bands, fireworks every night, and the most welcoming festival crowd imaginable. The UNESCO Angra do Heroísmo on Terceira is also one of the most perfectly preserved baroque port towns in the Atlantic.
snippet: Terceira's June Sanjoaninas festival — street bulls, brass bands, fireworks every night, and an extraordinarily welcoming local crowd — combined with the UNESCO baroque port of Angra do Heroísmo makes it one of the most joyful island experiences in the Atlantic.

text: As someone who surfs, the Azores offered something I hadn't expected — genuinely world-class waves on São Miguel's northern coast with almost no one in the water, because the island isn't on the international surf circuit yet. Ribeira Grande has a couple of surf camps that provide equipment and guidance, and spending mornings in the water then afternoons in the Sete Cidades volcanic lake caldera made for the most perfectly balanced active holiday I've had.
snippet: World-class surf on São Miguel's northern coast at Ribeira Grande with almost no one in the water, then afternoons at the Sete Cidades volcanic lake caldera — the Azores delivers the most perfectly balanced active holiday in the North Atlantic.

text: Freetown has an intensity and buzz that few other West African cities match. The beaches were pretty nice and the people were very friendly — always saying hello, which was genuine rather than performative. Traffic in the city is absolute hell on the narrow roads, and getting from the airport involves a ferry crossing that adds its own adventure. It's not an easy destination, but that's part of the appeal.
snippet: Freetown has an intensity and buzz that few West African cities match — the people are genuinely friendly and the beaches are lovely, though the traffic and airport logistics require serious patience.

text: The beaches within reach of Freetown are genuinely world-class — Tokeh, River No. 2, and Bureh Beach are all stunning and almost entirely undeveloped in the way that Caribbean beaches haven't been for decades. Getting there involves a mixture of poda-poda shared minibuses and motorbike taxis which is an experience in itself, but arriving at a white-sand beach backed by jungle with almost no one else around makes the effort feel completely worthwhile.
snippet: Tokeh, River No. 2, and Bureh Beach are Caribbean-quality white-sand beaches backed by jungle with almost no development and almost no one on them — Freetown's surrounding coast is one of West Africa's most spectacular and least-visited.

text: The cotton tree at the centre of Freetown — an enormous ancient tree that has stood in the city centre since the freed slaves arrived in the 18th century — has a resonance that stops you in your tracks when you understand its history. The Sierra Leone National Museum is small but gives important context for the country's remarkable story. Food was excellent and cheap; I ate grilled barracuda and cassava leaf stew at a street-side spot near Aberdeen Beach that I still think about.
snippet: The ancient cotton tree in Freetown's centre, planted by freed slaves in the 18th century, has a historical resonance that stops you in your tracks — and the grilled barracuda near Aberdeen Beach is the kind of meal you think about for years.

text: I visited Freetown as part of a West Africa loop and found it one of the most genuinely warm cities on the continent — the energy is hard to describe but it has a resilience and forward momentum that felt distinctive even compared to Accra or Dakar. The peninsula drive south through forested hills to Tokeh Beach and back via York gives you every version of the city and country in one long day. The ferry crossing from Lungi Airport remains a genuine adventure.
snippet: The peninsula drive south through forested hills to Tokeh Beach and back via York compresses Freetown, the forest, and Sierra Leone's coast into one extraordinary day — and the city's forward-moving energy feels distinctive even within West Africa.

text: What surprised me most about Freetown was its creative energy — there's a music and arts scene rooted in Afrobeats, Afropop, and the distinctly Sierra Leonean Bubu music tradition that comes alive at the bars and outdoor venues around Aberdeen on weekend nights. I stumbled into a live music night that had locals and expats mixed together in a way that felt genuinely communal rather than curated. Sierra Leone is harder to visit than its neighbours but that makes it more rewarding.
snippet: Freetown's weekend live music scene in Aberdeen — Afrobeats, Afropop, and the distinctly Sierra Leonean Bubu tradition in outdoor bars mixing locals and expats — is one of West Africa's most authentic and underappreciated musical experiences.

text: Laval is very much a suburban city designed around cars and shopping, not sightseeing. Centropolis and Carrefour Laval are the main gathering spots for evenings out, and the older districts like Sainte-Rose along the river have a quieter, genuinely pleasant feel. In winter there are maintained snowshoe trails through the Boisé Papineau, and the Cosmodome space museum is worth a visit if you have kids or an interest in space exploration.
snippet: Laval is a car-first suburb where Centropolis handles the nightlife and the old riverside district of Sainte-Rose offers a quieter, genuinely pleasant stroll — not a city for sightseeing, but comfortable to spend time in.

text: As a local resident I appreciate Laval most in summer when the Rivière des Prairies waterfront comes alive — kayak rentals, cycling paths, and outdoor restaurants in Sainte-Rose give the city a genuinely pleasant leisure character that doesn't require going into Montreal at all. The Parc de la Rivière-des-Mille-Îles is excellent for stand-up paddleboarding through the islands and is one of the better nature escapes within 30 minutes of a major Canadian city.
snippet: The Rivière des Prairies waterfront in summer, the cycling paths through Sainte-Rose, and stand-up paddleboarding through the Parc de la Rivière-des-Mille-Îles islands make Laval a genuinely pleasant leisure city without needing to cross into Montreal.

text: Laval surprised me with its Lebanese and Mediterranean restaurant scene — the density of very good Lebanese food along Boulevard Samson and in Chomedey is one of the best in the greater Montreal area, and several spots serve until late. The Centropolis outdoor area is lively on warm evenings and has a decent range of dining options across price points. It's not a destination city but it functions well as a quieter, more affordable base for exploring greater Montreal.
snippet: The concentration of excellent Lebanese restaurants along Boulevard Samson in Chomedey is among the best in the greater Montreal area — Laval works well as a quieter, more affordable base with some genuinely good dining of its own.

text: Brought my eight-year-old nephew to Laval for a school-holiday week and the Cosmodome was the clear highlight — the scale models, the simulation of living in space, and the mission control reconstructions kept him completely absorbed for most of a day, and the gift shop was dangerous for both of us. The Ski Prévost and nearby ski hills are a short drive away and are ideal for beginner and intermediate skiers without the crowds of major Quebec resorts.
snippet: The Cosmodome's space mission simulations and mission-control reconstructions can absorb a child for a full day, and the nearby ski hills offer a low-crowd, beginner-friendly winter alternative to the busier Quebec resorts.

text: The Vieux-Sainte-Rose neighbourhood along the river is worth a slow afternoon walk — the old stone church, the heritage houses, the locally-owned café terrasses in summer, and the art galleries in converted historic buildings give it a character entirely different from Laval's suburban commercial image. The weekend farmers' market there in late summer has excellent local Quebec produce and is a favourite among residents who know where to look.
snippet: Vieux-Sainte-Rose along the river — stone churches, heritage houses, café terrasses, and a summer farmers' market with excellent Quebec produce — is entirely different from Laval's suburban commercial image and well worth a slow afternoon.

text: Réunion is a stunning volcanic island that rewards hikers and nature-lovers above all else. I landed in Saint-Denis and then made my way around the island — the Cirques are breathtaking, the food is a wonderful mix of French, African, and Indian influences, and the whole place felt as lush as Hawaii but far less visited. Traffic around Saint-Denis can be bad, and French is absolutely essential as English is rarely spoken.
snippet: Réunion rewards nature-lovers above all else — landing in Saint-Denis and fanning out across the island's volcanic Cirques, waterfalls, and lush hiking trails felt like discovering a secret Hawaii that the world hasn't found yet.

text: Constanta is a port and beach resort town, and it's best understood that way rather than as a cultural destination. The Roman ruins and old town center have real historical interest, and the train ride from Bucharest through the flat Romanian steppe is memorably different. For atmosphere, the nearby stretch of coast around Vama Veche is far more interesting — fresh fish eaten on the beach, tiki bars, and spontaneous late-night parties with strangers.
snippet: Constanta's Roman ruins and old town have real historical interest, and the train ride from Bucharest is memorably different — but the nearby Vama Veche coastline, with its beach fish restaurants and spontaneous late-night parties, is where the real character lives.

text: I came to Constanta in late spring specifically to avoid the crowds and found the old town in an agreeable state of quiet — the Mosque of Carol I, the largest mosque in Romania, was open and serene without a tourist in sight, and the Archaeological Museum's collection of Greek and Roman artefacts from the ancient colony of Tomis genuinely impressed me. The seafood restaurants along the port promenade were excellent and priced for locals.
snippet: The Mosque of Carol I serenely empty in late spring, the Archaeological Museum's Greek and Roman artefacts from ancient Tomis, and local-priced seafood on the port promenade — Constanta out of season is a very rewarding and affordable Black Sea stop.

text: The casino building on the seafront at Constanta is one of the most beautiful pieces of art nouveau architecture in southeastern Europe and it broke my heart to see it in such disrepair — apparently restoration has been debated for years. Paired with the Roman mosaic discovered in the city centre and preserved in situ under a purpose-built pavilion, Constanta offers architectural highlights that feel completely disproportionate to its tourist profile.
snippet: Constanta's ruinous but spectacular art nouveau casino on the seafront and the enormous Roman mosaic preserved in situ under a purpose-built pavilion are two of southeastern Europe's most underrated architectural highlights.

text: We drove the Romanian Black Sea coast from Constanta south through Eforie Nord to Mangalia and found a completely different holiday culture from anything in western Europe — communist-era spa hotels, pensioners doing morning walks in the sea, sunflower fields right to the beach, and almost no foreign tourists at all. The Techirghiol salt lake near Eforie, reputed for its therapeutic mud, was one of the stranger and more memorable swim stops of my entire summer.
snippet: Driving south from Constanta through communist-era spa hotels, sunflower fields to the shoreline, and the therapeutic salt mud of Techirghiol lake near Eforie reveals a Black Sea holiday culture that feels completely off the international tourist map.

text: As someone who studies Byzantine and early Christian heritage, the churches and monastery complexes in the countryside around Constanta were as interesting as the city itself. The Dervent Monastery, the cave churches of Basarabi just outside the city, and the Histria ruins on the lagoon north of Constanta — all within an hour's drive — give the Dobrogea region a depth that rewards a dedicated few days rather than a beach stopover.
snippet: The Basarabi cave churches, Dervent Monastery, and the ancient Histria ruins on the lagoon — all within an hour of Constanta — give the Dobrogea region a Byzantine and early Christian heritage depth that rewards dedicated exploration far beyond a simple beach visit.

text: Puerto Vallarta completely won me over. We stayed in Zona Romántica, walked everywhere, took the local bus for 10 pesos a ride, and ate incredible street tacos for almost nothing. Compared to Cabo it feels intimate and immersive — there are galleries, street art, bioluminescent kayak tours, and day trips to Yelapa. The locals were genuinely the most kind and welcoming people I've encountered in any country I've traveled to.
snippet: Puerto Vallarta feels intimate and immersive compared to resort-heavy Cabo — walkable Zona Romántica, incredible street food, and locals who were genuinely the most welcoming I've encountered anywhere.

text: Cape Town is undoubtedly one of the most beautiful cities in the world — Table Mountain, the V&A Waterfront, the peninsula drive through Camps Bay and Cape Point, the penguins at Boulder Beach. It felt relatively safe in the tourist-friendly areas; I spent hours alone browsing the Waterfront and drinking wine, and spending an evening by myself was almost impossible because of how easy it was to meet people. I stayed at a hostel that had a WhatsApp group with planned activities every single day.
snippet: Cape Town is undoubtedly one of the most beautiful cities in the world — Table Mountain, the peninsula drive, the Waterfront — and the social scene made it nearly impossible to spend an evening alone.

text: I came to Cape Town for the wine and left having discovered one of the world's great wine regions essentially on the city's doorstep. A single day driving the Stellenbosch and Franschhoek valleys covered half a dozen excellent estates, and the mountain-backed vineyard landscapes are impossibly beautiful. Returning to Cape Town for a seafood dinner at the harbour with Table Mountain lit above felt like an absurdly luxurious day — and it wasn't that expensive.
snippet: Stellenbosch and Franschhoek vineyards against mountain backdrops in the morning, then a harbour seafood dinner with Table Mountain lit at dusk — Cape Town's wine country access makes it one of the world's most abundantly rewarding city breaks.

text: As a cyclist I found Cape Town almost impossibly good — the route from the city over Chapman's Peak to Hout Bay and back is one of the most scenic road rides I've ever done, and the Sea Point promenade is a 5km flat path along the Atlantic with incredible people-watching. The cycling culture is well established and most coffee shops on the route are set up for it. Just plan rides for clear weather, which requires some flexibility.
snippet: The Chapman's Peak road ride to Hout Bay and back is one of the most scenic cycling routes in the world, and the Sea Point Atlantic promenade delivers 5km of flat riding with extraordinary views — Cape Town's cycling culture is well-established and completely excellent.

text: The food markets in Cape Town are genuinely excellent and worth building days around — the Oranjezicht City Farm Market on weekends is my favourite in Africa, selling craft beer, fresh-caught snoek, artisan cheese, and experimental fusion dishes from a rotating cast of small producers. The afternoon food tour I did through Bo-Kaap, learning the Cape Malay culinary tradition and its Cape Spice history, was the single most satisfying food-focused few hours of my South Africa trip.
snippet: The Oranjezicht City Farm Market is one of the finest weekly food markets in Africa, and a Bo-Kaap Cape Malay culinary walking tour through the Cape Spice history is the most satisfying food afternoon in Cape Town.

text: Robben Island deserves more than the surface-level tour most visitors do — arriving by ferry, walking the cell where Mandela spent 18 of his 27 years in prison, and hearing the guide's own account of the apartheid era creates a weight that stays with you long after leaving. The contrast between that experience and the cafés and wine bars of the city later the same afternoon is jarring and illuminating in a way that feels very true to Cape Town's complexity.
