text: Phoenix really rewards people who get out into the outdoors — the Desert Botanical Garden is arguably the most beautiful spot in the city, and the hiking at Camelback Mountain and South Mountain is excellent. Roosevelt Row is the most vibrant neighbourhood with great restaurants and bars, and the sunsets in the desert are truly unlike anything you've seen before. You absolutely need a car since public transport outside the light rail is limited and everything is spread out.
snippet: Phoenix rewards outdoor explorers — the Desert Botanical Garden is stunning, Camelback Mountain hikes are excellent, and the desert sunsets are truly unlike anything you've seen before.

text: Phoenix in late October hits a sweet spot — temperatures drop into the low 80s, the light is golden all afternoon, and the city starts to reopen after the brutal summer. The Taliesin West campus in Scottsdale, Frank Lloyd Wright's winter home and architecture school, is one of the most thoughtfully designed spaces I've visited in North America, and the guided tour is genuinely illuminating. Pairing a morning there with an afternoon drive up the Apache Trail through the Tonto National Forest makes for a near-perfect desert day.
snippet: Taliesin West in Scottsdale is one of the most thoughtfully designed spaces in North America, and pairing it with an afternoon drive on the Apache Trail through the Tonto National Forest makes for a near-perfect desert day.

text: As a foodie traveler, the dining scene in Phoenix's central neighborhoods surprised me entirely. The stretch of Central Avenue through Uptown and Midtown has excellent James Beard-level restaurants sitting alongside taquerias that have been doing the same four dishes perfectly for thirty years — both worth seeking out. Scottsdale's Old Town adds a lively bar district for evenings, and the craft beer scene at places like Arizona Wilderness Brewing uses ingredients like saguaro fruit and prickly pear in ways that feel genuinely local rather than gimmicky.
snippet: Arizona Wilderness Brewing uses saguaro fruit and prickly pear in ways that feel genuinely local, and the stretch of Central Avenue through Midtown has excellent restaurants sitting alongside taquerias that have been perfecting four dishes for thirty years.

text: The arts infrastructure in Phoenix is far stronger than its reputation suggests — the Phoenix Art Museum is the largest in the Southwest and its rotating contemporary exhibitions can hold their own against any major US city. First Fridays in the Roosevelt Row district fill the streets with local galleries, food trucks, and live music on the first Friday of each month, and it draws a genuinely diverse crowd of locals rather than tourists. The Heard Museum's collection of Native American art and the nearby Musical Instrument Museum are also both exceptional.
snippet: First Fridays in Roosevelt Row draws a genuinely diverse crowd of locals to galleries, food trucks, and live music, and the Heard Museum's Native American art collection is exceptional — Phoenix's cultural scene far exceeds its reputation.

text: For spa travelers, the area around Scottsdale and north Phoenix is one of the best resort destinations in North America — the concentration of high-quality spa properties set against the Sonoran Desert backdrop is simply unmatched. I spent three nights at a smaller boutique resort near Cave Creek and did nothing but hike the surrounding trails in the morning, sit by the pool in the afternoon heat, and eat well each evening. It's the kind of trip that genuinely resets you, and the star-gazing from the desert at night is an unexpected bonus.
snippet: The Scottsdale and Cave Creek area is one of the best spa resort destinations in North America — desert hikes in the morning, pool in the afternoon heat, and star-gazing at night make for a trip that genuinely resets you.

text: Blantyre is a compact and manageable city that serves as a good base for exploring southern Malawi. There's a game park called Game Haven in the city itself that's fun if you like animals. Lake Malawi is the real draw — head to Senga Bay or Mangochi to the south for some of the clearest, most inviting freshwater in Africa. Malawi as a whole is known for having some of the friendliest people on the continent, and that warmth is very much present in Blantyre.
snippet: Blantyre is a good base for southern Malawi — the nearby Lake Malawi has some of the clearest freshwater in Africa, and the country lives up to its reputation for having the friendliest people on the continent.

text: I stayed at the Renaissance in Caracas for four nights and had an absolute blast — there were no incidents whatsoever. The city has improved considerably since 2019, with a noticeable security presence and a flourishing restaurant scene in the east side neighbourhoods of Chacao and Las Mercedes. It's still a city you visit by car rather than on foot, and having a local guide or local contact makes all the difference. The architecture and street art are remarkable.
snippet: Caracas has improved considerably in recent years — the east side neighbourhoods of Chacao and Las Mercedes have a vibrant dining scene, and the modernist architecture and street art are genuinely remarkable.

text: Da Lat is by far the most stunning city I visited in Vietnam — charming cafes, French-inspired architecture, and a pristine natural environment with cool mountain air that feels like a completely different country. The locals were wonderful. I went hiking alone in Da Lat and it turned out to be one of the most peaceful travel experiences I had in Vietnam — the cooler climate, pine woods, and quiet paths make it feel like a different world compared to the coast.
snippet: Da Lat is the most stunning city in Vietnam — French-inspired architecture, cool mountain air, pine forests, and a peaceful charm that feels like a completely different country from the coast.

text: Da Lat rewards travelers who stay long enough to discover the food scene — the city's cool highland climate means it produces nearly all of Vietnam's strawberries, artichokes, and avocados, and the local restaurants use them in ways you won't find on the coast. The night market near Xuan Huong Lake draws locals more than tourists, and the banh mi stalls with fresh avocado are outstanding. Renting a motorbike and riding the countryside to the Elephant Falls and Lang Biang peak makes for an exhilarating full day in the surrounding highlands.
snippet: Da Lat's cool highland climate produces Vietnam's finest strawberries and avocados, and the local night market banh mi stalls using them are outstanding — the city's food scene is as distinctive as its scenery.

text: I just went to New York City for the first time and it was also my first solo vacation — what an incredible experience. I absolutely love bustling cities with strong cultural energy, amazing food, and excellent public transit, and NYC delivered on all of it. I'd rate the trip 9.5/10 and would definitely do it again. Not only did I enjoy the destination, but navigating it independently gave me a fantastic sense of accomplishment and freedom.
snippet: New York City for my first solo trip was a 9.5/10 — the energy, the food, the museums, and the sheer scale of it all delivered completely. Navigating it independently was genuinely exhilarating.

text: Sapa is one of the most interesting and beautiful places I've ever been — the town is perched high in the mountains, surrounded by terraced rice fields and misty valleys, and it's significantly cooler than the rest of Vietnam. The highlight is hiking through the villages and connecting with local ethnic minorities to observe their way of life. I did a two-day trek with a local guide who hosted me overnight, and the scenery was breathtaking with an authentic, peaceful vibe. Avoid just staying in the town centre — venture out onto the trails to find the true beauty.
snippet: Sapa is one of the most beautiful places I've ever visited — terraced rice fields, misty mountain valleys, and a two-day homestay trek with a local guide that was completely authentic and breathtaking.

text: Sapa in September hits the peak of the rice harvest season — the terraced fields around Mu Cang Chai glow gold and amber, and the contrast against the misty mountain backdrop is one of the most beautiful agricultural landscapes I've photographed anywhere in Southeast Asia. Staying in a smaller guesthouse in the Hmong village of Cat Cat rather than in Sapa town itself gave me access to daily life that felt completely unscripted. The market in Bac Ha on Sunday mornings, a two-hour drive away, is one of the most colorful and genuine ethnic minority markets in northern Vietnam.
snippet: Sapa in September during the rice harvest — terraced fields glowing gold and amber against misty mountains — is one of the most beautiful agricultural landscapes in all of Southeast Asia.

text: Guadalajara is such an easy-going city — I've been twice and both times I made no plans but just walked around, met people, and spontaneously did side trips to Tlaquepaque and Tequila. The birthplace of Mariachi has beautiful architecture, a massive three-story market downtown, and the Chapultepec strip is a whole street of bars with a casual, local atmosphere. Tortas ahogadas and birria are absolute musts — don't leave without eating both.
snippet: Guadalajara is easy-going and underrated — beautiful colonial architecture, the birthplace of Mariachi, a fantastic food scene, and a day trip to the Tequila agave farms is genuinely fun and memorable.

text: Anchorage is a gateway rather than a destination in itself — spend as little time there as possible and get into Alaska's nature. The Kenai Peninsula south of Anchorage is extraordinary, and the town of Seward is about 2.5 hours away and absolutely worth a couple of days. Taking the Coastal Classic scenic train from Anchorage to Seward is a great experience in itself. The Flattop Mountain hike near Anchorage is the classic go-to and for good reason.
snippet: Anchorage is best used as a gateway to Alaska's extraordinary nature — the Kenai Peninsula and Seward are breathtaking, and the Coastal Classic train ride south through the mountains is an experience in itself.

text: Bhutan was great — gorgeous scenery, incredibly clean, and unique architecture unlike anywhere else. Overall it's a wonderful country with some of the nicest people I've encountered, with no scammers, no one fighting on the road, and everyone following the rules. The festivals are wonderful to witness if you can time your visit around one. It still feels like the least commercialised destination I've visited, and that sense of genuine cultural preservation is something you feel immediately.
snippet: Bhutan is gorgeous, incredibly clean, and has some of the nicest people I've encountered — it genuinely feels like the world's least commercialised destination, and that cultural preservation is palpable from the moment you arrive.

text: Samarkand was the one city on my Uzbekistan trip where I wish I had more time — you will love it. Crime is practically non-existent, and the people are super friendly and helpful despite the language barrier. At the bazaars, if a shopkeeper goes to pray they just cover their goods with sheets and leave — nobody steals. The Registan is jaw-dropping in real life, even more so than in photos, and the tilework throughout the city is some of the finest Islamic architecture in the world.
snippet: Samarkand was the city I wish I had more time in — the Registan is jaw-dropping in real life, crime is practically nonexistent, and the people are among the most genuinely welcoming I've encountered anywhere.

text: Wellington is probably New Zealand's nicest city — it has a wonderful cafe and arts culture, catch some live music and you'll understand why locals love it. The Te Papa museum is world-class and free, and Mount Victoria gives stunning views over the whole city and harbour in about 20 minutes on foot. It's also a great jumping-off point for the South Island via the Interislander ferry, which is itself a beautiful crossing through the Marlborough Sounds.
snippet: Wellington is New Zealand's most characterful city — world-class free museums, a brilliant cafe culture, and Mount Victoria gives stunning harbour views just a short walk from the city centre.

text: This was a trip like returning home — the Badshahi Mosque at sunset was the highlight, breathtaking and grand in a way that exceeded even my memories and all the photos online. It was really nice walking the winding alleyways of the Walled City early in the morning before the heat hit. The food at the Fort Road Food Street is phenomenal — Haveli with rooftop views of the mosque is a must, and the badam halwa for dessert is extraordinary. Pakistan often gets negative press, but Lahore is basically safe with a rich culture and some of the most generous people I've met.
snippet: The Badshahi Mosque at sunset in Lahore is one of the most breathtaking sights I've ever experienced — the Walled City, the food at Fort Road, and the extraordinary warmth of the people make it a genuinely unforgettable destination.

text: It is undoubtedly very beautiful, other-worldly and almost Mars-like, but for me it lacks life — it seems too dead. The breathtaking altiplanic lagoons, the streets of San Pedro and the sight of flamingos amidst the salt flats left a lasting impression on me. My personal highlights were the Lagunas Altiplanicas and the Geysers del Tatio — only downside is you need to get there super early. If you are into nature and don't mind the dryness, I highly recommend Chile.
snippet: It is undoubtedly very beautiful, other-worldly and almost Mars-like — the altiplanic lagoons and flamingos on the salt flats are truly breathtaking.

text: San Pedro de Atacama is extraordinary for stargazing — the Atacama is the driest desert on earth and sits at high altitude, making it arguably the best place in the world to observe the night sky with the naked eye. Joining a tour to the ALMA telescope complex during the day, then spending the night at one of the observatory camps watching the Milky Way arc overhead in total silence, is a humbling experience I still think about years later. The town itself is small and dusty but well set up for travelers, with good options at various price points.
snippet: The Atacama is arguably the best place on earth to observe the night sky — watching the Milky Way arc overhead in total silence from one of the desert observatory camps is a humbling experience.

text: A couple of days in San Pedro de Atacama during the winter months (June-August) reveals the salt flats and volcanic peaks dusted with snow — a striking contrast to the usual parched orange and white palette. The Valle de la Luna at sunset, when the salt formations turn pink and the shadows lengthen across the dunes, is genuinely one of the most alien-looking landscapes on the planet. Booking the Salar de Uyuni crossing from here into Bolivia also makes logistical sense and adds a spectacular finale to an already dramatic journey.
snippet: The Valle de la Luna at sunset — salt formations turning pink, shadows lengthening across the dunes — is genuinely one of the most alien-looking landscapes on the planet.

text: I was in Madeira for a week and I absolutely LOVED that place — I never wanted to leave. It's a first-world place that is extremely safe, and I once read it has one of the lowest crime rates in the western world. The scenery is staggeringly gorgeous; even after months living there I'd wake up every morning incredulous about how pretty it is. If you are into hiking, biking, or are outdoorsy in any way, Madeira is a paradise.
snippet: I was in Madeira for a week and absolutely LOVED it — I never wanted to leave. The scenery is staggeringly gorgeous and the hiking is world-class.

text: Gjirokastër is a fantastic option for those who want a more laid-back experience in Albania. Stone City Hostel there is wonderful, though the vibe is relaxed rather than party-focused. Besides the castle, check out the Ethnographic Museum — it's located in the former home of famous Albanian writer Ismail Kadare, which adds a really special layer. The old Ottoman architecture and cobblestone streets make this UNESCO-listed city feel genuinely timeless.
snippet: Gjirokastër's old Ottoman architecture and cobblestone streets make it feel genuinely timeless. The castle, the Ethnographic Museum, and the laid-back vibe make it a highlight of any Albania trip.

text: Gjirokastra makes an excellent base for exploring southern Albania's broader landscape — the Blue Eye spring at Syri i Kalter, about 30km away, is an extraordinary natural phenomenon where intensely turquoise water surges up from an underground source of unknown depth. Hiring a driver for a half-day to the Blue Eye and then continuing to the ruins at Butrint, an ancient city layered with Greek, Roman, Byzantine, and Venetian remains, is one of the most culturally rich day trips available anywhere in the Balkans.
snippet: A half-day drive from Gjirokastra to the Blue Eye spring and then to Butrint's layered Greek, Roman, Byzantine, and Venetian ruins is one of the most culturally rich day trips in the Balkans.

text: The hilltop castle above Gjirokastra is genuinely impressive — it contains a captured American spy plane from the Cold War era, which feels completely surreal to encounter inside an Ottoman fortress. Walking the steep stone lanes of the old city early in the morning, when the mist is still hanging over the Drinos Valley below and the slate rooftops catch the first light, is one of those travel moments that photographs poorly but stays with you perfectly. Albania is so far off the typical tourist trail that simply being there feels like a minor adventure.
snippet: Walking Gjirokastra's steep stone lanes at dawn — mist over the Drinos Valley, slate rooftops catching the first light — is one of those moments that photographs poorly but stays with you perfectly.

text: For travelers interested in Communist-era history, Gjirokastra offers a uniquely layered perspective — Enver Hoxha, Albania's longtime dictator, was born here, and the tension between that legacy and the city's Ottoman grandeur is palpable throughout. One of the concrete bunkers that Hoxha famously scattered across Albania in their hundreds of thousands has been converted into a small bunker museum just outside town. The combination of Ottoman architecture, Socialist realism, and ancient ruins within a few kilometers makes Gjirokastra unlike any other small city in Europe.
snippet: The tension between Gjirokastra's Ottoman grandeur and its status as the birthplace of dictator Enver Hoxha — with a converted bunker museum nearby — makes it unlike any other small city in Europe.

text: I visited Gjirokastra in late spring and found the combination of blooming wildflowers in the surrounding hillsides and the grey stone city creating a genuinely beautiful contrast. The local qofte (grilled meatballs) and tavë kosi (baked lamb with yogurt) at the small family restaurants in the bazaar area were some of the most satisfying meals I had in Albania. The pace of life is completely unhurried, and spending a couple of days here between the Adriatic coast and Tirana felt like a perfect calibration of the country's different characters.
snippet: The small family restaurants in Gjirokastra's bazaar area serve some of the most satisfying tavë kosi and qofte in Albania, and the completely unhurried pace of life here is itself a reason to visit.

text: Yeah the city itself is fairly quiet but it's got great stuff to do in every direction — that's definitely its strength. North Stradbroke Island is absolutely beautiful and well worth the trip, especially to cool off in the water. Plus you can visit Australia Zoo! It's a nice place to spend a few days relaxing, and the main beach experiences along the east coast are very accessible from here.
snippet: Brisbane has great stuff to do in every direction — that's definitely its strength. North Stradbroke Island is absolutely beautiful and well worth the trip.

text: Brisbane's South Bank precinct is where the city truly comes alive — a former Expo 88 site transformed into a riverside parkland with a man-made beach, excellent contemporary art museum (QAGOMA), and a food market on weekends that draws a genuinely enthusiastic local crowd. The Gallery of Modern Art is free and consistently has some of the most engaging exhibitions in Australia. Walking across the Goodwill Bridge at sunset with the city skyline reflecting in the Brisbane River is a simple pleasure that the city delivers effortlessly.
snippet: The Gallery of Modern Art at South Bank is free and consistently delivers some of Australia's most engaging exhibitions — and the Goodwill Bridge sunset walk with the city reflected in the Brisbane River is a simple pleasure the city delivers effortlessly.

text: As a wildlife traveler, Brisbane's position between two extraordinary nature reserves sets it apart from every other major Australian city. Moreton Bay's dugong population and the whale migration that passes close to shore between June and November are accessible on day trips from the CBD. The nearby Lamington National Park in the Gold Coast hinterland has ancient Antarctic beech forest and some of the best birdwatching on the continent — adding it to a Brisbane trip takes only a short drive and elevates the visit considerably.
snippet: Moreton Bay's dugongs and whale migration, plus the ancient Antarctic beech forest of Lamington National Park, make Brisbane a stronger wildlife destination than any other major Australian city.

text: The food and bar scene in Fortitude Valley and the inner suburbs has evolved impressively over the past decade. James Street in Fortitude Valley has developed into a genuinely sophisticated dining and shopping strip with restaurants drawing on Brisbane's diverse Asian communities alongside strong modern Australian kitchens. The weekly Eat Street markets at Hamilton draw enormous local crowds and offer an affordable, high-energy snapshot of the city's multicultural food culture in a waterfront setting.
snippet: James Street in Fortitude Valley has evolved into a genuinely sophisticated dining strip, and the Eat Street markets at Hamilton offer an affordable snapshot of Brisbane's multicultural food culture in a lively waterfront setting.

text: Brisbane works extremely well as a base for the Sunshine Coast, which is consistently underrated even by Australians — the Noosa headland hike, the Glass House Mountains, and the hinterland villages around Montville are all within 90 minutes of the CBD. Unlike the Gold Coast to the south, the Sunshine Coast has a relaxed, unhurried quality that suits families and couples looking for uncrowded beaches without sacrificing good food and coffee. Splitting a trip between a few nights in Brisbane and a few nights in Noosa is one of Queensland's best travel formulas.
snippet: Noosa headland, the Glass House Mountains, and the hinterland villages near Montville are all within 90 minutes of Brisbane — and splitting a trip between the city and Noosa is one of Queensland's best travel formulas.

text: Český Krumlov is a UNESCO World Heritage site situated in the South Bohemian Region of the Czech Republic, around 85 miles south of Prague. It's very compact — mostly just one street — and you can see everything in a single day, though it's also a beautiful spot to unwind. Waking up to the sound of the nearby tower bell is a lovely experience, and rafting on the Vltava River through the town is something you shouldn't miss. I stayed for two nights, and while one day is enough for the sights, the peaceful atmosphere makes you want to linger.
snippet: Český Krumlov is a stunning UNESCO town you can see in a day, but the peaceful cobblestone atmosphere makes you want to linger. Don't miss rafting on the Vltava River right through town.

text: I really liked the 'rail and sail' excursion from Basseterre — you do a train trip around the island, then go on a catamaran, which was a fantastic way to see Saint Kitts. We also did a fishing trip with the kids where we caught some red snapper, and visited the hot mineral baths and gardens. The island is small and friendly, and there are options for every type of traveler from resort days to the challenging caldera hike. Overall we were very pleased with the experience.
snippet: The 'rail and sail' excursion from Basseterre — a train trip around the island followed by a catamaran — is a fantastic way to see Saint Kitts and not to be missed.

text: Brimstone Hill Fortress National Park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site perched on the western side of Saint Kitts, is one of the best-preserved colonial military structures in the Caribbean and far more impressive than most visitors expect. The hilltop views from the ramparts on a clear day encompass five neighboring islands simultaneously. After the fortress, the drive down the southern peninsula to the twin-beach strip at Frigate Bay — Atlantic on one side, Caribbean on the other — is one of the Caribbean's more dramatic coastal roads.
snippet: Brimstone Hill Fortress is one of the best-preserved colonial military structures in the Caribbean, and on a clear day the ramparts offer views across five neighboring islands simultaneously.

text: Saint Kitts impressed me most as a destination for hikers — the Southeast Peninsula trails offer dramatic ridge walking above turquoise bays, and the crater hike up Mount Liamuiga through the rainforest to the rim of an extinct volcano is one of the most rewarding full-day hikes in the Eastern Caribbean. A local guide is strongly recommended and makes a significant difference in both safety and ecological commentary. The hike ends with a descent through dense tropical forest back to the northern coast, which feels like a genuine achievement.
snippet: The crater hike up Mount Liamuiga through rainforest to an extinct volcano rim is one of the most rewarding full-day hikes in the Eastern Caribbean — a local guide makes all the difference.

text: For history travelers, Saint Kitts carries an extraordinary amount of colonial and plantation history in a very small island. Several former sugar plantation great houses have been converted into boutique hotels and restaurants — dining at one of them, surrounded by stone estate ruins and sweeping views down to the sea, gives a very different perspective on Caribbean tourism. The National Museum in Basseterre and the Romney Manor estate with its batik craft workshop fill out a rich cultural day without ever leaving the island.
snippet: Several former sugar plantation great houses converted into boutique restaurants give a very different perspective on Caribbean tourism — dining among stone estate ruins with views to the sea is genuinely evocative.

text: Nevis, just a short ferry crossing from Basseterre, is worth at least a full day and ideally an overnight stay for anyone who makes it to Saint Kitts. The island is quieter, greener, and has a preserved colonial atmosphere centered around Charlestown and the Four Seasons resort. The Nevis Peak hike through monkey-inhabited forest to cloud level is a challenging morning, and the afternoon gin fizz at the historic Bath Hotel — once one of the Caribbean's most fashionable watering holes — is a perfect close to the day.
snippet: Nevis, a short ferry from Basseterre, rewards an overnight stay — the Nevis Peak hike through monkey-inhabited cloud forest followed by a gin fizz at the historic Bath Hotel is a near-perfect Caribbean day.

text: I went to Honiara last year for a week — fundamentally it's a developing nation with its challenges, and I was surprised how much rubbish was lining the streets. The accommodation was nice but quite expensive for what you get. That said, the people I met were extremely polite and respectful, and I got a lot of great stories out of it. Out in the provinces you can find really magical places — you feel like you're in a movie on one of those remote tropical islands.
snippet: Honiara is a developing capital with real challenges, but the people are incredibly welcoming and the remote provinces offer magical, almost movie-like tropical islands that few travelers ever see.

text: Honiara is one of the most significant WWII dive sites in the Pacific — Iron Bottom Sound, the strait between Guadalcanal and Florida Island, holds the wrecks of dozens of American and Japanese warships and aircraft from the 1942-43 campaign. Diving on the USS Aaron Ward or the Hirokawa Maru transport ship, with coral now encrusting every surface and reef fish drifting through the hulls, is a haunting and beautiful experience available to advanced divers willing to make the journey. The Guadalcanal battlefield memorials on shore add profound context.
snippet: Iron Bottom Sound holds dozens of WWII warship wrecks that are now coral-encrusted dive sites — diving the Guadalcanal campaign wrecks and then visiting the battlefield memorials on shore is a haunting, unforgettable combination.

text: The Honiara Central Market is the most vivid sensory experience the capital offers — vendors from across Guadalcanal and the neighboring islands sell betel nut, fresh fish, root vegetables, and shell jewelry in a chaotic and genuinely lively atmosphere. The market runs every day from early morning but is most active on weekends, when inter-island boats arrive and trade picks up considerably. Wandering it without any agenda — and accepting the inevitable curiosity of vendors who see very few Western visitors — gave me some of my most memorable Solomon Islands interactions.
snippet: Honiara's Central Market is the city's most vivid sensory experience — vendors from across the islands, fresh fish, betel nut, shell jewelry, and the genuine curiosity of vendors who see very few Western visitors.

text: For cultural travelers, the Solomon Islands Cultural Centre and National Museum in Honiara is a genuinely worthwhile stop — the traditional carvings, shell money, and ceremonial objects give real depth to what can otherwise feel like an opaque destination. The Bongi Bongi Arts & Crafts shop near the museum sells authentic local carvings directly from island artisans at fair prices. Understanding even a little of the kastom traditions of the different island groups transforms how you see the rest of your time in the country.
snippet: The Solomon Islands Cultural Centre in Honiara — traditional carvings, shell money, ceremonial objects — gives real depth to a destination that can feel opaque without some cultural context.

text: Getting out of Honiara to the western provinces transforms the Solomon Islands experience entirely. The Marovo Lagoon in Western Province is one of the largest lagoons in the world and a candidate for UNESCO World Heritage status — the coral gardens, dolphin pods, and traditional fishing villages accessible by longboat are genuinely extraordinary. Flying there from Honiara takes less than an hour on one of the small island-hopping planes, and the contrast between the capital's gritty energy and the lagoon's pristine serenity is striking.
snippet: The Marovo Lagoon — one of the world's largest, with extraordinary coral gardens and traditional villages — is just an hour's island-hopping flight from Honiara and transforms the entire Solomon Islands experience.

text: We love basing ourselves in Siena — after spending a day in Florence I couldn't stand the overwhelming crowds there, so I'm grateful we chose Siena as our home base. I found it charming, less congested, and just perfect. The main piazza is fantastic for families — just grab a seat at a bar along the edge and watch life unfold. I like to do day trips but come back in the evenings to walk to dinner and drink wine.
snippet: Siena is charming, far less congested than Florence, and just perfect as a Tuscan base. The main piazza is one of the most beautiful public squares in Italy.

text: La Palma is a beautiful island not really known for its beaches but more for its volcanic and forested interior. Taburiente is the massive caldera on the island which is great to hike or camp in — hiking the rim gives you a stunning view of the other Canary Islands, and the night sky is also phenomenal thanks to their famous observatory. The people on La Palma (palmeros) are known to be very kind and hospitable, and I can attest to that. I drove all over the island and it was one of my favorite trips ever.
snippet: La Palma is a beautiful island renowned for its volcanic interior and the Taburiente caldera. The night sky is phenomenal — there's a reason they built a world-famous observatory there.

text: Ghent has a much better soul than Bruges — what I love is that it feels like a 'living' city rather than a museum. It's incredibly charming — quiet yet vibrant, with lovely food, lovely people, and amazing architecture along the Graslei waterfront. I used Ghent as a base to visit Bruges and Antwerp, and Ghent itself kept surprising me with its energy. It was a really great way to kickstart solo travel in Belgium.
snippet: Ghent has a much better soul than Bruges — it feels like a living city rather than a museum, with beautiful canal-side architecture and a genuinely vibrant food and culture scene.

text: São Paulo is fantastic — I loved the city's open-minded atmosphere and would gladly return. Having visited both New York and Mexico City, I was still taken aback by the sheer scale of São Paulo, surrounded entirely by towering skyscrapers. The diverse culinary offerings were a highlight, from feijoada to Japanese cuisine — it has the largest Japanese diaspora outside Japan. If you appreciate an urban environment and cultural experiences without focusing purely on scenic landscapes, this destination is perfect for you.
snippet: São Paulo is enormous and genuinely cosmopolitan — the diverse food scene is world-class, and the city's open-minded, round-the-clock energy is unlike anywhere else in South America.

text: We loved Monteverde — being up in the mountains with a cooler climate was a really nice change of pace from the rest of Costa Rica. We did the cloud forest and a night tour during our full day and both were incredible. The ziplines are absolutely worth it — I did Selvatura and the Tarzan swing, and I was in awe. There are also good restaurants and beautiful scenery, and if you're not up for a hike you can do a chocolate and coffee tour.
snippet: The cloud forest, ziplines, and night tours in Monteverde are absolutely worth it — the cooler mountain climate and lush scenery make it a perfect contrast to the rest of Costa Rica.

text: Monteverde in February sits in the dry season, and the cloud forest is at its most mystical — the mist rolls through the Strangler Fig and giant oak canopy all morning, and the birdwatching at that time of year is outstanding, with resplendent quetzals reliably present near the forest edges. I hired a local naturalist guide for an early-morning bird walk through the Santa Elena Reserve, which is less visited than the main Monteverde Reserve, and saw more wildlife in three hours than in a full day at some other Costa Rican parks.
snippet: A dawn bird walk through Santa Elena Reserve in February with a local naturalist guide — resplendent quetzals in the mist, cloud forest canopy overhead — is among the best wildlife experiences Costa Rica offers.

text: The small farming community around Monteverde has a fascinating Quaker heritage — American conscientious objectors settled here in the 1950s and established the dairy farming tradition that still defines the area. The Monteverde Cheese Factory does free tours where you can watch the production process and taste the local goods, and the broader story of how a pacifist community helped protect one of Central America's most important cloud forests is genuinely moving. It adds a layer of human history to the trip that most visitors completely miss.
snippet: The Monteverde Cheese Factory tells the story of the Quaker conscientious objectors who settled here in the 1950s and helped protect one of Central America's most important cloud forests — a layer of human history most visitors completely miss.

text: Monteverde works particularly well as a couples' destination because the pace and variety of activities suit different comfort levels simultaneously. One of us hiked the hanging bridges trail through the forest canopy while the other did a butterfly garden and orchid exhibit in the same hour — we reconvened for lunch at a small café overlooking the Gulf of Nicoya far below and barely needed to compromise. The views across the Gulf that open up from the high ground on clear afternoons are a surprise bonus that the cloud forest reputation doesn't prepare you for.
snippet: On clear afternoons, the views across the Gulf of Nicoya that open up from Monteverde's high ground are a complete surprise — the cloud forest reputation doesn't prepare you for that sweeping coastal panorama.

text: Traveling to Monteverde as a family with school-age children was one of our best Costa Rica decisions. The frog pond in Santa Elena has a guided evening tour that keeps kids completely riveted for 90 minutes — seeing red-eyed tree frogs, glass frogs, and poison dart frogs at close range with a headlamp is the kind of thing children talk about for years. The serpentarium and hummingbird garden nearby are also excellent and require very little hiking, making them accessible for younger or less mobile travelers.
snippet: The Santa Elena frog pond's guided evening tour — red-eyed tree frogs, glass frogs, poison dart frogs at close range in the dark — is the kind of experience children talk about for years.

text: A full island tour from Nukuʻalofa can take a good 4–5 hours and covers the main sites — Captain Cook's landing, Ha'amonga (the Tongan Stonehenge), the Hufangalupe lookout, blowholes, and tsunami rock. Most services in Tonga are organized through Facebook rather than websites, which gives the whole place a wonderfully off-the-beaten-path feel. I'd also recommend taking the boat to Pangaimotu island just off the coast of Nukuʻalofa — the beach is nice for snorkeling and the sunset is gorgeous. Stick to the tourist sites and you'll find a safe and memorable experience.
snippet: Nukuʻalofa is charmingly off the beaten path — a full island tour covering Ha'amonga, the blowholes, and the coast is a fantastic way to spend a day before hopping a boat to Pangaimotu for snorkeling at sunset.

text: Nuku'alofa is one of the only places in the world where you can reliably swim with humpback whales in the open ocean — they arrive in Tongan waters between July and October to breed and nurse their calves, and the experience of entering the water beside a 40-tonne whale and her calf is genuinely life-altering. Small-group operators run strict encounter protocols that prioritize whale welfare, and the combination of the encounter and the spectacular blue water of the Tonga Trench makes the journey entirely worthwhile.
snippet: Swimming with humpback whales in Tongan waters between July and October — entering the ocean beside a 40-tonne mother and her calf — is one of the most life-altering wildlife encounters available anywhere on earth.

text: The Royal Palace in Nuku'alofa, a white Victorian-era wooden building visible from the waterfront, and the nearby Royal Tombs are among the most quietly affecting royal sites in the Pacific — Tonga is the only Pacific nation that maintained its monarchy through the colonial era. Sunday in Tonga shuts almost everything down for a day of church and family, and walking through the neighbourhoods hearing congregational hymns drifting from open church windows is an unexpectedly moving experience. Respecting that culture is essential and rewarding.
snippet: Walking through Nuku'alofa's neighbourhoods on a Sunday — congregational hymns drifting from open church windows in every direction — is an unexpectedly moving cultural experience.

text: The 'Eua Island, accessible by ferry from Nuku'alofa in about two hours, is the most underrated destination in the Tongan archipelago. 'Eua has genuine rainforest, dramatic sea cliffs, and cave systems that see almost no visitors — the hiking trails there are the best in Tonga and the birding, including the endangered Tongan megapode, is exceptional. A night or two on 'Eua adds a completely different dimension to a Tonga trip and rewards travelers willing to accept very basic accommodation.
snippet: 'Eua Island — two hours by ferry from Nuku'alofa, with rainforest, dramatic sea cliffs, and almost no visitors — is the most underrated destination in the Tongan archipelago.

text: Tonga's food culture deserves more attention than it typically receives from travelers. The 'umu, the underground earth oven, is the traditional cooking method and the meals that come out of it — slow-cooked pork, taro, breadfruit, and coconut — are deeply satisfying and quite unlike the food culture of neighboring Polynesian nations. Several guesthouses in Nuku'alofa offer 'umu dinners on request with enough advance notice, and sitting down to share one with a Tongan family is the warmest hospitality the islands offer.
snippet: The Tongan 'umu — slow-cooked pork, taro, and breadfruit from an underground earth oven — is deeply satisfying and unlike any other Polynesian food tradition; sharing one with a local family is the warmest hospitality Tonga offers.

text: Birmingham has some impressive things going for it — the canals around Brindley Place are lovely, the Jewellery Quarter is fascinating, and the Birmingham Museum and Art Gallery is world-class. The city centre is quite walkable, and Digbeth is a cool neighborhood with trendy pubs and warehouse parties. Broad Street is bustling with bars and clubs for nightlife. I'd spend a day wandering the city centre and the canals and then explore the areas beyond — there's more here than most people expect.
snippet: Birmingham has more going for it than its reputation suggests — the canals around Brindley Place, the Jewellery Quarter, and the vibrant Digbeth arts scene are genuinely worth exploring.

text: Tuscany is one of the greatest regions in the world for wine, and I couldn't recommend getting out and experiencing it enough. I would suggest finding an Agriturismo in the countryside to truly experience the local food and way of life. Cycling in the Val d'Orcia — a UNESCO landscape — is something I'll never forget, and San Gimignano and Montepulciano are incredibly charming towns to wander. The factory outlets near Florence for Gucci, Prada, and other Italian brands are also a surprisingly fun stop.
snippet: Tuscany is one of the world's greatest regions for wine, food, and landscapes. Staying at a countryside agriturismo and cycling through the Val d'Orcia are experiences that will stay with you forever.

text: I was absolutely amazed by how lovely St. Louis is — at least the parts I experienced. The zoo is not just one of the best in the country, it's honestly one of the best in the world, and the fact that it's completely free is just incredible. Central West End was buzzing with great breakfast spots, and the light rail is clean, quiet, and cheap. I'd definitely recommend the City Museum and the Missouri Botanical Gardens too.
snippet: St. Louis is genuinely underrated — the free world-class zoo, the City Museum, and the lovely Central West End neighborhood make it one of the most rewarding surprise cities in America.

text: Just got back from Vegas and it was absolutely perfect — down to the last minute detail. Great food, great hotels, amazing attractions all within a few blocks of each other. Three days felt like the perfect amount of time, and I appreciated that even losing a little gambling felt like part of the experience rather than a disaster. The strip at night is unlike anything else, and Fremont Street has a totally different, grittier energy worth exploring.
snippet: Las Vegas delivers on every level — incredible food, stunning hotels, and non-stop entertainment all within a few blocks. Three days is the sweet spot for soaking it all in without burning out.

text: Detroit is the most underrated city for tourism in the United States. The culinary landscape is truly remarkable — from Coney dogs and Detroit-style square pizza to James Beard-celebrated chefs and some of the finest Middle Eastern and Mexican food anywhere. The Detroit Institute of Arts and the Henry Ford Museum in Dearborn are world-class, and the stunning Gothic Revival architecture downtown will stop you in your tracks. Having previously visited as a tourist, I was genuinely surprised by the city's charm and vibrancy.
snippet: Detroit is the most underrated city for tourism in the US — world-class art, stunning architecture, an incredible food scene, and a Riverwalk that shows just how much this city has transformed.

text: Many people visit Suzhou as a day trip from Shanghai, but it's actually worth exploring for several days on its own. The classical Chinese gardens are the headline draw, but Suzhou is also famous for Taihu Lake and its tea culture — there's much more than the standard tourist circuit. It's known as the 'Venice of the East' for its canals, and while the comparison is imperfect, wandering the old waterways at dusk has a genuinely magical quality. The hairy crabs in season are also a must-eat local delicacy.
snippet: Suzhou is worth more than the typical day trip from Shanghai — the UNESCO classical gardens are stunning, and wandering the old canals at dusk has a genuinely magical quality all its own.

text: Sardinia is stunning — the water is unreal and the scenery absolutely delivers. From Cagliari westwards along the coast is delightful, with amazing beaches that aren't too crowded, and the entire west coast is wild and lovely. Many hotels can be expensive for what they offer in high season, so booking strategically makes a big difference. But the natural beauty — those impossible turquoise waters and rugged interior landscapes — makes it worth every effort to get there.
snippet: Sardinia is stunning — the water is unreal and the west coast beaches are wild and uncrowded. It's one of Italy's most beautiful and underappreciated destinations.

text: Sardinia rewards hikers with one of Europe's most dramatic coastal trails — the Selvaggio Blu, a multi-day trekking route along the Gulf of Orosei, is considered one of the hardest and most beautiful treks on the continent. Even doing a single day section of the trail from Cala Gonone with a guide reveals limestone gorges, sea caves, and secret beaches accessible only on foot. The interior Barbagia region, meanwhile, is a completely different world of ancient villages where traditional Nuragic culture still has deep roots.
snippet: Even a single guided day on Sardinia's Selvaggio Blu coastal trail reveals limestone gorges, sea caves, and beaches accessible only on foot — and the interior Barbagia villages offer a completely different ancient world.

text: Arriving in Sardinia for Carnevale in February — particularly in the village of Mamoiada in the Barbagia highlands — is one of the most extraordinary folk festival experiences in the Mediterranean. The Mamuthones, masked figures in sheepskin and heavy cowbells, parade through the village streets in a ritual that predates Christianity and feels genuinely ancient in a way that most European folklore events no longer do. Sardinia in February is also completely tourist-free and remarkably inexpensive.
snippet: Mamoiada's Carnevale Mamuthones — masked figures in sheepskin and heavy cowbells parading through the streets in a pre-Christian ritual — is one of the most extraordinary folk festival experiences in the Mediterranean.

text: The Nuraghi, the mysterious Bronze Age stone towers scattered across Sardinia's interior, are unlike anything else in Europe and demand at least one focused day of exploration. Su Nuraxi di Barumini, the UNESCO-listed site near the town of Barumini, is the most impressive complex — its central tower dates to around 1500 BC and is surrounded by a later village of circular stone huts. Spending a morning there and then driving the winding roads through Marmilla to a family agriturismo lunch feels like Sardinia at its most authentic.
snippet: Su Nuraxi di Barumini — a Nuragic tower complex dating to 1500 BC, unlike anything else in Europe — followed by lunch at a family agriturismo in the Marmilla countryside is Sardinia at its most authentic.

text: Alghero on the northwestern coast is the most distinctive town in Sardinia — Catalan is still spoken here, the architecture reflects centuries of Aragonese rule, and the medieval walls overlooking the sea have a genuinely Iberian feel that surprises everyone who hasn't heard about it. The nearby Grotte di Nettuno sea caves, accessible by boat from Alghero port or by a dramatic staircase carved into the cliffside, are among the most beautiful caves in Italy. Pairing an afternoon at the caves with aperitivo on Alghero's bastions at sunset is very hard to improve upon.
snippet: Alghero still speaks Catalan and its Aragonese medieval walls have an unmistakably Iberian character — pairing the nearby Grotte di Nettuno sea caves with aperitivo on the bastions at sunset is very hard to improve upon.

text: Vanuatu was a destination I had longed to visit, and it certainly lived up to my expectations. Port Vila was a super chill place, gorgeous, felt very safe, and I highly recommend it — especially for those who like to get off the beaten path. The snorkeling at Hideaway Island was phenomenal with vibrant corals and an abundance of fish, and you can even mail a waterproof postcard from the underwater post office. Getting around by public minibus was easy and cheap, and taxis have fixed rates from the airport so there's no haggling.
snippet: Port Vila is a gorgeous, super-chill capital with phenomenal snorkeling at Hideaway Island — including the world's only underwater post office. It's perfect for travelers who want to get genuinely off the beaten path.

text: Sibiu had some of the most beautiful medieval architecture I've seen anywhere in Europe. The Saint Mary's Evangelical Cathedral was particularly stunning, offering breathtaking views from the top. The people throughout Transylvania were warm, inquisitive, and outgoing — the beer and wine were great too. Two full days felt just right here, and the meal at Spic Paste si Vin with the cathedral view was genuinely one of the best experiences of the whole trip.
snippet: Sibiu has some of the most beautiful medieval architecture in Europe — the Evangelical Cathedral, the Old Town squares, and the warmth of the local people make it a standout highlight of any Romania trip.

text: Sibiu in December, when the Christmas market fills the Grand Square, is one of the most atmospheric winter experiences in Central Europe — consistently ranked among the best Christmas markets on the continent, and far less crowded than Vienna or Prague. The mulled wine, cozonac sweet bread, and langoș stalls run by local families give it an authenticity that the bigger markets have largely lost. The snow on the medieval rooftop eyelid windows and the Lower Town's rooftops makes the city look like an illustration from a fairy tale.
snippet: Sibiu's Christmas market on the Grand Square is one of Europe's most atmospheric — langoș stalls run by local families, medieval rooftop 'eyes' dusted with snow, and none of the overwhelming crowds of Vienna or Prague.

text: The village of Viscri, about 50km northeast of Sibiu through rolling Transylvanian farmland, is one of the most quietly beautiful places I've visited in Romania — a Saxon fortified church, white-washed farmhouses, and horse carts still in daily use. Prince Charles owns a restored guesthouse there and has been instrumental in Saxon village preservation, which has brought sensitive attention without overcrowding. Combining a morning in Sibiu's old town with an afternoon driving the Transylvanian village circuit — Viscri, Biertan, Saschiz — is a near-perfect Romanian day.
snippet: The Transylvanian village circuit from Sibiu — Viscri, Biertan, Saschiz — with their Saxon fortified churches and horse-cart roads is one of the most quietly beautiful drives in Romania.

text: As a solo traveler focused on cuisine, Sibiu's food scene is genuinely excellent for a city its size — the traditional Transylvanian dishes, like ciorbă de fasole (bean soup) and tochitură (pork stew with polenta), are done with real care at restaurants throughout the old town. The weekly Saturday market near the Huet Square draws farmers from surrounding villages and has excellent local cheeses, smoked meats, and wild mushrooms. Paired with a glass of Feteasca Neagra from a local winemaker, it's the kind of market morning that reaffirms why slow travel is worth it.
snippet: Sibiu's Saturday market near Huet Square draws farmers from the surrounding Transylvanian villages with local cheeses, smoked meats, and wild mushrooms — a market morning that reaffirms why slow travel is worth it.

text: Sibiu is an excellent base for the Transylvanian Alps — the Fagaras ridge, one of the most dramatic hiking areas in Eastern Europe, begins less than an hour south of the city. The trail along the main Fagaras crest, including the ascent of Moldoveanu, Romania's highest peak, is serious high-altitude mountain walking that few Western hikers know about. Combining two days of city exploration in Sibiu with a Fagaras day hike makes for a trip that covers medieval culture and genuine mountain wilderness in one compact itinerary.
snippet: The Fagaras ridge — one of Eastern Europe's most dramatic high-altitude hiking areas — begins less than an hour from Sibiu's old town, making the combination of medieval city and serious mountain wilderness one of Romania's best travel formulas.

text: I can't stress it enough — Luxor, Karnak, the Valley of the Kings, and Hatshepsut temple were far more impressive than the Giza Plateau to me. The Valley of the Kings alone gives you a real sense of history and mystery that is hard to describe. I would definitely visit in winter if possible, and a Nile cruise or night bus from Cairo is well worth it. Luxor Temple at night is also spectacular — nowhere else in the country has such a concentration of ancient temples and tombs.
snippet: Luxor surpassed all my expectations — the Valley of the Kings, Karnak Temple, and Hatshepsut's mortuary temple are more impressive than the Giza Plateau. Seeing Luxor Temple illuminated at night is genuinely unforgettable.

text: I loved my trip to Turks and Caicos — we went snorkeling while our guides caught fresh lobster, then grilled it on the beach. It was like a fairytale! TCI is the most expensive place I've ever traveled to — pricier than Iceland or Switzerland — but it doesn't have to break the bank if you rent a car and make your own meals. Stay in the northeast part of the island to be closest to the best beaches and restaurants, and rent a car for the freedom to explore remote stretches of Grace Bay.
snippet: Providenciales is breathtakingly beautiful — the turquoise water and Grace Bay beaches are genuinely as incredible as advertised. Go snorkeling, grill fresh lobster on the beach, and budget carefully — it's expensive but absolutely worth it.

text: Grace Bay Beach has repeatedly been rated the best beach in the world and it earns that reputation honestly — the sand is so fine it squeaks underfoot, the water is a range of blues that shifts as you move through it, and the shelf is shallow enough to wade for 100 metres. What surprised me was how relatively uncrowded it stays even at peak season, largely because the Turks and Caicos's high price point naturally limits visitor numbers. Arriving at dawn before the resort guests appeared felt like having one of the world's great beaches entirely to myself.
snippet: Grace Bay's sand squeaks underfoot and the water cycles through blues that shift as you move through them — and at dawn before the resort guests appear, you have one of the world's great beaches entirely to yourself.

text: For scuba divers and snorkelers, Providenciales is one of the top wall-diving destinations in the Western Hemisphere — the Caicos Wall drops from 15 metres to over 2,000 metres with virtually no transition, and the coral health on the shallower sections is exceptional. The Coral Gardens site near Grace Bay requires no boat — just wade in from the beach and swim out to find nurse sharks resting on the sand and hawksbill turtles grazing the reef. It's the kind of marine experience you'd normally pay a small fortune and a long boat ride to access.
snippet: The Caicos Wall drops from 15 metres to over 2,000 metres with virtually no transition, and the Coral Gardens snorkel site off Grace Bay beach — nurse sharks, hawksbill turtles, no boat required — is one of the Caribbean's great free wildlife experiences.

text: Visiting the conch farm on Providenciales is a fascinating, unexpected hour for anyone curious about the ecology of the islands — the Turks and Caicos is one of the last places where conch remains reasonably abundant, and the farm is working on repopulation efforts while giving you a close look at the full life cycle. Cracked conch, conch fritters, and conch salad are the essential local foods, and the shacks at Bugaloo's on Five Cays give you all three in an unpretentious setting that feels nothing like the resort strip. It's the most authentically Belonger food experience on the island.
snippet: The conch shacks at Bugaloo's on Five Cays — cracked conch, conch fritters, conch salad in an unpretentious local setting — is the most authentically Belonger food experience on Providenciales.

text: Middle Caicos, the largest island in the archipelago and reachable by ferry from Providenciales, offers a dramatically different experience from the resort atmosphere of Provo — a largely unpopulated landscape of tidal creeks, cave systems, and wild beaches that see almost no visitors. The Conch Bar Caves, the largest accessible cave system in the Caribbean, stretch for several kilometres and contain Lucayan Taíno artifacts. Coming back on the afternoon ferry after a full day of exploring empty beaches and caves makes the resort pool feel like a very welcome finish line.
snippet: Middle Caicos — ferry from Providenciales, largely unpopulated, with the Conch Bar Caves (the Caribbean's largest accessible cave system) and wild beaches that see almost no visitors — is the best day trip in the archipelago.

text: Siwa is amazingly beautiful with some of the most exquisite scenery imaginable — it has its own unique culture and architecture that feels completely different from the rest of Egypt. The oasis only got a tarmac road in the 1980s, so a genuinely distinct identity developed here, far removed from Cairo or Luxor. Getting a driver and guide to take you into the Great Sand Sea for a desert camping experience is an absolute must. Siwa is a hotspot for both Egyptian and foreign tourists, and once you've been, you'll understand why.
snippet: Siwa is one of the most exquisite places in Egypt — a true desert oasis with unique architecture, ancient springs, and sand dunes stretching to the horizon. Camping in the Great Sand Sea under the stars is a once-in-a-lifetime experience.

text: Siwa's salt lakes, particularly Fatnas Island on Lake Siwa, offer one of the most surreal swimming experiences I've encountered — the salt concentration is high enough to float effortlessly while looking across a palm-fringed oasis to the dunes of the Great Sand Sea beyond. The best time to visit is October through February when the heat is manageable and the dates are being harvested from the thousands of date palms that define the oasis landscape. Arriving by overnight bus from Marsa Matruh and emerging into that silent, ancient landscape at dawn is a genuinely disorienting and wonderful experience.
snippet: Floating effortlessly in Siwa's Fatnas Island salt lake while looking across palms to the Great Sand Sea dunes beyond is one of the most surreal swimming experiences imaginable.

text: The Oracle Temple of Amun at Siwa is where Alexander the Great came in 331 BC to confirm his divine status — the priests apparently told him what he wanted to hear. Standing in the ruins of that temple at the top of Aghurmi village, looking across the same oasis Alexander would have seen, is one of those moments where history feels genuinely present rather than distant. The adjacent ruins of Aghurmi's mud-brick city, slowly dissolving back into the desert, add an additional layer of poignant impermanence.
snippet: Standing in the Oracle Temple ruins where Alexander the Great came to confirm his divine status — looking across the same oasis he would have seen in 331 BC — is a moment where history feels genuinely present.

text: Siwa is one of the most distinctive Berber communities in the world — the Siwans have their own language, Siwi, their own customs, and a traditional silver jewelry tradition that produces some of the most beautiful work in North Africa. The Siwa House Museum in a restored mud-brick building in the town centre documents this culture with genuine depth, including the unique bridal traditions and the embroidered wedding dresses that can take a year or more to complete. Shopping for silver directly from the families who make it, rather than from the tourist shops, is a far more rewarding experience.
snippet: The Siwa House Museum documents the Siwans' distinct Berber culture — their own language, unique bridal traditions, and extraordinary silver jewelry — with genuine depth that most oasis visitors overlook.

text: For travelers who want to push further into the desert, the White Desert National Park and the Crystal Mountain are within reach of Siwa on a longer overland circuit through the Western Desert. The otherworldly chalk formations of the White Desert, rising like giant mushrooms from the flat sand, are best experienced camping overnight when the full moon turns them silver-white. Crossing the Qattara Depression route between Siwa and Bahariya by 4WD with an experienced driver is an adventurous alternative to the standard highway.
snippet: Camping overnight in the White Desert National Park, reachable on a Siwa overland circuit, and watching the moon turn the chalk formations silver-white is one of Egypt's most extraordinary experiences.

text: I took the overnight train to Mombasa from Nairobi and really enjoyed the city — it has some beautiful beaches, an old Portuguese fort right on the beach, and a super atmospheric old Arab Quarter worth wandering around. Mombasa was actually my favorite part of Kenya. Nature, beaches, and nearby national parks — everything is extremely beautiful and surprisingly pristine. The locals were incredibly polite, and the quality of service was excellent for the price.
snippet: Mombasa was my favorite part of Kenya — the Old Town's Arab Quarter, the Portuguese fort on the beach, and the stunning coastline combine to make it one of East Africa's most rewarding cities.

text: Cusco is the undeniable gateway to Machu Picchu and an ancient Inca capital in its own right — the food is excellent, as is all Peruvian food, and the colonial architecture layered over Inca stonework gives the city a uniquely layered character. Most day tour excursions from Cusco left like clockwork and without a hitch. Give yourself a day or two to acclimatize to the altitude before rushing into activity — the Sacred Valley and surrounding archaeological sites are well worth the extra time.
snippet: Cusco is the perfect base for exploring the Sacred Valley and Machu Picchu, with excellent food and stunning colonial-over-Inca architecture. Give yourself time to acclimatize to the altitude before diving into the day trips.

text: Nara is amazing and a unique, out-of-this-world experience. There were deer everywhere, which was so fun — lots of space and it didn't feel crowded at all. The area around Nara Park is a splendid combination of nature, temples and shrines, and traditional architecture. I walked from the park to Naramachi to explore the shops and narrow streets, then stopped at Nakatanidou for fresh mochi — absolutely phenomenal.
snippet: Nara is a unique, out-of-this-world experience — free-roaming deer, ancient temples, and the Great Buddha Hall make for an unforgettable half-day or day trip from Kyoto or Osaka.

text: Nara in mid-November during the autumn foliage season is one of Japan's most beautiful sights — the maples around Kasuga Grand Shrine turn deep crimson and gold, and the deer wander through the fallen leaves as if completely indifferent to the extraordinary scenery around them. The evening lantern festivals that Kasuga hosts several times a year, when thousands of stone lanterns are lit simultaneously along the paths to the shrine, are even more atmospheric and require advance booking. Staying overnight rather than day-tripping from Kyoto or Osaka means you get the park essentially to yourself at dusk.
snippet: Kasuga Grand Shrine's lantern festivals — thousands of stone lanterns lit simultaneously along the forest paths — are among Japan's most atmospheric evening experiences, and staying overnight in Nara means you get the park to yourself at dusk.

text: Beyond the famous deer, Nara holds some of Japan's most important Buddhist treasures — the Tōdai-ji's Great Buddha Hall is the largest wooden building in the world, and the Daibutsu within it, at 15 metres high, retains a physical presence that photographs simply cannot convey. The smaller temple of Tōshōdai-ji, founded in 759 by the Chinese monk Jianzhen and a UNESCO site, is one of the most perfectly preserved examples of Nara-period architecture surviving in Japan. Architecture enthusiasts will find the walking circuit between these temples one of the most rewarding half-days in the country.
snippet: The Tōdai-ji Great Buddha Hall is the largest wooden building in the world, and the 15-metre Daibutsu inside has a physical presence that photographs simply cannot convey.

text: Nara is an excellent destination for craft and design travelers — the city has a long tradition of calligraphy brush-making, ink production, and lacquerware that has been going since the imperial capital period. Several ateliers in the Naramachi merchant district still practice these crafts and offer workshops for visitors willing to look beyond the deer-bowing souvenir shops. Spending a morning trying calligraphy with proper inkstone and brush in a centuries-old studio is the kind of unhurried cultural experience that Japan does better than anywhere else.
snippet: Naramachi's surviving calligraphy and lacquerware ateliers offer workshops in crafts that have been practiced here since the imperial capital period — unhurried, hands-on, and completely off the deer-photo tourist trail.

text: Buenos Aires is such an eclectic city with amazing and wide-ranging architecture, a great café culture and food, and it's just very functional — easy to get around and felt safe throughout. I spent time in Palermo, Recoleta, San Telmo, and El Centro, walking from barrio to barrio after dark without ever feeling worried. Palermo is a charming area with quiet, tree-lined streets and great cafés and restaurants. The Argentinian people are very warm, friendly, and helpful — there was just a good energy here.
snippet: Buenos Aires is an eclectic, walkable city with beautiful architecture, amazing food, and a warmth and energy unlike anywhere else in South America. The Recoleta Cemetery, Teatro Colón, and the neighborhood of Palermo are absolute highlights.

text: Córdoba is a university city with a lot of youth coming from all over the country, great bars, and good nightlife — especially around downtown and the Nueva Córdoba neighborhood. The Cordobeses (locals) are some of the nicest people in Argentina, so expect to have a lot of fun if you interact with them. La Manzana Jesuítica (the Jesuit block in downtown) is a fascinating UNESCO site, and the nearby Sierras Chicas mountains are worth a day trip. I recommend spending more time exploring outside the city itself to find the stunning countryside around Calamuchita and Traslasierra.
snippet: Córdoba has great nightlife, charming Jesuit colonial architecture, and some of the friendliest people in Argentina. Use it as a springboard into the surrounding sierra countryside — that's where the real magic is.

text: Córdoba's culinary culture revolves around the asado tradition taken to real heights — the parrillas in the Villa General Belgrano area and throughout the Calamuchita valley smoke beef ribs and chorizo over quebracho wood embers in a way that makes a Buenos Aires parilla feel rushed by comparison. The regional specialty of empanadas cordobesas, with their distinctive fried pastry and filling of egg, olives, and ground beef, are worth seeking out at the local panaderías rather than the tourist-facing restaurants on the main square.
snippet: The fried empanadas cordobesas — egg, olives, and ground beef in a distinctive pastry — found at local panaderías are one of Argentina's most underrated regional foods.

text: As a student of modernist architecture, I spent a day in Córdoba focused entirely on its 20th-century built heritage, which gets virtually no attention compared to the Jesuit block. The Ciudad Universitaria campus, designed in the 1950s, has several remarkable rationalist buildings, and the Teatro del Libertador General San Martín in the city centre is one of the most beautiful opera houses in South America. Walking the Nueva Córdoba neighbourhood reveals a dense concentration of eclectic early 20th-century apartment buildings that give the barrio its distinctive Buenos Aires-adjacent character.
snippet: Córdoba's Teatro del Libertador is one of the most beautiful opera houses in South America, and the Nueva Córdoba neighbourhood's dense eclectic apartment buildings give it a distinctly sophisticated urban character.

text: The Punilla Valley north of Córdoba city — particularly the stretch between Villa Carlos Paz and La Cumbre — is a popular Argentine summer retreat that foreign travelers almost never discover. The village of La Cumbre has an extraordinary concentration of English Tudor-style houses built by British railway engineers in the early 20th century, a paragliding launch site with spectacular Andean foothills views, and a surprisingly sophisticated dining scene built around locally sourced trout. Combining a day there with a visit to the Jesuit estancias at Alta Gracia is a day that rewards getting a rental car.
snippet: La Cumbre in the Punilla Valley — Tudor houses built by British railway engineers, a paragliding launch site with Andean views, and locally sourced trout — is a day trip from Córdoba that foreign travelers almost never discover.

text: Córdoba's nightlife operates on a timeline that confuses many visitors — dinner before 10pm marks you as a tourist, and the bars in Güemes and General Paz don't properly fill until well after midnight. The cumbia villera and cuarteto music scenes centered around these neighbourhoods are distinctly Cordobese and a very different experience from Buenos Aires tango tourism. Spending a weekend here in festival season, when the city's student population swells and the parks along the Suquía River become open-air social venues from Friday to Sunday, shows the city at its most exuberant.
snippet: Córdoba's cuarteto and cumbia villera music scene in the Güemes and General Paz neighbourhoods — bars that don't fill until well after midnight — is a distinctly Cordobese nightlife experience very different from Buenos Aires.

text: Sydney is an amazing city — lots to do, places to go, with excellent cuisine. It's a beautiful city, very walkable and vibrant, and even with a day trip to the Blue Mountains you can see the highlights in about four or five days. The harbourside setting with the Opera House and Harbour Bridge is simply iconic, and the beach culture across Bondi, Manly, and beyond is the best in the world. Totally worth the long flight.
snippet: Sydney is a beautiful, walkable, and vibrant city with an iconic harbourside setting. The combination of world-class beaches, great food, and the Blue Mountains day trip makes it well worth the trip.

text: The BridgeClimb experience over the Sydney Harbour Bridge is one of those touristy things that fully justifies its reputation and its price — three hours at the top of an arched steel span 134 metres above the harbour, watching container ships and ferries move below while the city skyline recedes into the afternoon haze, is a perspective on Sydney available no other way. Booking the twilight climb to watch the sun drop behind the Blue Mountains in the west while the city lights come on below is the version worth paying the premium for.
snippet: The Sydney BridgeClimb twilight option — watching the sun drop behind the Blue Mountains while the city lights come on below you — is a perspective on the city available no other way.

text: Sydney's inner west neighbourhoods — Newtown, Marrickville, and Petersham — offer a completely different city from the harbourside postcard. Newtown's King Street is one of Australia's most genuinely eclectic strips, with vegan cafes, vintage clothing shops, independent bookstores, and drag bars sitting side by side in Victorian terrace buildings. The Lebanese bakeries in Petersham open early and the weekend dumpling houses in Marrickville draw queues of locals who know the quality matches anything in Chinatown at considerably lower prices.
snippet: Newtown's King Street is one of Australia's most genuinely eclectic strips — vegan cafes, vintage shops, and drag bars in Victorian terraces — and the Lebanese bakeries in Petersham and dumpling houses in Marrickville are the city's most underrated food destinations.

text: Sydney's ferry network is arguably its single best feature as a city for visitors — for the price of a transit ticket, you can glide past the Opera House and Harbour Bridge, through the sandstone coves of Middle Harbour, out to the zoo on Taronga's waterfront, or all the way to Manly with its surf beach and coastal walking trail. The Manly to Spit Bridge track, a 10km walk through native bushland that drops repeatedly to hidden harbour beaches, is Sydney's best free outdoor experience and virtually unknown to most international visitors.
snippet: The Manly to Spit Bridge coastal walk — 10km through native bushland dropping to hidden harbour beaches, starting from a ferry — is Sydney's best free outdoor experience and virtually unknown to most international visitors.

text: Visiting Sydney during the Vivid Light festival in late May and June transforms the harbour into something extraordinary — the Opera House sails, Harbour Bridge, and Botanical Garden are all mapped with large-scale projections and light installations that draw enormous crowds but remain thoroughly worth attending. The winter timing also means mild days and almost no crowds at the beaches, making it possible to have Coogee or Maroubra to yourself in the morning before heading back for the evening light shows. June in Sydney is genuinely one of the best times to visit.
snippet: Sydney's Vivid Light festival in late May and June transforms the Opera House and harbour with large-scale projections, while mild winter days mean the beaches are virtually empty — June is genuinely one of the best months to visit.

text: Despite Taroko Gorge being partially closed after the 2024 earthquake, Hualien still has some nice spots to visit. I spent a few hours hiking through the jungle, watching the massive Pacific Ocean waves, visiting Hualien Temple, and savoring a tranquil lotus tea at a lotus farm. The east coast scenery — mountains plunging straight into the Pacific — is unlike anything else in Taiwan. Check current conditions for Taroko before you go, as access continues to improve over time.
snippet: Hualien's east coast scenery — mountains plunging straight into the Pacific — is unlike anything else in Taiwan. The Pacific Ocean waves, jungle hikes, and lotus farms make it a beautiful destination even beyond Taroko Gorge.

text: Hualien's night market is one of the most enjoyable in eastern Taiwan — smaller and less overwhelming than Taipei's famous markets, it has excellent local specialties including scallion pancakes, stinky tofu done at a quality level that converts skeptics, and a papaya milk stand that draws a queue every evening without fail. The city has a genuinely relaxed pace compared to western Taiwan, and renting a bicycle to ride the coastal path south toward Qixingtan beach — where black volcanic pebbles and dramatic waves replace the sand beaches of the west — is a perfect easy morning.
snippet: Qixingtan beach south of Hualien, where black volcanic pebbles meet dramatic Pacific waves, is a striking contrast to Taiwan's western coast beaches — easily reached by rental bicycle on a perfect coastal morning ride.

text: As a foodie traveler, I found eastern Taiwan's indigenous culinary traditions most accessible in Hualien's specialized restaurants and market stalls. The Amis, Truku, and Sakizaya indigenous communities have distinct food cultures using wild boar, millet, taro, and foraged mountain herbs that differ completely from Han Taiwanese cooking. Several restaurants in Hualien now specifically focus on indigenous recipes — ordering the millet wine and the grilled mountain pork at one of them is a genuinely distinctive meal that almost no short-stay Taiwan visitor discovers.
snippet: Hualien's restaurants specializing in Amis and Truku indigenous food — millet wine, grilled mountain pork, foraged herbs — offer a culinary experience completely unlike Han Taiwanese cooking and largely undiscovered by short-stay visitors.

text: The coastline between Hualien and Taitung, known as the East Rift Valley and Coastal Range loop, is one of Taiwan's most beautiful drives — a landscape of paddy fields between two mountain ranges, with the Pacific visible through occasional gaps in the coastal hills. The Fuyuan National Forest Recreation Area and the scenic Yuli Canyon are accessible stops along the route. Renting a scooter in Hualien and spending a full day exploring south along this coast toward Taitung gives a completely different and far less-visited perspective on Taiwan than the western corridor between Taipei and Kaohsiung.
snippet: Scootering south from Hualien along the East Rift Valley — paddy fields between two mountain ranges, Pacific glimpses through the coastal hills — is one of Taiwan's most beautiful drives and almost entirely off the tourist trail.

text: Baku, in a single word, is breathtaking. The city is distinguished by its well-preserved Russian-era architecture, lively atmosphere, relaxed vibe, and well-kept tourist attractions. It presents an intriguing mix of the modern and the historical — parts that feel distinctly Parisian or British, while other areas resemble a Turkic Dubai. Everyone I met was incredibly hospitable, making it one of the warmest countries I've ever visited, and the Icherisheher (Old City) with its narrow winding streets felt like a fairy tale.
snippet: Baku is breathtaking — a stunning mix of Parisian architecture, Soviet history, and gleaming modern towers, all wrapped in genuinely warm Azerbaijani hospitality. The Old City alone is worth the trip.
