text: The Absheron Peninsula surrounding Baku holds one of the strangest and most fascinating landscapes I've encountered anywhere — eternal flames burning from natural gas seeping through the ground at Yanar Dag, mud volcanoes burbling in the arid coastal landscape, and the ancient fire temple of Ateshgah where Zoroastrian pilgrims once came from across Asia. Spending a full day on the peninsula rather than staying within the city reveals an Azerbaijan that the gleaming Flame Towers give absolutely no hint of.
snippet: The Absheron Peninsula around Baku — eternal flames burning from the ground at Yanar Dag, mud volcanoes, and the ancient Zoroastrian fire temple at Ateshgah — reveals an Azerbaijan the gleaming Flame Towers give no hint of.

text: Baku's cuisine sits at a genuinely distinctive crossroads of Caucasian, Persian, and Russian influences that produces a culinary identity unlike its neighbors. The traditional restaurants around the Old City serve excellent piti (slow-cooked lamb and chickpea soup cooked in individual clay pots), qutab flatbreads with pomegranate and greens, and the layama lamb dishes of the Lezgin minority. Drinking Azerbaijani tea from an armudu glass with a cube of sugar held between the teeth is a daily ritual I found myself adopting within my first twenty-four hours.
snippet: Azerbaijani piti — slow-cooked lamb and chickpea soup in individual clay pots — and qutab flatbreads with pomegranate are a culinary tradition that sits at a genuinely distinctive crossroads of Caucasian, Persian, and Russian influences.

text: As a solo architecture traveler, Baku's downtown presented one of the most surprising urban contrasts I've found anywhere. The Oil Barons' villas from the 1890s petroleum boom — extravagant European historicist mansions built in a single generation of extraordinary wealth — stand directly alongside Soviet constructivist buildings from the 1920s and Norman Foster's Heydar Aliyev Centre from 2012. Walking a single city block can span more than a century of radically different architectural ideas, and the result is visually fascinating rather than incoherent.
snippet: A single block in central Baku can span 1890s Oil Baron mansions, Soviet constructivist buildings, and Norman Foster's 2012 Heydar Aliyev Centre — one of the most architecturally eclectic city streetscapes I've encountered anywhere.

text: Using Baku as a base for a trip into the Caucasus highlands to the west, particularly the Sheki region, reveals a completely different Azerbaijan from the coastal capital. The 18th-century Khan's Palace in Sheki, with its extraordinary stained glass shebeke windows assembled without glue, is one of the most beautiful rooms in the Caucasus. The drive through the mountain villages of the Greater Caucasus range, stopping at roadside stalls selling local honey, walnuts, and cherry preserves, is one of those drives that makes you recalibrate entirely what the country is.
snippet: The 18th-century Khan's Palace in Sheki — its extraordinary shebeke stained glass windows assembled without glue — is one of the most beautiful rooms in the Caucasus and worth the drive west from Baku alone.

text: My experience in Minsk was quite positive. While the city may not be the most picturesque due to its wartime destruction and Soviet reconstruction, it has a unique charm and fascinating history. It was impressively clean — no litter in sight — and I felt completely safe during my visit. I highly recommend the Great Patriotic War Museum, Gorky Park at sunset, and trying the local borscht, draniky (potato pancakes), and the famous Shuba herring salad.
snippet: Minsk has a unique Soviet-era charm — impressively clean, completely safe, and fascinating for anyone interested in 20th-century history. The War Museum, Gorky Park, and the local food make it a surprisingly rewarding destination.

text: Stalin-era architecture enthusiasts will find Minsk almost overwhelming in the best possible way — the city was almost completely destroyed in WWII and rebuilt according to Stalin's 'Socialist Realism' aesthetic, resulting in one of the most coherent and monumental urban ensembles of that style still intact anywhere in Europe. The kilometre-long sweep of Independence Avenue with its granite facades, neoclassical colonnades, and Soviet stars is genuinely awe-inspiring if you approach it on its own aesthetic terms. Coming at dusk when the pale stone turns gold and the scale of the planning becomes fully apparent is the right time to see it.
snippet: Minsk's Independence Avenue — a kilometre of Stalin-era granite facades and neoclassical colonnades rebuilt after WWII — is one of the most coherent Socialist Realist urban ensembles surviving anywhere in Europe, best seen at dusk when the scale becomes fully apparent.

text: The Trinity Hill neighbourhood across the Svislač River from central Minsk offers a human-scaled counterpoint to the Soviet grandeur — small coloured houses, riverside cafes, art studios, and the Upper Town's Baroque churches cluster together in an area that survived the wartime destruction better than most. The Minsk Art Museum holds an underappreciated collection of Belarusian art from the 19th and 20th centuries that documents the country's cultural identity with striking clarity. Spending a morning in the galleries and an afternoon in Trinity Hill gave me a much richer picture of Belarus than the Stalin boulevards alone.
snippet: The Trinity Hill neighbourhood and Minsk Art Museum together offer a human-scaled and culturally rich counterpoint to the monumental Soviet boulevards — an underappreciated side of the city that most visitors miss.

text: Minsk surprised me as a destination for live classical music and theatre — the National Academic Bolshoi Opera and Ballet Theatre is a stunning building and the productions are genuinely world-class at prices that would be absurdly low by Western European standards. Getting tickets the day of for a ballet performance in the ornate Soviet-era hall, filled with locals dressed for the occasion, is an experience that feels both frozen in time and completely alive. The Belarusian philharmonic and the chamber music scene in the city's smaller venues are also active and excellent.
snippet: Minsk's National Bolshoi Theatre offers world-class opera and ballet in a stunning Soviet-era hall at prices absurdly low by Western standards — getting day-of tickets for a performance is one of the city's great simple pleasures.

text: Day-tripping from Minsk to the Khatyn Memorial — where a Belarusian village was burned by Nazi forces in 1943 with its entire population locked inside the buildings — is a profoundly sobering experience that fundamentally shapes how you understand Belarus. The memorial is one of the most powerful and architecturally considered sites of remembrance in Europe, with individual chimneys and bells marking each destroyed household. Pairing it with the nearby open-air architectural museum at Dudutki, where traditional Belarusian windmills and manor buildings have been preserved, balances the day with something more hopeful.
snippet: The Khatyn Memorial outside Minsk — individual chimneys and bells marking each household of a village burned with its entire population — is one of the most powerful and architecturally considered sites of remembrance in Europe.

text: I thoroughly enjoyed my time in Bolivia overall, and Santa Cruz ranks near the top of my list of South American countries visited. Nice people, plenty to do, and not overrun by digital nomads or travelers. The city itself is more of a gateway — the main square and nearby Cotoca village with its sloths and church are pleasant, but the real draw is using Santa Cruz as a base for Bolivia's wider wonders like the Jesuit Missions circuit. The infrastructure is fine despite what people say, and food is cheap and good.
snippet: Santa Cruz is a lively, undervisited gateway to Bolivia's best — use it as a base for the Jesuit Missions circuit and the wider country. The people are warm and the food is cheap and excellent.

text: Mostar is unbelievably beautiful — it's mostly just the old town close to the bridge but you will feel like you are in a completely different world. The Stari Most (Old Bridge), the Ottoman-style bazaars, and the overall atmosphere are spectacular. My overall experience in Bosnia was remarkable — the only downside is the palpable reminder of the war, which is sobering, but the warmth and friendliness of the Bosnian people truly stood out. I did both Mostar and Sarajevo two years ago and was captivated — Mostar in particular is truly beautiful.
snippet: Mostar is unbelievably beautiful — the Stari Most bridge, the Ottoman bazaars, and the timeless old town atmosphere are spectacular. The Bosnian people are some of the warmest you'll meet anywhere.

text: Mostar in the evening, after the day-trip crowds from Dubrovnik have departed, becomes an entirely different city — the Stari Most bridge is lit from below, the sound of the Neretva River rises in the quiet, and the few remaining vendors pack up while locals reclaim the old town lanes for their own evening stroll. Staying overnight rather than visiting as a day trip is the single best piece of advice I can offer anyone considering Mostar. Waking up to walk the bridge in the early morning mist before anyone else has arrived is one of the most serene moments I've experienced in the Balkans.
snippet: Waking up in Mostar to walk the Stari Most bridge in the early morning mist before any day-trippers arrive — the Neretva rushing below, no one else in sight — is one of the most serene moments available in the Balkans.

text: The food culture in Mostar is worth taking seriously — Bosnian cuisine is distinct and deeply satisfying, and the restaurants just off the main tourist thoroughfare do it far better than the ones on the bridge approach. Cevapi served with fresh somun bread and raw onion, dolma stuffed with lamb and rice, and the honey-soaked baklava from the Ottoman-era sweets shops are all essential eating. The restaurant terrace at Tima-Irma, set back from the tourist strip with a garden and excellent ćevapi, is a reliable favourite among those who know it.
snippet: Mostar's cevapi with fresh somun and raw onion, and the honey-soaked baklava from Ottoman-era sweets shops, are essential eating — and the restaurants just off the tourist strip do it all considerably better than those on the bridge approach.

text: For travelers interested in the 1990s Balkan conflicts, Mostar offers a uniquely layered reckoning with a recent and complex history. The front line between Croat and Bosniak forces ran through the middle of what is now the tourist area, and evidence of the conflict — bullet-scarred buildings, the Sniper Tower, and the entirely rebuilt Stari Most (destroyed in 1993 and reopened in 2004) — is woven through the city without being fetishized. The War Photo Exhibition in the old town handles this history with intelligence and without sensationalism.
snippet: The War Photo Exhibition in Mostar's old town handles the 1990s conflict — including the destruction and reconstruction of the Stari Most itself — with intelligence and without sensationalism, giving essential context to what you're walking through.

text: Kravice waterfalls, about 45 minutes from Mostar by car, are one of the most beautiful natural sites in the western Balkans — a sweeping horseshoe of cascades dropping into a turquoise swimming hole surrounded by travertine cliffs and dense vegetation. Going in early June before the peak summer crowds arrive means the pools are still refreshing and the falls more powerful from spring snowmelt. Combining a morning at Kravice with an afternoon in Mostar's old town makes a near-perfect Herzegovinian day that the city is an ideal base for.
snippet: Kravice waterfalls — a horseshoe of cascades dropping into a turquoise swimming hole 45 minutes from Mostar — are most beautiful in early June before the crowds arrive, and make the city an ideal base for a near-perfect Herzegovinian day.

text: I really liked Salvador — some interesting sights, Pelourinho is beautiful, and the nightlife is awesome. It's a very fun city with warm, welcoming people. The food is perfect — the beaches are beautiful (maybe an 8/10 on the Brazilian beach scale), and there's a rich culture to see and experience, including museums about Carnival, samba, and Bahian food like moqueca. Safety requires some awareness, as with most large Brazilian cities, but sticking to tourist areas like Pelourinho and Rio Vermelho is fine.
snippet: Salvador's Pelourinho is beautiful, the food is incredible, and the Afro-Brazilian culture, music, and nightlife give it an energy unlike anywhere else in Brazil. The warmth of the Bahian people makes it unforgettable.

text: Salvador is one of the most extraordinary cities in the world for Afro-Brazilian cultural heritage — the Candomblé religious tradition, brought by enslaved West Africans, is still actively practiced and the terreiros (houses of worship) in the Liberdade and Bonfim neighbourhoods welcome visitors to ceremonies with proper protocol and advance arrangement. The Museu Afro-Brasileiro in the old city is exceptional, and the Igreja do Bonfim, where the famous ribbon-tying tradition originates, is an evocative visit regardless of your religious background.
snippet: The Museu Afro-Brasileiro and the active Candomblé terreiros of Liberdade make Salvador one of the most extraordinary cities in the Americas for Afro-Brazilian cultural heritage — a depth of history that far exceeds what most visitors come expecting.

text: The Bonfim and Ribeira neighbourhoods in northern Salvador gave me a more authentic local experience than the tourist-heavy Pelourinho. Eating acarajé — black-eyed pea fritters stuffed with vatapá and dried shrimp — from a Bahiana vendor in traditional white dress at the street market near Bonfim church felt like the most distinctly Salvadoran food experience I found in the city. The Ribeira fish market on weekend mornings draws locals buying freshly landed catch and is one of those chaotic, photogenic scenes that rewards getting up early.
snippet: Eating acarajé from a Bahiana vendor at the Bonfim street market — black-eyed pea fritters stuffed with vatapá and dried shrimp — is the most distinctly Salvadoran food experience the city offers.

text: For outdoor travelers, the Costa do Dendê south of Salvador — accessed by ferry from the Terminal Marítimo — has some of the most beautiful beaches in Bahia without the development of the Morro de São Paulo circuit. The beaches at Boipeba, reachable by boat from Morro de São Paulo, are pristine and remote enough that some require walking through tidal flats to reach. The combination of turquoise water, warm Bahian hospitality, and very basic infrastructure makes the area feel like an earlier, slower version of the Brazilian coast.
snippet: The beaches at Boipeba on the Costa do Dendê — some reachable only by walking through tidal flats, all pristine and unhurried — feel like an earlier, slower version of the Brazilian coast that the more developed destinations have left behind.

text: Visiting Salvador during the February Carnival or the January Festa do Bonfim reveals a city operating at a completely different frequency — the Festa do Bonfim draws enormous crowds for the washing of the church steps by Candomblé priestesses in white, and the subsequent street party continues through the night. Salvador's Carnival is often considered more authentic than Rio's ticketed grandstands — the trio eléctrico trucks blasting axé and pagode move through the streets with crowds following freely around them, and the energy is utterly democratic and completely joyful.
snippet: Salvador's Carnival — trio eléctrico trucks moving through the streets with crowds following freely, axé music at full volume, no ticketed grandstands — is more democratic and arguably more joyful than Rio's famous version.

text: It was interesting to be in a country with so little tourism, but I think there's a reason for it — the capital has real challenges. That said, the countryside around Bujumbura is genuinely beautiful, and the shores of Lake Tanganyika — one of the largest and deepest lakes in the world — are stunning and make for a memorable setting. The city itself is more of an experience for the adventurous traveler who wants to go somewhere truly off the beaten path and has very few Western visitors.
snippet: Bujumbura is one of the least-visited capitals in Africa, sitting on the stunning shores of Lake Tanganyika. It's a destination for genuinely adventurous travelers who want to experience something truly off the beaten path.

text: Lake Tanganyika in front of Bujumbura is genuinely one of Africa's great natural wonders — the clearest freshwater lake on the continent, stretching 670km along four countries, with water visibility that rivals the best reef diving anywhere in the world. The endemic cichlid fish found nowhere else on earth make snorkeling off the beach clubs on the northern shore an experience that serious wildlife travelers go out of their way to reach. Sundowners from the beach while dhows cross the copper-lit water with the Democratic Republic of Congo mountains behind them is a view that very few Western travelers ever see.
snippet: Lake Tanganyika's endemic cichlids and extraordinary underwater clarity make snorkeling off Bujumbura's northern shore a wildlife experience that serious freshwater divers go out of their way to reach.

text: Bujumbura's lively Quartier Asiatique market is one of the most vibrant trading districts in the Great Lakes region — a dense network of stalls selling everything from mobile phone parts to imported fabrics, with a soundtrack of competing music and the smell of brochettes grilling over charcoal. The Burundian drumming tradition, performed by the Royal Drummers of Burundi on specific evenings at venues that change seasonally, is a UNESCO-recognized cultural heritage that produces one of the most powerful live percussion experiences available anywhere in Africa. Tracking down a performance is worth considerable effort.
snippet: The Royal Drummers of Burundi — UNESCO-recognized, performing a percussion tradition of extraordinary power — are one of Africa's most compelling live cultural experiences and worth tracking down during any visit to Bujumbura.

text: The Rusizi National Park on the delta where the Rusizi River empties into Lake Tanganyika is only a few kilometres from Bujumbura's city centre and shelters hippos, crocodiles, and a rich array of waterbirds in a surprisingly wild setting. Taking a guided pirogue trip through the delta channels at dawn, when the hippos are still active and the crowned cranes are flying overhead, is one of those accessible wildlife experiences that doesn't require a multi-day safari. The park's proximity to the capital makes it an easy half-day addition to any Bujumbura visit.
snippet: A dawn pirogue trip through Rusizi National Park's delta channels — hippos still active, crowned cranes overhead — is a remarkably accessible wildlife experience just a few kilometres from Bujumbura's city centre.

text: Burundi's coffee is among the finest produced anywhere in Africa — the high-altitude arabica grown in the northern highlands around Kayanza and Ngozi has a bright, fruit-forward acidity that specialty roasters around the world actively seek out. Visiting one of the washing stations during the harvest season (March-June) through an arranged tour gives an extraordinary close-up view of how specialty coffee goes from cherry to cup. Drinking a properly prepared Burundian pour-over in Bujumbura, knowing you are in the country of origin, is one of those travel experiences that sharpens your appreciation for what you're tasting.
snippet: Burundian coffee from the northern highlands is among Africa's finest — visiting a washing station during the March-June harvest and then drinking a Bujumbura pour-over knowing you're in the country of origin sharpens your appreciation entirely.

text: Yaoundé surprised me with its relatively safe feel during the day — unlike some other African capitals, I didn't feel unsafe wandering around the central districts. The city is spread across seven hills and has a genuine urban energy, with lively markets, good local food, and a surprisingly vibrant cultural scene. Getting around requires some planning as it's quite spread out, but the local restaurants serving ndolé and grilled fish are excellent and very affordable. Cameroon as a country genuinely rewards curious travelers willing to go beyond the guidebook.
snippet: Yaoundé is spread across seven hills and has a genuine urban energy with lively markets and excellent local food. It's a safe and surprisingly vibrant capital for travelers willing to explore beyond the obvious African destinations.

text: As a food traveler, Yaoundé was a quiet revelation — the neighborhood of Bastos has some surprisingly sophisticated restaurants serving Franco-Cameroonian fusion, but it's the roadside brochettes and peanut-stew joints in Mvog-Mbi that really steal the show. I spent a morning at the Marché Central picking through spices, dried fish, and handwoven baskets, and the energy there was completely addictive. The city's hillside geography means every taxi ride offers sweeping views across rooftops and green valleys.
snippet: The roadside brochettes and peanut-stew joints in Mvog-Mbi steal the show, and the Marché Central is an addictive sensory experience.

text: Visiting Yaoundé in the dry season (November to February) means manageable roads and pleasant temperatures in the low 20s — perfect for exploring the forested hills on foot. The Musée National, housed in the former presidential palace, is genuinely impressive and traces Cameroonian history from pre-colonial kingdoms through independence. For a city that rarely appears on bucket lists, it has a depth of cultural experience that rewards anyone willing to slow down and look carefully.
snippet: The Musée National, housed in the former presidential palace, is a genuinely impressive window into Cameroonian history that most visitors miss entirely.

text: As a couple traveling together, we found the Nlongkak neighborhood a great area to base ourselves — quieter than the centre, with a cluster of good local restaurants and an easy walk to Rond-Point Nlongkak in the evenings. The Benedictine Monastery of Mont-Fébé on the city's highest hill offers a stunning panoramic view over Yaoundé's green, hilly sprawl and a serene contrast to the buzz below. Cameroon's mix of Francophone culture, Central African tradition, and genuine hospitality made us feel like we'd found something genuinely off the beaten track.
snippet: The Benedictine Monastery of Mont-Fébé offers one of the finest panoramic views in any African capital, overlooking Yaoundé's green, hilly sprawl.

text: Yaoundé's cultural institutions are underrated — the small but well-curated Musée d'Art Camerounais at the Benedictine monastery has some extraordinary traditional masks and carvings that most visitors walk straight past. The political buzz of a capital city gives the café terraces around Avenue Kennedy an energy that feels more cosmopolitan than you'd expect. Spend an evening in one of the hillside maquis bars listening to bikutsi music and you'll understand why Cameroonians are proud of their city.
snippet: Spend an evening in a hillside maquis bar listening to bikutsi music and you'll understand immediately why Cameroonians are proud of Yaoundé.

text: We found Galway to be a wonderful city and wished we had more time to explore it. We did a self-guided walking tour taking in Eyre Square, Lynch's Castle, the Latin Quarter, and the colorful Long Walk houses by Nimmo Pier — it was all so charming. In the evening, we headed to The Crane in the West End for traditional music and the atmosphere was electric, especially with the young musicians performing. We could have easily spent another day there, venturing into Connemara.
snippet: Galway is a wonderful, charming city — the Latin Quarter, the colorful Long Walk houses, and the traditional music sessions in the West End pubs make it one of the most atmospheric cities in Ireland.

text: As a solo foodie, Galway's food scene blew me away — the Saturday market at St. Nicholas' Church is one of the best in Ireland, with fresh Connemara oysters, artisan cheeses, and wood-fired sourdough all within arm's reach. Kai on Sea Road and Aniar are worth booking well in advance; both represent a kind of quietly confident Irish cooking that uses local west coast ingredients brilliantly. The compact city center means you can move from coffee shop to craft beer bar to seafood restaurant all on foot without any planning.
snippet: The Saturday market at St. Nicholas' Church is one of Ireland's finest, with fresh Connemara oysters and artisan producers that reflect the west coast's exceptional larder.

text: Galway in October has a completely different mood to the summer crowds — the arts and film festival season is in full swing, the pubs feel genuinely local rather than touristy, and the Atlantic light on the Salthill promenade has a dramatic, moody quality that photographers love. The Claddagh, just across the river from the Spanish Arch, is one of the oldest fishing villages in Ireland and still feels remarkably authentic. Autumn is honestly the best time to visit if you want the place to feel like it belongs to you for a few days.
snippet: Galway in October has a genuinely local feel — festival season, moody Atlantic light, and fewer crowds make it the ideal time to experience the city as it really is.

text: Hiring a bike in Galway and cycling out to Salthill along the bay is a perfect half-day — the Blackrock diving tower, the long promenade, and the views across Galway Bay toward the Burren are all spectacular and easily missed if you stay in the city centre. The Spanish Arch area along the river is beautiful for a slow afternoon coffee, and the buskers on Shop Street add a soundtrack to everything. Galway rewards walkers and cyclists far more than drivers.
snippet: Cycling out to Salthill along the bay — past Blackrock and across views toward the Burren — is a half-day you shouldn't skip.

text: We used Galway as a base for three days of day trips and it worked perfectly — Connemara National Park one day, the Aran Islands by ferry another, and the Cliffs of Moher via the coast road on the third. The city itself has a compact, energetic centre with great accommodation at every price point, and the nightlife on Quay Street is genuinely fun without feeling rowdy or unsafe. Coming back into Galway after a day in the wild Atlantic landscape and warming up with a pint and live fiddle music is one of those simple travel pleasures that's hard to beat.
snippet: Using Galway as a base for Connemara, the Aran Islands, and the Cliffs of Moher works brilliantly — and coming back to a pint and live fiddle music in the evening is the ideal reward.

text: Calgary is probably my favourite place by far — loads to see and do, lots of really nice, friendly people, relatively clean and safe, and gorgeous mountains just an hour away. I've traveled a lot and can say Calgary is a great city: clean, tons of sunshine, great walking areas, excellent restaurants, a vast selection of breweries, and lots of cultural things to check out. Even the film crew who came to shoot The Last of Us was amazed at how clean and friendly the city was. And Banff and Jasper right next door make this one of the best base cities in North America.
snippet: Calgary is a genuinely underrated city — clean, sunny, friendly, with a fantastic food and brewery scene — and having Banff and the Canadian Rockies just an hour away makes it one of the best base cities in North America.

text: I visited Calgary in January for the winter experience and it was genuinely magical — the city is well-adapted to cold weather, with an extensive Plus 15 indoor walkway system connecting downtown buildings so you can explore without braving the -20°C outside. Catching a Calgary Flames NHL game at the Scotiabank Saddledome was a fantastic evening out, and the energy in a Canadian hockey arena is something every sports fan should experience at least once. The craft brewery scene along 17th Avenue SW is thriving year-round and the breweries themselves are warm, welcoming spaces in winter.
snippet: The Plus 15 indoor walkway system makes Calgary fully navigable in January, and catching a Flames NHL game at the Saddledome is a bucket-list night out.

text: As a foodie couple, we spent three days focusing entirely on Calgary's restaurant scene and barely scratched the surface — the Beltline neighborhood has some of the best restaurants in western Canada, from elevated Albertan beef tartare to excellent Vietnamese and Ethiopian options. The Calgary Farmers' Market on weekends is enormous and full of local producers showcasing Alberta beef, honey, and artisan preserves. The city's size means you need a car or rideshare to get around properly, but the food scene alone justifies the trip.
snippet: The Beltline neighborhood alone has some of western Canada's finest restaurants, and the weekend Calgary Farmers' Market is a feast of local Alberta producers.

text: For hikers, Calgary is the obvious base camp for the Canadian Rockies — Banff and Lake Louise are under two hours by car, and the Icefields Parkway beyond is one of the world's great scenic drives. The city itself has excellent urban trail systems along the Bow and Elbow Rivers if you want a run or walk before heading into the mountains. September and early October offer golden larch season in the Rockies, crisp days, and far fewer crowds than summer at the same trailheads.
snippet: For hikers, Calgary is the ideal Rockies base camp — September's golden larch season in Banff and Lake Louise offers some of the most spectacular trail scenery in North America.

text: The Stampede in July transforms Calgary into something genuinely extraordinary — the world's biggest outdoor rodeo, midway rides, First Nations cultural performances, and a city-wide party atmosphere that runs for ten full days. Beyond Stampede week, the Studio Bell music museum downtown is one of the finest music heritage institutions in North America and completely free on certain days. The East Village along the Bow River has transformed dramatically in recent years into a thoughtfully designed neighborhood with independent cafés, public art, and riverside walking paths.
snippet: The Calgary Stampede in July is a genuine spectacle — ten days of rodeo, First Nations culture, and city-wide festivities unlike anything else in North America.

text: Sal was my first solo travel experience, and I deliberately chose it because touristy locations tend to be safer — and it did feel safe throughout. The beaches in Santa Maria are beautiful and the water is warm and clear. The island has become a little touristy in places, but it's a lovely destination nonetheless. I found a good mix of laid-back beach culture, water sports, and friendly locals, making it an easy and enjoyable first destination for solo travelers.
snippet: Sal's Santa Maria beaches are beautiful with warm, clear water and a relaxed atmosphere that makes it perfect for first-time solo travelers. It's become a popular destination, but its natural beauty and easy vibe remain intact.

text: Sal is one of those rare places where a couple can have a genuinely different holiday on the same island depending on their mood — my partner spent mornings kitesurfing on the world-class flat-water lagoon at Kite Beach while I explored the eerie abandoned salt flats at Pedra de Lume, where you float effortlessly in hypersaline water, and we reunited each evening at one of Santa Maria's seafront grills for fresh grilled tuna and cold Strela beer. The sunsets over the Atlantic from the pier are consistently spectacular. It's a small island but it has more variety than its compact size suggests.
snippet: Between the kite-surfing lagoon, the surreal salt flats at Pedra de Lume, and fresh grilled tuna at sunset on the pier, Sal packs remarkable variety into a small island.

text: I gave Bath a 9/10 on my trip report — it's one of the most beautiful cities in England, with the Roman Baths and the Georgian architecture making it feel truly special. The free walking tour in the morning was decent and gave great context for exploring the rest of the day. What I loved most was the ability to randomly wander and explore without a strict schedule — smaller cities like Bath reward spontaneity. It's well worth a night or two rather than just a day trip from London.
snippet: Bath is one of England's most beautiful cities — the Roman Baths, the sweeping Georgian architecture, and the walkable streets make it a 9/10 destination that rewards a night's stay over a rushed day trip.

text: Bath in December has a charm that rivals anywhere in England — the Christmas market fills the area around Bath Abbey with mulled wine and artisan gifts, and the Georgian crescents and squares look especially beautiful when lit at dusk. The Thermae Bath Spa rooftop pool, with its warm natural mineral waters and views over the skyline, is a genuinely restorative experience and worth booking even on a short visit. The city handles the Christmas season with more elegance than most.
snippet: Bath's December Christmas market and the rooftop thermal pool at Thermae Bath Spa make it one of England's finest winter city breaks.

text: For architecture lovers, Bath is essentially an open-air museum of Georgian design — the Royal Crescent, the Circus, Pulteney Bridge, and Prior Park are all within walking distance of each other and represent some of Britain's finest eighteenth-century urban planning. The Jane Austen Centre is popular but feels a little commercial; the Assembly Rooms and Fashion Museum next door are more substantive and worth the extra time. Taking the long route along the canal towpath toward Bathampton is a lovely half-day walk that most visitors skip entirely.
snippet: The Royal Crescent, the Circus, and Pulteney Bridge form one of Britain's finest Georgian streetscapes — Bath is an architecture lover's dream laid out at a walkable scale.

text: As a solo traveler spending a full weekend in Bath, I found the city rewarding beyond the famous landmarks — the independent shops on Walcot Street have great vintage books and local art, and the small café scene around Margaret's Buildings is a lovely spot to recharge between sights. The American Museum in Britain at Claverton Manor, just outside town, is a genuinely surprising find with beautifully curated rooms of American decorative arts and wonderful gardens. Bath has enough depth for a long weekend if you're willing to look beyond the Roman Baths.
snippet: Bath rewards slow exploration — Walcot Street's independent shops and the American Museum in Britain at Claverton Manor offer depth that most visitors on a day trip never find.

text: We visited Bath as a foodie couple and were pleasantly surprised by how good the dining scene has become — The Bertinet Kitchen is excellent for a cooking class, and restaurants like Menu Gordon Jones offer genuinely creative tasting menus at prices you'd never see in London for the same quality. The Saturday market at Green Park Station is a fantastic morning stop for local cheese, charcuterie, and fresh pastries. Bath felt like a city that takes food seriously without being pretentious about it.
snippet: Bath's food scene has grown impressively — Menu Gordon Jones delivers creative tasting menus at remarkable value, and the Saturday market at Green Park Station is a highlight for food lovers.

text: Kosovo is probably my favorite country in southern Europe — friendly people, beautiful architecture, and fantastic food. Pristina has a unique energy, and the people of Kosovo LOVE Americans, which makes for a very warm welcome. The National Library is a fascinating piece of brutalist architecture, and the Cathedral of Saint Mother Theresa and the historic mosques in the Çarshia area are worth visiting. Try the traditional dish 'fli' before you leave and enjoy a drink at one of the local rakı cafés in the evening.
snippet: Pristina is a warm, welcoming capital with a fascinating mix of Ottoman, communist, and modern architecture. The local food — especially fli and grilled meats — and the incredibly friendly people make Kosovo one of the best surprises in southern Europe.

text: Pristina's café culture is extraordinary for a city of its size — Fehmi Agani Street and the surrounding pedestrian zone are lined with packed terraces from morning until late at night, and the standard of coffee is genuinely impressive for a country that takes its espresso seriously. The Bill Clinton statue and the Hillary Clinton boutique on Bill Clinton Boulevard are genuinely surreal and charming in equal measure. Spending an afternoon just wandering and cafe-hopping here costs almost nothing and gives you a real sense of how young and optimistic the city feels.
snippet: Pristina's café terraces along Fehmi Agani Street are packed from morning to midnight, and the Bill Clinton statue and Hillary boutique on the boulevard named after him are gloriously surreal.

text: For a hiker based in Pristina, the Sharr Mountains and Rugova Gorge are within two hours and offer spectacular scenery that rivals anything in the western Balkans. The city itself is a good logistics base — accommodation is very affordable, the food is filling and cheap, and Pristina's nightlife scene around the Qyteti district is surprisingly lively for a capital that still sometimes gets overlooked on Balkan itineraries. Day trips to the Patriarchate of Peć and the medieval Visoki Dečani Monastery are both well worth the drive.
snippet: Pristina is an underrated base for the Balkans — the Rugova Gorge and Sharr Mountains are within reach, and the medieval Visoki Dečani Monastery is one of the region's finest.

text: Visiting Pristina as a couple interested in recent history, we found the city more layered and thought-provoking than we anticipated — the Museum of Kosovo, the 1999 NATO memorial sites, and conversations with locals who remember the war firsthand gave a depth to the visit that guidebooks alone can't prepare you for. The Çarshia quarter's Ottoman-era bazaar still functions as a real marketplace with copperware, embroidery, and traditional clothing being sold alongside tourist trinkets. Kosovo's story is one of Europe's most recent and remarkable, and Pristina is the best place to begin understanding it.
snippet: The Museum of Kosovo and conversations with locals who lived through 1999 give Pristina a historical depth that's rare in European capitals — it's a genuinely moving place to spend time.

text: The food scene in Pristina is one of the Balkans' best-kept secrets — the local bakeries open at dawn and serve extraordinary burek and byrek pastries for almost nothing, and the evening restaurant scene around the pedestrian zone has improved dramatically in recent years. Drinking rakia at a local shisha café while watching Pristina's dressed-up young population promenade past is a quintessential evening ritual. Budget travelers will be amazed at how far money goes here — a full evening including dinner and drinks for two rarely exceeds twenty euros.
snippet: Budget travelers take note — a full evening in Pristina including dinner and drinks for two rarely exceeds twenty euros, and the food, from dawn burek to evening rakia, punches well above its price.

text: I absolutely fell in love with Belgrade — it stands out as one of the most distinctive places I've been to in Europe. I felt genuinely welcomed and at ease, and was truly impressed by the warmth of the hospitality. I felt secure throughout my stay, even during nighttime strolls, without any concerns about hustlers or pickpockets. The Belgrade Fortress was my favorite — its architecture, historical significance, and stunning views of the city are remarkable — and the Church of Saint Sava is breathtaking inside.
snippet: Belgrade is a remarkable hidden gem — warm hospitality, incredible fortress views over two rivers, a stunning Orthodox cathedral, and a vibrant food and nightlife scene that stays going until sunrise.

text: Dallas has some genuinely excellent things going for it — the Arts District with the Dallas Museum of Art and the Nasher Sculpture Center is world-class, the Sixth Floor Museum at Dealey Plaza is a powerful historical experience, and Deep Ellum is known for its vibrant music scene and impressive street art. Klyde Warren Park is a great spot, often hosting food trucks and events, and the Tex-Mex and BBQ — especially Terry Black's — are not to be missed. It's best explored by Uber or Lyft as it's very spread out, but the individual neighborhoods reward the effort.
snippet: Dallas has an underrated arts scene, great Tex-Mex and BBQ, and the moving Sixth Floor Museum at Dealey Plaza. Deep Ellum and the walkable Arts District are the highlights — explore by Uber since the city is very spread out.

text: For sports fans, Dallas is one of the great American cities — the AT&T Stadium in Arlington is a cathedral of American football and worth a tour even out of season, and catching a Dallas Mavericks or Stars game at the American Airlines Center downtown is a fantastic evening out. The Trinity Groves neighborhood across the Margaret Hunt Hill Bridge has evolved into a buzzing food and drink hub, and the bridge itself is one of the city's most photogenic spots at sunset. Dallas rewards those who plan around its specific highlights rather than trying to walk everything.
snippet: AT&T Stadium is a cathedral of American football worth visiting even on a quiet day, and Trinity Groves across the Margaret Hunt Hill Bridge is one of Dallas's best food neighborhoods.

text: I spent a long weekend in Dallas specifically chasing the BBQ and dining scene and came away genuinely impressed — beyond the obvious spots, the Bishop Arts District in Oak Cliff has a wonderful walkable cluster of independent restaurants, wine bars, and cocktail spots that felt more Austin than Dallas in the best possible way. The Dallas Arboretum is spectacular in spring when the azaleas bloom across the lakeside gardens. Getting around requires accepting that you'll be in a car or Lyft for much of the day, but the city's neighborhoods are rewarding enough to justify it.
snippet: The Bishop Arts District in Oak Cliff has a walkable cluster of independent restaurants and bars that feels genuinely distinct from the rest of Dallas — don't skip it.

text: Dallas has an unexpectedly strong museum quarter — beyond the famous DMA, the Perot Museum of Nature and Science is one of the best science museums I've visited anywhere in the US, and the Crow Museum of Asian Art in the Arts District is small but beautifully curated and free to enter. The Margaret Hunt Hill Bridge over the Trinity River is worth walking across just for the views and the architectural drama of Santiago Calatrava's design. As a cultural destination Dallas consistently gets underestimated.
snippet: The Perot Museum of Nature and Science and the free Crow Museum of Asian Art join the DMA to make Dallas's museum quarter genuinely underrated among American cities.

text: Uptown Dallas on a summer evening is a revelation for anyone who thought the city was purely a corporate hub — the McKinney Avenue Trolley connects a strip of patio bars and restaurants that fills up with a young, lively crowd, and the energy rivals any nightlife neighborhood in Chicago or Houston. Design District has also transformed into an eclectic gallery and restaurant zone that's worth an afternoon. Dallas is a city where the interesting parts are scattered, but each pocket repays the effort of getting there.
snippet: Uptown Dallas on a summer evening — connected by the McKinney Avenue Trolley and packed with patio bars — surprises visitors who expect nothing but corporate towers.

text: I went to Guilin and Yangshuo for 3 days — the Lijiang River cruise was absolutely awesome and the karst mountain scenery is some of the most dramatic and recognizable in the world. I highly recommend the 'Impression of Liu Sanjie' outdoor show, which is truly unreal. The cave systems around the area are also worth a visit, and Yangshuo itself is a fun, lively town to explore by bicycle. Just be aware that the area has become quite popular, so expect crowds at the main spots, but the landscape itself never disappoints.
snippet: The Lijiang River cruise in Guilin delivers the most iconic karst mountain scenery in the world — it genuinely looks like a traditional Chinese painting come to life. The 'Impression of Liu Sanjie' outdoor show at night is a stunning bonus.

text: Taking the two-day slow boat from Guilin down the Li River to Yangshuo, stopping at small villages along the way, is an entirely different experience to the standard tourist cruise — the landscape unfolds at a contemplative pace and the morning mist over the karst peaks is otherworldly. The cormorant fishermen on bamboo rafts at dawn near Xingping are one of those travel images that actually lives up to the photographs. It's the kind of journey that makes you understand why Chinese landscape painters have been depicting this scenery for over a thousand years.
snippet: The cormorant fishermen at dawn near Xingping, glimpsed from the river in morning mist, is one of those rare moments where reality surpasses the photograph.

text: As a cyclist, Yangshuo was one of the best destinations I've ever visited — renting a bike for a few dollars and riding through the rice paddies between the karst towers to the village of Yulong and the Dragon Bridge is simply stunning. The countryside is dotted with local noodle shops where lunch costs almost nothing, and the riding is mostly flat with the mountains providing the drama. Guilin itself is a useful and comfortable base, but it's Yangshuo and the surrounding countryside that will stay with you forever.
snippet: Cycling through Yangshuo's rice paddies between karst towers to the ancient Dragon Bridge is one of the most scenic bike rides in all of Asia.

text: The Reed Flute Cave on the outskirts of Guilin is one of China's most dramatic natural wonders — a vast limestone cavern illuminated in colored lights that turns the stalactites and formations into something surreal and otherworldly. Elephant Trunk Hill in the city center, set where the Li and Peach Blossom Rivers meet, is beautifully lit at night and surrounded by pleasant riverside parks that locals use for tai chi in the mornings. Guilin manages to be a genuinely pleasant Chinese city to explore on foot between its headline attractions.
snippet: Reed Flute Cave's illuminated limestone formations are among China's most dramatic natural spectacles — a world unto itself beneath the hills.

text: Visiting in late autumn brought the bonus of smaller crowds and the spectacular sight of golden ginkgo and maple trees against the grey-green karst peaks — Guilin in October and November is absolutely stunning and far quieter than the peak summer season. The local cuisine is excellent and underappreciated: beer fish from the Li River, stuffed tofu, and Guilin-style rice noodles for breakfast are all worth seeking out in local spots rather than tourist restaurants. Staying two nights in Guilin and three in Yangshuo gives you the best of both without feeling rushed.
snippet: Guilin in late autumn, with golden ginkgo trees against the karst peaks and crowds at a fraction of summer levels, is arguably the destination at its finest.

text: I spent a full 24 hours in Hallstatt and honestly the scenery is absolutely storybook — the lakeside views are like nothing else in Europe. The salt mine tour is genuinely fun, and taking the funicular up to the skywalk gives you a breathtaking panorama of the lake and valley below. That said, go early or stay overnight to beat the day-tripper crowds, because by mid-morning it gets absolutely rammed and parking is a nightmare. I'd say two days max is the right amount of time here.
snippet: The lakeside views are like nothing else in Europe, and the salt mine tour is genuinely fun. Go early or stay overnight to beat the day-tripper crowds — by mid-morning it gets absolutely rammed.

text: Arriving by ferry from Hallstatt's train station across the lake at dusk, with the village reflecting perfectly in the still water, is genuinely one of the most beautiful arrival moments in European travel. The village's Catholic church with its charming ossuary — decorated skulls bearing the names of past residents — is a fascinating and slightly macabre cultural tradition unlike anything I'd encountered elsewhere in Austria. Winter here, when snow settles on the rooftops and the crowds have gone, transforms it into something straight from a fairy tale.
snippet: Arriving by ferry across the lake at dusk, with Hallstatt's rooftops reflecting in still water, is one of the most beautiful arrival moments in all of European travel.

text: For hikers, Hallstatt is more rewarding than the Instagram photos suggest — the trail up to the Echern Valley and the Waldbachstrub waterfall takes you quickly away from the village crowds into quiet alpine forest, and the view back down toward the lake from the upper trail is arguably better than any shot taken from the village itself. The Dachstein Ice Caves above the funicular station are an extraordinary geological spectacle, genuinely vast and ancient, and usually far less busy than the village below. Give yourself at least a full day for the walking options alone.
snippet: The trail to the Waldbachstrub waterfall and the Dachstein Ice Caves take you well beyond the crowds and into an alpine landscape that's arguably more spectacular than the famous village view.

text: Staying overnight in Hallstatt made all the difference — after the day-trippers left by late afternoon, the village became a completely different place: quiet lanes, swans on glassy water, and dinner at a lakeside restaurant with almost no one else around. The sunrise over the Dachstein glacier, seen from the village, turns the mountains a deep copper-pink that no photograph quite captures. If you can only manage one night, arrive in the late afternoon and leave the following morning after breakfast.
snippet: Hallstatt after the day-trippers leave is a completely different village — quiet lanes, glassy water, and a copper-pink sunrise over the Dachstein glacier that defies photography.

text: The salt mine tour above Hallstatt is more entertaining than I expected — riding the miners' slide in the dark, learning about the 7,000-year history of salt production, and emerging into a chamber where preserved Celtic-era tools were found creates a genuine sense of place that complements the postcard scenery below. The Celtic heritage of the region is actually remarkable: the Hallstatt culture is so named because the Iron Age civilization that flourished here was first identified in this valley. It's a place that rewards intellectual curiosity as much as photography.
snippet: The salt mine tour — complete with a miners' slide and 7,000 years of Celtic history — adds genuine intellectual depth to what could otherwise be a purely visual destination.

text: Nicosia is the only divided capital in the world, and that alone makes it fascinating to visit. The walled old city is an underappreciated jewel of historical significance, and food-wise it's arguably the most affordable place in Cyprus while still having a good variety of cuisines. You can cross into the northern part on foot and it genuinely feels like stepping into a different country — bring cash and download offline maps because connectivity on the northern side is spotty.
snippet: The walled old city is an underappreciated jewel, and you can cross into the northern half on foot — it genuinely feels like stepping into a different country. Food-wise it's the most affordable spot in Cyprus with great variety.

text: San José gets a bad rap but if you put in a little effort you'll find a lot to enjoy. Barrio Escalante is fantastic — great restaurants, craft breweries, and bars in a real neighbourhood feel, busy and safe even late at night. The Gold Museum and Jade Museum downtown are genuinely world-class, and the Central Market is a great place for a cheap local lunch. Just use common sense, avoid isolated areas after dark, and use it as a base for day trips to Volcán Poás or coffee plantation tours nearby.
snippet: Barrio Escalante has great restaurants and craft breweries in a real neighbourhood feel — safe even late at night. The Gold and Jade Museums are genuinely world-class, and the Central Market is perfect for a cheap local lunch.

text: I stayed in Old Havana close to Plaza Vieja and it was safe, quiet, and walkable to most major attractions. Despite Cuba's very real hardships right now, Cubans are some of the warmest and most resilient people I've ever met — they seek dignity, not pity. The Fábrica de Arte Cubano is a nightclub inside an art museum and genuinely one of the coolest things I've ever done, and watching sunset on the Malecón is unforgettable. Bring all your cash in USD or euros because ATMs are unreliable and the currency situation is complicated.
snippet: Old Havana is walkable and surprisingly safe, and the Fábrica de Arte Cubano — a nightclub inside an art museum — is genuinely one of the coolest experiences anywhere. Cubans are some of the warmest, most resilient people I've ever met.

text: As a musician, Havana was an almost overwhelming experience — live son, salsa, and jazz spilling out of every doorway in Centro Habana and Vedado at all hours, with a standard of musicianship that's simply staggering. The Casa de la Música in Miramar is a legendary venue for seeing top-tier Cuban bands, and getting there by classic 1950s American car (the only practical mode of transport in many areas) adds to the sense that you've somehow traveled through time. Book private casa particular accommodation rather than hotels — the hosts are fantastic and the food they cook is often better than any restaurant.
snippet: Live son and jazz at professional standard spills from every doorway in Havana, and the Casa de la Música in Miramar hosts top-tier Cuban bands in a legendary setting.

text: The Vedado neighborhood is where Havana feels most layered — grand Art Deco and Modernist mansions from the 1940s and 50s line wide avenues, the Necropolis Cristóbal Colón cemetery is one of the most architecturally extraordinary burial grounds I've ever visited, and the rooftop bar at the Hotel Saratoga offers a spectacular view over the Capitol. As a couple we spent a full day just walking Vedado's streets and felt we'd barely begun to understand it. Old Havana gets all the attention, but Vedado is where the city's complexity really reveals itself.
snippet: Vedado's Art Deco mansions, the extraordinary Necropolis Cristóbal Colón, and the Saratoga rooftop view make it the neighborhood where Havana's complexity reveals itself.

text: Renting a classic convertible for a day and driving east to Playas del Este — Havana's local beach strip — is one of the best day trips you can do from the city, offering white sand and turquoise Caribbean water just thirty minutes from the Malecón. The contrast between a morning wandering crumbling Old Havana and an afternoon swimming in warm clear sea is genuinely surreal. The practicalities of Cuba require patience, but the country delivers experiences that simply don't exist anywhere else.
snippet: A classic convertible east to Playas del Este — from crumbling Old Havana to white-sand Caribbean beach in thirty minutes — is one of travel's great contrasts.

text: Havana's street art and visual culture are remarkable for a city operating under such economic constraints — the murals in Central Havana, the amateur baseball in any available open space, and the dignified way ordinary Cubans dress and carry themselves despite everything all create a profound impression. The Callejón de Hamel in Centro Habana is a painted alley dedicated to Afro-Cuban Santería culture, with rumba performances on Sunday mornings that are electrifying and completely authentic. Cuba demands humility from its visitors but gives back something genuinely rare.
snippet: The Callejón de Hamel's Sunday morning rumba, rooted in Afro-Cuban Santería tradition, is one of the most electrifying and authentic cultural experiences Havana offers.

text: Dominica is the nature island of the Caribbean and Roseau is your base for all of it — there are regular buses from here to the incredible hiking at Trafalgar Falls and Boiling Lake. The locals are genuinely welcoming and the town has a relaxed, authentic Caribbean vibe without the resort crowds you find elsewhere. Renting a car is highly recommended once you arrive because public buses stop running in the early afternoon. It's not a polished tourist destination, and that's exactly what makes it special.
snippet: Dominica is the nature island of the Caribbean, and Roseau is the perfect base for world-class hiking at Trafalgar Falls and Boiling Lake. The locals are genuinely welcoming and the town has an authentic Caribbean vibe without the resort crowds.

text: I went to Carcassonne specifically to see the castle, and it was 100% worth the trip. The medieval citadel sits majestically atop a hill above the modern lower town, and watching the sunset from the ramparts while eating duck cassoulet inside the walls is just extraordinary. I had never truly grasped the scale of a place like this before — it's one of my favourite castles in Europe by a wide margin. Plan to spend at least a full day here, and if you can, stay the night inside the citadel itself.
snippet: The medieval citadel sits majestically on a hill, and watching the sunset from the ramparts while eating duck cassoulet inside the walls is just extraordinary. It's become my favourite castle in Europe — absolutely worth the trip.

text: Lagos is one of the most beautiful towns on the Algarve — the dramatic sea cliffs, the golden grottos at Ponta da Piedade, and the long sandy beaches are all within easy walking distance of the old town. It's busier in summer than you'd expect, and accommodation is pricier during peak season, but it never feels as overwhelmed as some Portuguese destinations. Uber operates in the area so getting around is easy even without a car. Budget a full week if you want to properly explore the coastline.
snippet: Lagos is one of the most beautiful towns on the Algarve — the dramatic cliffs at Ponta da Piedade and the golden grottos are within easy walking distance. It's busy in summer but never as overwhelmed as some Portuguese destinations.

text: I did a quick 24-hour trip from Cairo to Alexandria on the train for about $30 and honestly it was a mix of gritty, moody, and pretty chill. The Bibliotheca Alexandrina is an architectural marvel inside and out, the Qaitbay Citadel has fantastic Mediterranean views, and the fresh seafood lunch overlooking the water was some of the best food I had in Egypt. The city has a completely different feel to Cairo — more Mediterranean, more melancholy — and I was hoping to extend my stay because there were still local gems I wanted to explore.
snippet: Alexandria has a completely different feel to Cairo — more Mediterranean, more melancholy — and the Bibliotheca Alexandrina is an architectural marvel. Fresh seafood overlooking the Corniche was some of the best food I had in all of Egypt.

text: Alexandria is a city for slow walkers and readers of history — the neighborhood of Anfushi near the Eastern Harbor has an Ottoman-era atmosphere that few tourists reach, with old mosques, crumbling mansions, and fish-sellers conducting their business exactly as they have for generations. The Greco-Roman Museum, when open after its long renovation, is outstanding, and Pompey's Pillar and the adjacent Serapeum catacombs are far more impressive in person than photographs suggest. This is a city that will reward anyone who knows a little of its ancient and colonial history before arriving.
snippet: The Anfushi neighborhood near the Eastern Harbor has an Ottoman-era atmosphere that few tourists reach — Alexandria at its most atmospheric and unhurried.

text: As a seafood lover, Alexandria is arguably the best destination in Egypt — the stretch of restaurants along the Corniche and in the Stanley neighborhood serve prawns, calamari, and sea bass straight from the Mediterranean at prices that feel almost impossibly low compared to Europe. Stanley Beach itself is a charming semicircle of pale sand backed by the colorful Stanley Bridge, popular with local families on weekends and much more relaxed than tourist beaches elsewhere. The city's Mediterranean soul, Levantine and Greek influences all mixed together, gives its food a distinctive character you won't find in Cairo.
snippet: For seafood lovers, Alexandria is Egypt's finest destination — the restaurants along the Corniche serve prawns and sea bass from the Mediterranean at prices that feel almost impossible by European standards.

text: Visiting Alexandria in spring means comfortable temperatures in the mid-20s and a city that feels genuinely alive — the Corniche fills with Alexandrian families strolling in the evening light, the café culture around Raml Station is buzzing, and the famous patisseries like Délices and Trianon offer pastries that still carry the ghost of the city's cosmopolitan early twentieth-century identity. The Royal Jewelry Museum in the Zizinia district, housed in a lavish former palace, is one of Egypt's most underrated attractions. Alexandria in spring, before the summer heat and crowds, is close to perfect.
snippet: The famous patisseries of Alexandria — Délices and Trianon — still carry the ghost of the city's cosmopolitan early twentieth-century identity, best enjoyed on a spring afternoon along the Corniche.

text: El-Montaza Palace and its sprawling public gardens at the eastern end of the Corniche are where Alexandrians go on Fridays to picnic, stroll, and breathe — a genuinely lovely and very local experience that costs almost nothing. The palace itself was a royal summer residence and the grounds include a small private beach and views along the coast that feel a world away from the city centre. If you want to see Alexandria as its residents experience it rather than as a tourist, spend a Friday afternoon at Montaza.
snippet: Spending a Friday afternoon at Montaza Palace's gardens — where Alexandrians picnic and promenade under royal palms — is the most local and authentic experience the city offers.

text: In May 2024 I visited Tallinn and it quickly became my favourite destination on my entire Baltic trip. I genuinely think it stands out as one of the most overlooked capital cities in Europe — the medieval Old Town is stunning, the contemporary Rotermann Quarter is worth an afternoon, and the Kalamaja wooden house district is so peaceful and atmospheric you forget you're in a capital. The city isn't yet overrun with tourists, it's near water with amazing sunsets, and I'd happily go back.
snippet: Tallinn quickly became my favourite destination on my entire Baltic trip — I think it's one of the most overlooked capitals in Europe. The medieval Old Town is stunning and the Kalamaja wooden house district is so peaceful you forget you're in a capital city.

text: I recently visited Paris and I absolutely adore the city — the rich history, stunning Haussmannian architecture, and the way every neighbourhood has its own distinct character all captured my heart. The food is genuinely remarkable; you can pop into almost any café without checking reviews and in nine out of ten cases you'll be pleased. The main downside is the constant queues at major sights — the Louvre is packed with people pushing and shoving, so book your timeslots in advance. Stay in Montmartre or the Marais rather than near the main tourist corridors for a more genuine experience.
snippet: The Haussmannian architecture and the way every neighbourhood has its own character completely captured my heart — and the food is remarkable almost everywhere you go. The main downside is the relentless queues at major sights, so book timeslots well in advance.

text: Paris in November is a different city from summer — the chestnut leaves turn gold, the tourist queues at the Eiffel Tower drop dramatically, and the museums feel as they should: places of contemplation rather than spectacle. The Musée d'Orsay on a quiet weekday morning, moving through the Impressionist collection with room to breathe, is one of the great art experiences in the world. Pair it with lunch at a classic zinc-bar brasserie in the 7th arrondissement and you'll have a day that defines what Paris can be at its best.
snippet: The Musée d'Orsay on a quiet November morning — moving through the Impressionist rooms with genuine space — is one of Europe's great cultural experiences.

text: As a solo traveler spending a week in Paris, I found the less-visited neighborhoods more rewarding than the famous ones — the Canal Saint-Martin in the 10th, the Buttes-Chaumont park in the 19th, and the covered passages like Galerie Vivienne in the 2nd all offered a Paris that felt genuinely lived-in and local. The city's public transport system is excellent and makes the outer arrondissements completely accessible, so there's no excuse for staying permanently in the tourist corridors around the 1st and 4th. Paris reveals itself slowly and generously to those willing to get mildly lost.
snippet: Canal Saint-Martin, Buttes-Chaumont, and the covered passages of the 2nd arrondissement offer a Paris that feels genuinely lived-in — rewards reserved for those who venture beyond the famous postcard zones.

text: We visited Paris specifically for its dining scene across five days and found it easily worth a focused trip — the bistronomy movement has produced a wave of small, serious restaurants like Septime, Frenchie, and Clown Bar that offer genuinely inventive cooking at well below fine-dining prices, and pre-booking is essential for all of them. The city's covered markets — Marché d'Aligre in the 12th being the finest — are spectacular morning destinations for cheese, charcuterie, and wine to take back to your apartment. Paris for food lovers who are willing to do their research is one of the world's great destinations.
snippet: Paris's bistronomy scene — restaurants like Septime and Clown Bar offering serious cooking at accessible prices — makes the city a destination for food lovers willing to book well in advance.

text: The Père Lachaise cemetery in the 20th arrondissement is one of the most atmospheric and moving places in Paris — a city of the dead that's also a forest of ancient trees, Art Nouveau sculpture, and extraordinary silence in the middle of a metropolis. Spending a morning there, following the maps to graves of Chopin, Oscar Wilde, Édith Piaf, and Jim Morrison, is a cultural experience that rivals any museum. The eastern edges of the city — the 11th, 12th, and 20th — have some of the city's best natural wine bars and organic bistros, making them ideal for evenings that feel genuinely Parisian rather than performatively so.
snippet: Père Lachaise cemetery in the 20th — ancient trees, Art Nouveau sculpture, and extraordinary silence — rivals any museum in Paris for atmosphere and cultural depth.

text: We just returned from a wonderful eight days in Puglia based in Ostuni and it was amazing — wonderful food, delightful beaches, and a pace of life that immediately slows you down in the best way. Renting a car is really essential to get the most out of the region; Polignano a Mare, Lecce, Alberobello's trulli, and the coastline around Otranto are all within easy reach and all spectacular. July can be quite crowded in the main towns so it's best to explore in the morning and retreat to your masseria in the afternoon. Honestly I don't think you can go wrong anywhere in Puglia.
snippet: Eight days based in Ostuni was amazing — wonderful food, delightful beaches, and a pace of life that immediately slows you down. Rent a car, because Polignano a Mare, Lecce, and Alberobello are all within easy reach and all spectacular.

text: Lecce is probably the finest Baroque city in southern Italy and one of the most underrated in all of Europe — the golden sandstone used throughout the old town takes on an extraordinary honey glow at sunset, and the Basilica di Santa Croce's facade is a masterpiece of sculptural extravagance that genuinely stops you in your tracks. As a solo architecture lover I spent two full days there and could have happily stayed longer. The passeggiata culture in Lecce's streets each evening, the coffee culture, and the cost of living make it an ideal base for a slower, more independent trip through Puglia.
snippet: Lecce's golden Baroque old town at sunset — especially the facade of Santa Croce — is one of the most beautiful architectural spectacles in all of southern Europe.

text: The coastline between Otranto and Santa Maria di Leuca at the very tip of Italy's heel is the kind of drive that makes you pull over every five minutes — turquoise bays, whitewashed hilltop towns, and almost no other tourists once you leave the main sites. Otranto's old cathedral has an extraordinary twelfth-century mosaic floor covering the entire nave, and the town's small historic centre is completely charming. For couples who want beautiful beaches, good local wine, and genuine Italian atmosphere without the Amalfi crowds, the Salento coast is close to perfect.
snippet: Otranto's twelfth-century cathedral mosaic floor is one of southern Italy's most extraordinary medieval treasures, and the drive south along the Salento coast is one of the country's great routes.

text: Puglia in April or May is a different proposition to the summer rush — the olive groves and masserie are wrapped in wildflowers, the roads between trulli farms and vine yards are almost empty, and the weather is warm without being oppressive. The Valle d'Itria between Alberobello and Locorotondo is a patchwork of white-stone trulli houses and dry-stone walls that looks like nowhere else on earth, and cycling through it in spring is one of Italy's great slow travel experiences. Arriving before the summer prices kick in also makes the excellent local wines and burrata cost almost nothing.
snippet: Puglia in May — wildflowers around the masserie, empty roads, and cycling through the Valle d'Itria — is the region at its most magical and a fraction of the summer price.

text: The food of Puglia is arguably the best regional cuisine in Italy for its simplicity and quality — orecchiette with cime di rapa, burrata at source in Andria, frisella bread soaked in olive oil, and the grilled octopus in the fishing towns along the Adriatic coast are all revelatory. Visiting a local masseria for an afternoon cooking class, then eating what you've made under a pergola of vine leaves as the sun goes down, is the kind of experience that makes Puglia feel more than just a beautiful place — it feels nourishing in a deep sense. The olive oil here, often sold in unlabeled bottles direct from the producer, is some of the finest in the world.
snippet: Puglia's food — burrata at source in Andria, orecchiette with cime di rapa, and grilled octopus straight from the Adriatic — makes a serious case for the region having Italy's finest regional cuisine.

text: Queenstown is the adventure capital of the world for a reason — bungee jumping, skiing, skydiving, and paragliding are all on your doorstep, and the scenery of Lake Wakatipu surrounded by the Remarkables is genuinely jaw-dropping. Fergburger is mandatory, but don't overlook Pedro's House of Lamb just up the road. I'd highly recommend renting a car for at least a couple of days to drive through the Crown Range to Wanaka and down to Glenorchy — these are some of the most beautiful roads I've ever driven. The city is very walkable and the hostel scene is incredibly social.
snippet: Queenstown is adventure central with bungee jumping, skiing, and paragliding all on your doorstep, and the scenery of Lake Wakatipu surrounded by the Remarkables is genuinely jaw-dropping. Rent a car for a day to drive to Wanaka and Glenorchy — some of the most beautiful roads you'll ever drive.

text: Queenstown in winter transforms into one of the southern hemisphere's finest ski resorts — Remarkables and Coronet Peak are both within thirty minutes of town, the après-ski scene is excellent, and the combination of world-class mountain access and a fully functioning lakeside town means the off-mountain options are far better than at most ski destinations. Arriving in July for snow-season prices and uncrowded pistes is one of New Zealand's best-value travel experiences. The Skyline gondola at night, with the snow-covered Remarkables lit by moonlight, is extraordinary.
snippet: Queenstown in winter — Remarkables and Coronet Peak within thirty minutes, excellent après-ski, and snow-covered peaks reflected in Lake Wakatipu — is one of the southern hemisphere's finest ski experiences.

text: For a couple on a honeymoon or romantic trip, Queenstown delivers something genuinely special — a scenic helicopter flight over the fiords to Milford Sound, followed by a cruise on the water, is one of the most dramatic and romantic day trips available anywhere in the world. The wine region of Central Otago, a forty-minute drive from Queenstown, produces some of the world's finest Pinot Noir and the cellar doors are beautiful settings for an afternoon. Queenstown has a tendency to feel like a theme park, but with the right planning it reveals itself as a place of genuine natural grandeur.
snippet: A helicopter flight over the fiords to Milford Sound followed by cellar doors in Central Otago's Pinot Noir country makes Queenstown one of the world's finest destinations for a romantic trip.

text: As a budget backpacker, Queenstown surprised me — yes, some things are expensive, but the hostel scene is genuinely one of the world's most social, the hiking options around Ben Lomond and the Queenstown Hill Track are completely free, and the views from the top of either are as dramatic as anything you'd pay a tour operator for. The Remarkables produce excellent craft beer at the local microbreweries, and the lake shore is lovely for a free afternoon. The adventure activities get the attention, but Queenstown's free hiking is the real secret.
snippet: The free hike up Ben Lomond above Queenstown delivers panoramic views of the Remarkables and Lake Wakatipu that rival anything you'd pay an adventure tour operator for.

text: Te Anau and Fiordland are three hours from Queenstown by road, and making that drive rather than flying gives you a journey through some of the most remote and spectacular high-country scenery in New Zealand — empty roads, mirror lakes, and the moment the bush-covered Fiordland mountains appear on the horizon are genuinely awe-inspiring. Spending a night in Te Anau before taking the boat into the glowworm caves and then on to Milford Sound means you experience Fiordland at a pace that day-trippers from Queenstown can't match. Queenstown is the adventure capital, but Fiordland is where New Zealand's soul lives.
snippet: Driving from Queenstown to Te Anau and into Fiordland rather than flying gives you one of New Zealand's great road journeys — empty high-country roads building to the drama of Milford Sound.

text: I love Biarritz — the attractions are mostly walking around, seeing the old port, the gorgeous beaches, and the coastal path that runs along the cliffs with stunning Atlantic views. It's more laid-back and authentic than you might expect for a famous resort town, and combining it with nearby Bayonne (a lot less touristy) and a day trip into the Pyrenees gives you a near-perfect trip. The Les Halles covered market is the best place to eat lunch. For accommodation it can be expensive in peak season so consider staying in Bayonne and day-tripping in.
snippet: Biarritz is mostly about walking — the old port, the gorgeous beaches, and the coastal cliff path with stunning Atlantic views. It's more laid-back and authentic than you'd expect, and combining it with nearby Bayonne makes for a near-perfect trip.

text: Biarritz in October is a revelation for surfers — the Atlantic swells are at their most powerful and consistent, the famous Grande Plage and La Côte des Basques are far less crowded than summer, and board rental is easy and affordable along the seafront. The Cité de l'Océan museum near the beach is an excellent rainy-day option covering the history and science of the ocean with real quality. The Basque Country in autumn also means wild mushroom season at the local markets, and the combination of surf and serious food makes it hard to leave.
snippet: Biarritz in October is a surfer's dream — powerful Atlantic swells, empty Côte des Basques, and the Basque Country's wild mushroom season at the local markets.

text: As a foodie traveler, spending a week based in Biarritz and eating across the French and Spanish Basque Country is one of the great culinary road trips of Europe — San Sebastián is just forty-five minutes across the border, Bayonne has its own distinct charcuterie and chocolate tradition, and the local pintxos bars in Biarritz itself are excellent and far less touristy than their counterparts in Spain. The Basque cheeses at the market, particularly the aged Ossau-Iraty with black cherry jam, are extraordinary. This is a region where eating well is not an indulgence — it's the entire point.
snippet: A week in Biarritz eating across the French and Spanish Basque Country — Bayonne's chocolate, San Sebastián's pintxos, and the local Ossau-Iraty cheese — is one of Europe's great culinary circuits.

text: The Villa Belza on the Côte des Basques cliff and the Russian Orthodox Church near the Casino are two of Biarritz's less-visited architectural gems — the town's belle époque and imperial Russian history gives it an unlikely grandeur for what is essentially a small surf resort. Walking the coastal path from the lighthouse above the Grand Plage south to the Basque Museum in Bayonne via Anglet is one of the best half-day walks in southwestern France. Biarritz works best when you treat the whole Basque coast as your destination rather than the town itself.
snippet: The coastal path from Biarritz's lighthouse south to Bayonne via Anglet is one of southwestern France's best half-day walks, threading cliff tops, pine forest, and beach in one sweep.

text: Coming to Biarritz in February for the off-season means empty beaches, Atlantic storms rolling in spectacularly from the west, and accommodation at a fraction of August prices — and the town still has excellent restaurants open and a genuine local life operating without the tourist overlay. The storm-watching from the lighthouse above the Rocher de la Vierge statue is genuinely dramatic when the swells are up, with white water crashing across the rocky coastline in every direction. Biarritz belongs to the Basques in winter and it's a privilege to share it with them.
snippet: Biarritz in February — empty beaches, Atlantic storms watched from the lighthouse, and accommodation at a fraction of summer prices — is a quietly dramatic winter destination.
